## Thread: QUESTION — 8 msgs

### QUESTION

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T00:54:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000831205450.00793.00000297@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
I am very new to cobol i have only taken 2 class days on it and i have a slight
question so if it is something simple dont laugh;).  We are just typing in a
program off of a sheet and there is a line 

PIC S9(5)V9(4)

i cant tell if the s is big or small or if there is spaces inbetween the ) and
the V, does any of this matter?  or wont it affect anything.  Thanks, i hope i
provided enough info.

Deek
```

#### ↳ Re: QUESTION

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T01:42:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KPDr5.27646$K5.450439@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20000831205450.00793.00000297@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```

Deek40 <deek40@aol.com> wrote in message
> PIC S9(5)V9(4)
>
> i cant tell if the s is big or small or if there is spaces inbetween the )
and
> the V, does any of this matter?  or wont it affect anything.  Thanks, i
hope i
> provided enough info.

if P is big, S is big, no space before V. usually does matter (depends
a little bit on your compiler)
```

#### ↳ Re: QUESTION

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T02:57:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dP48Ox497vNU@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<20000831205450.00793.00000297@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
On Fri, 1 Sep 2000 00:54:50, deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:

> I am very new to cobol i have only taken 2 class days on it and i have a slight
> question so if it is something simple dont laugh;).  We are just typing in a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Deek

The PIC is always followed by at least one space. The values after
that space are always typed with no spaces between the values.

AFAIK most COBOL compilers are "case insensitive" and it does
not matter if the code is typed in UPPER lower or MixEd case.

Case only makes a difference when you are calling external
routines that are developed using a "case sensitive" language
like C or C++ or others of that ilk.

Some people code with UPPER case for variable names
but this is a convention only. Others write their code with
the COBOL keywords in upper case (ie: MOVE variablea TO gender.)
but this does not make any difference in any of the compilers
I have used.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: QUESTION

- **From:** "mike sheehan" <sheehan_mj@iolfree.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T10:33:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yBLr5.698$44.3480@news.iol.ie>`
- **References:** `<20000831205450.00793.00000297@ng-fb1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dP48Ox497vNU@tcpserver>`

```
Depending on the editor you use ... some will highlight key words i.e. COBOL
reserved words
Very useful when you want to get the syntax right...
Some editors will only do this if you have the COBOL words e.g. MOVE x TO y
in upper-case/capitals...

Mike

lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote in message ...
>On Fri, 1 Sep 2000 00:54:50, deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:

slight

in a

>>

the ) and
>> the V, does any of this matter?  or wont it affect anything.  Thanks, i
hope i

>>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Lorne Sunley
```

#### ↳ Re: QUESTION

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T07:05:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39AFA985.A1C522EA@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000831205450.00793.00000297@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```


Deek40 wrote:

> I am very new to cobol i have only taken 2 class days on it and i have a slight
> question so if it is something simple dont laugh;).  We are just typing in a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Deek

In old compilers everything was upper case.  Nowadays case doesn't matter here.
Spaces do matter though (you don't want them in your picture).  But you were safe
anyway, if you had experimented, either the compiler would have worked or it would
have told you your syntax was wrong.   Dangerous errors are when the compiler
thinks you mean one thing, but you meant something else.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Merant's Personel Cobol Window 3.1 ver1.0??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T07:00:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oo5of$c4v$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39AE7634.F0C95216@ms4.hinet.net> <8om18g$1l4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <39AF0574.4CEF62ED@ms4.hinet.net> <39AF4DBE.B113EF70@mail.medecine.unige.ch>`

```
I am fairly certain that MERANT *never* places prices on their web pages,
because the prices are different from country to country.

If you "click" on the CONTACT option, that will give you a list of countries
and allow you to find someone who is "local" to help you with demo's, prices,
etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Merant's Personel Cobol Window 3.1 ver1.0??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T07:11:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1Nr5.5328$pJ.78453@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<39AE7634.F0C95216@ms4.hinet.net> <8om18g$1l4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <39AF0574.4CEF62ED@ms4.hinet.net> <39AF4DBE.B113EF70@mail.medecine.unige.ch>`

```
That's because Merant doesn't want to be responsible for lawsuits
from people suffering heart failure from seeing their prices in a
public forum. ;-)  I recently upgraded from MF COBOL 3.2 16 bit to
Net Express 3.0, and it cost $2500 (yearly maintenance is $660).
The purchase price for Net Express is a substantially higher.  You
need to contact a Merant sales person.  Fujitsu COBOL is a lot
cheaper, but Net Express compiled all my old MF COBOL 3.2 programs
virtually unchanged, and no file conversions needed.  That was worth
more than $2500 to me. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Opening

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T07:01:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39AFA88B.6BEDA3A0@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<sqqv3qntc5d89@corp.supernews.com> <8ok9if$qd9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <b5tr5.119$Ep3.35918@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <_0Dr5.114$CT1.77867@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:

> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> .
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> rewarded more (rock bands and baseball players being the exceptions).

Actually, they are not the exceptions, as mass media entertainers
contribute a tiny bit to a great many people and reap the benefits.   But
we do not get paid by how much we contribute to society, we get paid by
supply and demand - and by how much we contribute to our employer.  If our
employer could get us cheaper, he would pay us less, or if he didn't make
a profit by hiring us, he wouldn't hire us.  Society has nothing to do
with it (unless society is paying you - which applies to mass media
entertainers which you mentioned as the exceptions).

Otherwise, teachers would earn more than programmers, even though there is
no positive relationship between teacher pay and teacher performance.  And
politicians would earn negative money...
```

the purse strings, and it ignores market forces, we have intractable
shortages.

Harry (who *was* a teacher many years ago and a COBOL programmer not so many

<a id="t-88334b9b2c"></a>
## Thread: PRECOSP and DSS? — 1 msgs

### PRECOSP and DSS?

- **From:** "pwmeister" <pwm@nomail.se>
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T12:46:14+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oo1iu$4bq$1@news1.enator.se>`

```
Hi all

Does anybody knows anything about Precosp and DSS on mainframe?
We, at our site, are about to take over a box with Precosp
and DSS running with DL/1 maybe some DB2 and IMS. We are a
bunch of ol' cobolers, but none of us have ever heard of precosp and
DSS.  We are guessing that precosp and DSS is some kind of a
precompiler and code generator. It has some verbs and syntax
extensions, but thats about all we know. So, If there is anyone
who will shed some light - I would be very thankful.

- What is Precosp and DSS?
- Who owns/markets Precosp/DSS? Are they still on market?
- The role of Precosp and DSS in development?
- Where to find more info on precosp and DSS?
- Can precosp or DSS code [easily] be ported to say VS COBOL II?
Or should we/have to stay with precosp or DSS (maybe a needless
question???)
- generally pros and cons with precosp / DSS.

TIA
peter meister
cbldabbler
```

<a id="t-a6fa8ad76b"></a>
## Thread: Dynamic memory allocation in Microfocus cobol — 1 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2000-08](2000-08.md#t-a6fa8ad76b)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic memory allocation in Microfocus cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** quazhi@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-01T18:21:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oos30$5b4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8olsii$ki3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <zUur5.145680$c5.3912692@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8om5ou$mb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8om7h2$li8$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks William,

But in our case, my understanding tells me that the routine C++ called
by the main program Cobol uses "memory allocation" to meet its private
usage. So the question is where the memory pool comes from for this
allocation and who manage this allocation (Cobol library or C library).
Plus, we don't use any "extensions" feature such as cbl_routines for
this purpose.

In article <8om7h2$li8$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> There is something VERY wrong in this post.  Dynamic Memory
allocation is
> *not* a part of *any* existing COBOL Standard (before or after
1993).  It
> *is* a part of the PROPOSED (draft) Standard - but is very definitely
NOT a
> part of ANSI X3.23b-1993.
>
> MERANT (previously Micro Focus) does provide some callable cbl_
routines for
> dynamic memory allocation, if that is what you are asking about. (BUT
these
> are very definitely "extensions" - not Standard).
>
> Many existing compilers (MERANT among them) also include "USAGE
POINTER" data
> items - AGAIN as an extension to the current Standard.  These too
will be
> part of the NEXT Standard (when/if it is approved).
>
> Finally, as I understand your REAL problem, you are finding that your
> application "fails" because of memory allocations - and you don't
know who
> *is* getting this memory.  If that is the real problem, then it is
true (and
> always has been) that the COBOL run-time system may WELL "acquire"
storage to
> accomplish some tasks.  Among other things, if your COBOL program
calls other
> COBOL programs - without canceling them when done, then such storage
is
> acquired via the first DYNAMIC access to the subprogram and never
released.
>
> --
> Bill Klein
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
> <quazhi@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8om5ou$mb$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> > Thanks Oscar! But I want more details. I mean the dynamic
allocation of
> > memory during calls of the routine C++, not addressable memory
> > exchange. I learned that the early versions of the standards  before
> > ANSI X3.23b-1993 don't support the feature of the dynamic memory
> > allocation (see
> >
http://directory.google.com/Top/Computers/Prgramming/Languages/Cobol/Sta
> > ndards/). So I want to know whether our current version of the
> > Microfocus Cobol uses the early ANSI standard of Year1993 or later.
> >
> > Currently, our cobol programe calls a routine C++ to process XML
> > documents and return the results back. The problem is that the
system
> > is often deadly locked due to lack of the memory when several big
XML
> > documents are processed. We doubt it is caused by our cobol
environment
> > can not allocate new memory to the routines written in other
languages
> > like C++ to process big document. Is it right?
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > routines. The answer to both is yes. The details are documented
in the
> > > "Object COBOL Programmer's Guide to Writing Programs - Chapter 10:
> > > Interfacing and Mixed-language Programming"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > compatible with the version of microfocus 4.1 has the feature
of the
> > > > dynamic memory allocation (uses in calls to routine written in
C++)?
> > > > Thanks in advance!
> > > >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-567868147b"></a>
## Thread: AS400 — 1 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

_Thread started [2000-08](2000-08.md#t-567868147b)._

#### ↳ Re: AS400

- **From:** piasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-02T06:17:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oq60o$kis$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8om277$37k$13$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
I was using a book called Programming in COBOL/400, by Cooper, Stern &
Stern. Publisher Wiley.  This book does not have much about DB2 in it
though.  You can link files together logically on the AS400 using
logical files and use files as a database.  You can also run querries
to make reports.  This book has some example programs that are pretty
good, FULL LENGTH PROGRAMS, not just bits and pieces.  It is written
with the beginner in mind.  There may be other books exclusively for
DB2 or the new universal database which works on both the AS400 and NT
and other platforms.

I had a book at work by an author by the last name of LIN who taught at
Washington University, in St Louis, MO, that had a DB2 project in it.
I think it was published by Prentice Hall.  Surely there are some
Better DB2 books out there.  Your AS400 may have RPG also.  RPG can be
useful for control break programs if you can get the hang of it.

Just look at Amazon.com  they have lots of programming books, Or just
look at the examples already in the as400.  Must be a few things you
can plagerise, I mean borrow.  Why reinvent the wheel?

In article <8om277$37k$13$1@news.t-online.com>,
  "Tim" <tim171072@gmx.net> wrote:
> Hi everybody, in january I will start my first job as COBOL-
programmer on an
> IBM-Machine (AS400).
> For now I am a programmer with 0.5 year experience in COBOL-
programming and
> that only on a Bull DPS-9000.
>
> Is there anybody who can tell me something about the special things
of COBOL
> on AS400 and DB2? Are there any good courses in Germany? Are there
any books
> that could help me?
>
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want Cobol Source Code

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-03T15:49:12-05:00

Why in the WORLD is anyone trying to convert code to "VS COBOL II" any more?
That product is going out of service next March - and is already "far from
strategic".  You should be looking at converting to IBM's current product,
i.e.
    IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM

<a id="t-42d2bf407e"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Debug Tool on MVS — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL Debug Tool on MVS

- **From:** "Steve Dearth" <sdeart31@linuxworld.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-03T19:58:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N3ys5.8147$Oc.2350500@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
I'm fairly new to COBOL on MVS, and am trying to gain some understanding of
existing code using the LE DEBUG TOOL.  I have a bunch of code compiled
using IBM COBOL for MVS and VM.  I can't seem to get the debug tool working.
Any help or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

I have compiled with TEST(ALL,SYM), and have allocated all the required
datasets.  When I execute, I dump and get the following message:

CEE0374C CONDITION = CEE3201S TOKEN = 00030C81 59C3C5C5 00000000
         WHILE RUNNING PROGRAM EQA00EVH WHICH STARTS AT 8CBA80A0
         AT THE TIME OF INTERRUPT
         PSW     078D0000 80000004
         GPR 0-3 00025728 000252F8 00025330 0000007B
         GPR 4-7 00025098 00025334 0CBAC09C 000254E8
         GPR 8-B 0CBAB09D 0CBAA09E 0CBA909F 8CBA80A0
         GPR C-F 00015A80 00025168 8CBA83C2 00000000

I notice that R15 is zero, which could definitely explain the problem.  I
suspect that I've got a linkage problem, but don't notice any unresolved
references in the binder output.  Anyone have any idea what I am doing
wrong?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Debug Tool on MVS

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-03T21:30:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B2C300.C087B41E@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<N3ys5.8147$Oc.2350500@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
R15 = 0.  PSW shows 31 bit addressing mode ON.  PSW shows you are
at 00004, which is the middle of the Restart New PSW.  This means
that you have branched, jumped, GOTO'ed to address x'00000000'
(R15).  It would appear that you have gotten a PIC 1 (ABEND
S0C1).

If you are using SYSUDUMP, you need to find R14 (8CBA83C2).  The
actual address is x'0CBA83C2'.  The save area chain is at
x'00025168', and you should run this back to see who called who. 
Also look at x'0CBA80A0' and see what program lies at that
location.  It appears that this program is the one that choked.

Assuming that you are getting RC=4 from BINDER, and that you are
not using LET and/or NCAL as an option, perhaps you need to check
with IBM for APAR/PTF info using MSGCEE0374C ABENDS0C1 for
keywords.  Also, perhaps you need to use TEST as an option for
BINDER?

This is as much as I can do for you at this point, w/o having any
further info.  I'm more of an ALC programmer, who happens to do
COBOL stuff.


Steve Dearth wrote:
> 
> I'm fairly new to COBOL on MVS, and am trying to gain some understanding of
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>          GPR C-F 00015A80 00025168 8CBA83C2 00000000
<snip>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Debug Tool on MVS

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T02:54:43+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C24643.FA912A00@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<N3ys5.8147$Oc.2350500@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <39B2C300.C087B41E@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:

> R15 = 0.  PSW shows 31 bit addressing mode ON.  PSW shows you are
> at 00004, which is the middle of the Restart New PSW.  This means
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> in use

Based upon the above,  I think that you are probably overwriting program area.
You might consider compiling with SSRANGE to enable bounds checking and
correcting the problems that crop up.

Thanks Ken
```

```

Colin Campbell
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Debug Tool on MVS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Tony Piner" <tony_piner@uk.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qncpa$vp0$1@sp4en10.hursley.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<N3ys5.8147$Oc.2350500@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <39B2C300.C087B41E@neo.rr.com> <39C24643.FA912A00@zip.com.au> <39C3F6EB.5E5780D7@attglobal.net>`

```
New Debug Tool users getting ABEND0C1s from EQA00EVH/EQA00DBG
usually have not run the two jobs EQAWLU62 and EQAWLECS from
the **.SEQASAMP library.

If the problem persists, please get a PMR raised, and we'll be happy to look
into it.

Tony Piner - Debug Tool Development, IBM

<a id="t-de256c9105"></a>
## Thread: ACCESS 97 SQL and Fujitsu COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ACCESS 97 SQL and Fujitsu COBOL

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-03T20:10:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B2B03D.C651F43@neo.rr.com>`

```
I am having a few problems with reading an Access (97) data base
via ODBC.  When I code the SQL statements, I have set up for 2
byte binary (PIC S9(4) COMP-5) and I get back zeros (Microsoft
says in Access that INTEGER is 2 byte binary!).  I also am
attempting to obtain a date field, but, I can't seem to figure
out what Microsoft puts in it.  Interesting considering I know
what I put in the records when I built them using Access.

I have been through several books on Access, SQL, and Fujitsu's
COBOL manuals.  I can't seem to figure out just what format
Microsoft is using for some of their data formats.

Does anyone have any ideas, or pointers you can give me.  I'm at
the point now of using relative addressing and writing a "hex
dump" formatting routine in COBOL to figure out what they're
doing (yes, I'm an old MVS dinosaur who use to do ALC).
```

#### ↳ Re: ACCESS 97 SQL and Fujitsu COBOL

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-05T22:12:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B56FC0.5494728A@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<39B2B03D.C651F43@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:
> 
> I am having a few problems with reading an Access (97) data base
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> what I put in the records when I built them using Access.
<snip>

Well, this turned out to be a succession of rookie errors.

1) Make sure that you code the SELECT correctly (I think I have
found some new twists in being syntactically correct, and
logically wrong).

2) Microsoft hands the date back as a character string thusly: 
"yy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss" and if you were not doing "long dates" then
the hhmmss are all zeros.

The INTEGER fields are PIC S9(4) COMP-5 compatible, and,
contained what I expected once I stopped tripping over the SELECT
syntax.

Now on to more and better things -- UPDATING the table, and JOIN,
and DELETE, and, buying a new hard drive, rebooting NT once or
twice...
```

---

I wonder how many errors you get if you misspell ENVIRONMENT.   Actually the
worst misspellings are when you cut and paste 0500-EXIT.   to 0600-EXIT. and
forget to change the spelling, or when you forget that you need to keep
MY-INDEX and use it again.  Of course good programming practices make these
less likely - but you sometimes have to maintain programs written with
practices you don't agree with.

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-12T08:54:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lAqv5.6391$%p.39350@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20000910175425.17145.00000099@ng-fw1.aol.com> <20000910215642.00605.00000196@ng-ci1.aol.com> <k1errsc0nmvif49q6bnodhnprutfhscd59@4ax.com> <8pl56f$m3v$1@news.igs.net> <39be1b2f_4@news5.newsfeeds.com>`

```
After debugging one of my early COBOL programs, back during the last
Ice Age, I was surprised to see where a paragraph name was misspelled.
Curious as to why the program could even compile with a misspelled
paragraph name reference, I was amazed to find that I had misspelled
the paragraph name the same way in every place.  One of the marks of
a good programmer is consistency, consistency, consistency. ;-)
```

(above 200 octal) are being lost when written to the various char types.  And I
still can't get the BINARY type to work either.

Jeff Burris

<a id="t-02027b244d"></a>
## Thread: Cobol with C++ routine, memory management — 1 msgs

### Cobol with C++ routine, memory management

- **From:** quazhi@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-05T13:45:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p2teg$hbm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
When a main program in Cobol calls a c++  routine which uses the
dynamic memory allocation, who manages the memory pool of c++, Cobol or
the heap of the operating system. We believe the c++ is not serious
enough to manage its memory pool. Our environment is hp unix. We have a
big problem in using the cobol with c++ routines. The system often
fails due to lack of memory. We want to make clear for some system
memory management mechanism. Can anyone give us the help? Thanks in
advance!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-08a165195b"></a>
## Thread: IBM "drop service date" for COBOL for MVS & VM — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### IBM "drop service date" for COBOL for MVS & VM

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-05T10:10:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p32cr$ht8$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI,
  Today  IBM announced a Dec 31, 2001 "drop of service" date for IBM COBOL
for MVS & VM.  (Usually such announcements are for the US - check your own
"support dates" if you are in some other country.)

This product is "replaced" by IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM.

See:
 http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_900-220

(This announcement also includes "drop dates" for various other
product/releases such as TSO/E, IMS, DB2 etc.  It also tells what
product/releases replace them)

It *might* seem as if this means that there will be ONLY one product/release
of COBOL available for the "MVS" environment for the first time since the
early 1990's - HOWEVER, I have every reason to believe that there will be
another announcement this month (that hasn't been made yet) of a "new"
release (version?) of COBOL for OS/390 & VM.

Keep your eyes open (and I will "post" an announcement when I see it).
```

<a id="t-d09f876f1c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL "reserved columns" (was: Philosophical question — 1 msgs

### Re: Fw: COBOL "reserved columns" (was: Philosophical question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-05T14:22:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p3h5n$qfg$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<NBBBJPGEPBMHMOJGKPFFAEMEEDAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <39B5226D.6F5D3F65@home.com> <39B54078.304CCCCB@brazee.net>`

```
I do NOT think that anyone thinks that the type of code in that examples is
the reason that the restrictions on A-/B-margin were removed.  HOWEVER, what
the Standards committees did think (as I recall) is that there is ABSOLUTELY
no place in Standard COBOL that you "need" to enforce A-/B-margin rules.
Furthermore, as there are many COBOL implementations (not IBM) that have not
"enforced" them for years - and their programmers have not felt this caused
any problems,
 therefore, it was felt that this restriction SHOULD be removed from the
Standard.

There is NO reason whatsoever that programmers can't still code the "old"
way - it is simply that they won't be FORCED to.

Interestingly enough, I wrote a paper on this very topic requesting J4 (the
ANSI-ish COBOL committee) to at least allow for an OPTION to require
paragraph and section names to be the first thing on the line (to avoid
paragraph names in the middle of a line of source code - as in that example).
The committees rejected this paper.

If you think that it would be DESIRABLE to have such an option, then please
look at my paper (in MS Word format) at:
  http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4htm/m225/00-0391.doc

and send your comments (not to me) but to
   doncobol <at> mediaone.net

asking for them to be submitted to J4 as an "input document".

*ALTERNATIVELY* (or in addition),
  Submit a SHARE requirement (or support REQUEST) that IBM provide such an
option - even if the next COBOL Standard doesn't require it.
```

<a id="t-11b0f58d67"></a>
## Thread: MERANT - on notification of Net Express V3.1 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MERANT - on notification of Net Express V3.1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-05T14:34:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p3hrb$m33$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Passing on the reply that I received from the Product Manager for Net
Express,

"Hi Bill,

Thanks for passing this on.

 ... I'll focus specifically about the notification of Net Express 3.1. A Net
Express 3.1 upgrade letter was sent out by post a couple of months ago to all
NA Net Express customers on maintenance and this gave details of all the new
features in 3.1, the number to call for the upgrade and advise to consult the
license agreement and the users sales representative about deployment
charges.

This mailshot was in addition to the SupportNet informer notification which
again maintenance customers would have received.

I think the problem here is that from your note it seems <that> this user's
maintenance had expired in 1999 and that would explain why he didn't receive
the letter.   As indicated <elsewhere> renewal notices are now being handled
more
smoothly to avoid this in the future.

I am quite keen that we keep a good information flow in place, and for the
future I see SupportNet Informer in particular being a good way to
communicate Net Express technical updates to our customers."
```

#### ↳ Re: MERANT - on notification of Net Express V3.1

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>

- **References:** `<8p3hrb$m33$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

mention more times over the years) they have told me I was not on a
maintenance agreement...And each time I had to go back to my credit card
statement and prove them wrong...This is not a NEW problem for them at
all...It has been a constant pain in the ass with MF and with MERANT...You
would think that they could afford a decent system to keep track of this
stuff...As far as I know, I have NEVER been without a maintenance contract

On the other recent post, concerning notification of a change over to the

a posting...Reminds me of when MF discontinued their CompuServe
forum...WITHOUT any kind of replacement...SUCKS...They did not even carry
over the postings from the old answerlab to the new one, even though they
are using the same vendors software...

NO WONDER THESE FOLKS ARE LOSING MONEY!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

kenmullins

You may try "Cobol Access +" from www.rldt.fr.

The only problem that I see is that it may not support Acubobol.

It works well, although the speed is not the same as the normal
indexed files.

I have a few customers (mainly hospitals) that use this product
with RM/COBOL and either Oracle or Informix in Unix machines.


Frederico

(financial package with an large Oracle DB and 150 concurrent users).

grtx,
Anton

#### ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL/SQL solution

- **From:** Duncan Kinnear <duncan@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-09-06T13:50:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B5A2CB.EB43DFAB@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<39B42A08.CAB08020@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz> <8p3scc$j86$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Douglas Morris wrote:

> Have you looked at acuODBC ?  I think their acuconnect/acuserver products
> are designed to alleviate some of the network congestion issues you might
> have seen with
> acu4gl.

Yes we have looked at AcuODBC, but it only allows ODBC-compliant applications
(such as Access and Excel) to access vision files.  In other words, it's the
wrong direction.  We want to move away from vision index files.

The performance problems with Acu4GL were not network based.  We were running
the 'client' on the same unix server that the database was installed on. (our
application is purely host-based)

Basically, there seems to be something fundamentally wrong with the way Acu4GL
translates START and READ NEXT/PREVIOUS statements.  Most things we do in Cobol
involve starting an index, then reading until you find a record outside your
search scope.  Acu4GL would do this, but with several SELECT statements that
got more and more general until it was selecting the rest of the table just so
that we could read one record outside the original search scope!

One example we had was chronic.  One program started up and before doing
anything else, it attempted to read one record for the current year to check if
there were any records.  In Cobol this was just a START and one READ NEXT.
However, Acu4GL would select ALL the records for the year just so we could read
one!  For a full year's worth of data it took about 3 minutes for it to
complete that one SELECT!

But this has got to be because of the way that Acu4GL does it.  Our new GUI
development language can perform a similar 'cursor open' and 'cursor fetch' in
about 30ms!  And that's on exactly the same database on exactly the same unix
server.

Acu's own support staff told us: "It's a square peg for a round hole"!

Admittedly, we did test Acu4GL over three years ago, but there's been no word
from Acu about any drastic changes to Acu4GL that have increased performance.
```

Duncan Kinnear wrote in message
<39B47ECE.DAF1D68C@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz>...
>Thanks for the prompt reply.
>
>Paul Raulerson wrote:
>
>> Well, if you are prepared to shift off AcuCOBOL, then you have a plethora
of
>> choices. In general, I would say, pick a database and start from there.
>
>Now there lies one of the problems.  We can't "pick a database".  Our
customers
>do that!
>

Is this your product or a customer product? If it is your product, then you
can pretty easily
standardize on something like DB/2 or Oracle. They both run on everything
out there
and it not hard to use both of them at the same time, merely complex.

If it is a customer product, then pretty much all bets are off, because you
have no way to
standardize the data source. I would suggest taking over the data source for
the
customer and becoming an ASP. :)


>Basically we have to support access to multiple databases, on multiple
platforms
>(see my follow-up of my original post)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Let's suppose you choose, say, an RS/6000 box. (You could choose Windows
NT,
>> Solaris, HP/UX, a Mainframe, or even Linux...)
>
>There seems to be a commonality in what you're suggesting!  You don't
happen to
>work for the big blue by any chance?   ;-)
>

Nope- though I do admit that I am predjudiced towards IBM. I can call IBM
and
get support for any product that I own from them. They even try to support
out of
date or emergeing products.  I ran into a really nasty issue with using SQLJ
a couple months ago and some of those folks in Santa Theresa went *way* out
of their way to help. Other folks did the same for me a couple years ago
with a COBOL
problem.

I also don't seem to have much trouble convincing customers that IBM isn't a
fly by night
operation, and they are going to be able to get support. <grin>

On the other paw, I really like Oracle, and am currently involved in with
some Wang
stuff, as hard as that may be to believe. :)

On the gripping paw, I am absolutely disgusted with most Microsoft stuff.
And don't
talk to me about  Borland... (*sigh*) Great Plains is not on my 'happy' list
either.


>
>> Okay, the best choice for this (IMNSHO of course...) would then be IBM's
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<grin> I work (during the day) for a large company in central Texas,
and it has a lot more letters in the name than IBM.

>> How do you want to structure your application? As a host based system? As
a
>> multi-tier product? As a thin client? As a web client? As some
combination
>> of both?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>We are actually rewriting the system in a new GUI language, but that is a
couple
>of years off, and before then we want to change the underlying data
structure of
>the old system to be more normalised, to help us with the eventual
migration.
>

Well, it may seem to be a lot to take a GUI port now, but then again, it may
be that
doing it *now* can save mega-bucks *later*.  You probably need to sit back
and run a test
port to a couple different systems to see what the issues really are. Again,
this may
be a little difficult to justify, but belive me, *one* expensive mistake can
be bad enough
to justify 10 test ports.


>
>> If you want my opinion (for free, so take it for what it is worth),
avoiding
>> the COBOL's that are solely PC based and demand runtime license for each
>> client is a wise thing to do. Consider hosting your database on a 'host',
be
>> that Windows NT, and of a number of UNIX systems, AS/400's, or
mainframes.
>> Don't try to distribute it
>> onto each PC if you can possibly avoid it. And be sure to avoid the
'silver
>> bullet' syndrome of thinking that a single product is going to solve all
>> your needs. AcuCOBOL comes close for small applications. (I have one or
>> two apps out there with AcuCOBOL right now.)
>
>That's exactly what we want!  Do you have any suggestions now that you know
more
>of what we are trying to achieve?  How does the Fujitsu compiler fit into
>things?
>

Fujitsu is a fine COBOL compiler, though I'm not sure how well it will scale
up on the PC
platform. I personally prefer avoding ODBC as the primary interface to the
database in
preference to compiler co-processors. (i.e. DB/2 or Oracle both pre-process
your code.)
On the other paw, take a look at Neon Systems for some really *good* ODBC
implementations.

Pretty much you can do just about anything, but you won't go wrong if you
stick close to host
based systems. <grin> They have been out there for enough years that you can
not only
pretty well be assured of being able to do what you want to do, but also you
can find
people who know how to do it.


>Anyway, Paul, thanks heaps for your reply.  It certainly makes me feel like
it
>is possible to acheive what we want.


Post some of your findings and results when you start making decisions and
such.
It's always great fun to watch someone else have a few good 'wins'!  :)

-Paul

Paul Raulerson wrote:

> Fujitsu is a fine COBOL compiler, though I'm not sure how well it will scale
> up on the PC
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> implementations.

I think of these as different things.  I am used to pre-compilers for databases
such as IDMS.  But that is for a proprietary database which cannot be easily
ported.  I have longed for an ODBC interface to export data to a location where
users can use their tools of choice to do whatever they want with the data.
They want reports, spread sheets, and limited queries without having to put in a
request for us to make minor cosmetic changes.  That's when I think of ODBC.
```

- **References:** `<39B58DC1.502D8FA2@RosebudUSA.com> <39B5CC12.3B0FC6C8@worldnet.att.net> <39B62E9C.4D3961D5@RosebudUSA.com>`

```
On Wed, 06 Sep 2000 07:46:36 -0400, Ethan Schultz
<ethans@RosebudUSA.com> enlightened us:

>That's an idea...   unfortunately bookmgr (on the web)
>didn't have it - and poking through 1000's of page of
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here's the breakdown:

First, visualize a byte as:

X  1  U/P  A/N  D/SPD  Reserved  MDT
0   1    2      3       4  5           6            7

Where the top row is the usage and the bottom row is the bit position
left to right.  The postions are further explained as:

0 = Value determined by contents of bit 2-7

1 = Always a 1

2 = If 0, then Unprotected
      If 1, then Protected

3 = If 0, then Alphameric
      If 1, then Numeric

     Note:  Bits 2 and 3 equal to 11 causes an automatic skip

4 & 5 = If 00, then display/not selector pen detectable
            If 01, then display/selector pen detectable
            If 10, then intensified display/selector pen detectable
            If 11, then Non-display, non-print, non-detectable

6 = Reserved.  Must always be 0

7 = Modified Data Tag:  
      0 = Field has not been modified
      1 = Field has been modified

More notes:  Bits 0 and 1 are not decoded when received by the display
unit (3270).  When transfereing characters to the mainframe (CPU) bit
1 is a 1 and bit 0 is set depending upon the character being
transfered.  All attribute characters are part of the defined
character set.  The default option (bits 2 through 7 all set to 0)
results in an unprotected alphameric, displayed, non detectable field.

Examples:

Unprotected, alphameric, high intensity, detectable, MDT off:

bits 2-7 = 00 1000 (EBCD C8, ASCII 48, Character H)

Protected,  high intensity, detectable, MDT on:

bits 2-7 = 10 1001 (EBCD E9, ASCII 5A, Character Z) 

If you need to know about underlining, blinking etc.  post here and
I'll pass that along.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Today's tip for annoying co-workers:
Ask people what gender they are.
 

Boycott Mitsubishi Industries
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8p6jqd$puk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39B58DC1.502D8FA2@RosebudUSA.com>`

Go to your CICS copylib (for example, it might be called
CICS410.SDFHMAC) and browse member DFHBMSCA. If you can't find it,
write a quick and dirty CICS/COBOL program, copying in DFHBMSCA into WS.

Then, using your handy-dandy PC calculator in scientific mode (or a
hand held calculator if you wish) enter each BMS attribute byte (in
hex) from DFHBMSCA and switch the result mode to BINARY.

I think this will be much easier and you'll be using IBM's definitions.

HTH....

Cheers,

Bill

> If you have no numbering system for the lines how do you know where the
> error is?  will the error messages be immedately after the line in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

Mainframe listings still have line numbers when your source code doesn't.
But it is a pain searching for the same line in your source code if it
doesn't have a sequence number as a cross reference.  Maybe PCs could have
some type of hypertext cross reference, moving you to the source code
error.  I would like that, but I have several copy libraries, including
IDMS COPY from the data dictionary which is put in during the pre-compiler
stage.  I don't see compiler software knowing how to get to the IDD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL "reserved columns" (was: Philosophical question

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-09-08T02:13:30+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B7BEAA.4705451A@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Typically on PC or Unix the development environmetns are set up so that when
you click on the error message it takes you to the source line in question.
The source line number is just the physical sequence number in the file
starting from 1. This also works for included files (like COBOL copy
libraries). You can also go to an arbitrary label with a couple of keystrokes,

Tim Josling

Tim Josling

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Charles Hammond wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> stage.  I don't see compiler software knowing how to get to the IDD.

I don't know for sure, but I'm guessing Fujitsu is very similar to
NetExpress. Without line numbers being used the compiler does do a
source line count, (but that's really only info). All errors are listed
in a separate window at the bottom of the screen. You click the
appropriate error message and the top window (source) displays the line
marked with a red 'X" in left margin. Alternatively you can page-down
through the source looking for lines marked as errors.

When it comes to copy files, they are treated in sequence as part of
your source.

It really is an excellent feature in the IDE.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL "reserved columns" (was: Philosophical question

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T22:43:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B81A17.EAC307FE@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<NBBBJPGEPBMHMOJGKPFFEENAEDAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <8p48sj$jdc$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I am NOT quoting myself - but thought that comp.lang.cobol might be
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> > functionally "compatible" - but not the same syntax)
<snip>

How about some thoughts on this for a bit?  If there are no
sequence numbers in the code, then how does one merge fixes into
the code?

Example:  IBM supplies CPCS (Check Processing Control System) in
source (could be any source maintained product by any vendor). 
When they send out fixes, it is based on the sequence numbers of
the **ORIGINALLY SUPPLIED SOURCE** (not yelling, but trying to
put in emphasis).  Now, going with the new COBOL standards, if we
decide to go to variable length lines, how can we provide
maintenance to our clients?

Mind you, the clients have been doing their own modifications to
the code on the basis of the sequence numbering.  

(Forgive me if this is plainly obvious to most) -- So, here you
are with a product from some vendor, where you are allowed to
make your own modifications.  You do your mods by using a utility
to merge your changes into the base code from the vendor.  The
vendor now sends out a new update.  So, you merge their "deck"
with the original, and then your mod "deck" with the result. 
Immediately prior to this, you ran a check to see if there were
any collisions between your respective changes.

With the new standard, the above will not be possible, if the
COBOL compiler now does not recognize that the first 6 columns
are not actually source.

Am I the only one that sees a problem with this picture?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL "reserved columns" (was: Philosophical question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T19:40:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p9ci5$jep$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<NBBBJPGEPBMHMOJGKPFFEENAEDAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <8p48sj$jdc$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39B81A17.EAC307FE@neo.rr.com>`

```
This is NOT a problem that I think the committee has ever addressed.  I will
say that CURRENTLY the Standard (and IBM COBOLs) do not require you to put
sequence numbers in 1-6, it allows them.  This will be a continued option for
fixed format in the future.

In addition, any "free form" (variable length) line may END with "in-line
comments".  These COULD include sequence numbers or any other "identifier"
that you want.

If this doesn't convince you, then you might want to send your original note
to the J4 chair at
  doncobol <at> mediaone.net

I think this is a "new" issue, so it might be worthwhile for J4 to think
about it.
```

"Duncan Kinnear" <duncan@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz> >
> Now there lies one of the problems.  We can't "pick a database".  Our
customers
> do that!
>
> Basically we have to support access to multiple databases, on multiple
platforms
> (see my follow-up of my original post)

MERANT's compilers (Net Express for NT, Server Express for UNIX) has a
feature called "Open ESQL". basically this allows you to single source an
application with embedded SQL statements that are platform and database
independent.

<a id="t-d54c172ef6"></a>
## Thread: Random versus Dynamic was Re: Antwort: IDCAMS IMBED Attribute — 1 msgs

### Re: Random versus Dynamic was Re: Antwort: IDCAMS IMBED Attribute

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-05T19:48:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pIft5.2$ju4.1466@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<2E492FD6A9E7D2118A9B0008C70DE0A103A28829@sl-ex01.conectiv.com> <39B10434.1F1A75EF@istar.ca>`

```
Actually according to IBM, when access is RANDOM (you only read by key and
do not
READ NEXT - not even compiled in but not executed!), then VSAM will only USE
ONE
D A T A  B U F F E R.   Excess Data buffers IMPEDS the VSAM buffering
especially
if your time between reads is long - VSAM needs the buffer space (your
system group probably
has buffer pools assigned anyways, so you don't get EXCLUSIVE use of those
buffers anyways)

ASK your DASD / VSAM group for 'tuning' info Buffer Pools, etc.  They will
most likely
be very happy to provide guidance.


Even if you read a VSAM KSDS /RRDS file mostly sequentially, if you
'rewrite' or 'delete' using
the same FD, expect a PERFORMANCE HIT as the Buffers are 'invalidated'.  If
you need
to process a Keyed file sequentially (even just partially sequentially) AND
rewrite or delete
as you go along then do this:

SELECT INPUT-MASTER-RD assign to INPUT
    Access is Dynamic  ...
    RECORD KEY IS IM-KEY.
SELECT IO-MASTER-UPD  assign to IOFILE
    Access is RANDOM ...
    RECORD KEY IS IO-KEY.

FD INPUT-MASTER-RD
01 ..
    05  IM-KEY PIC X(nn).
...
FD IO-MASTER-UPD
01 ..
    05 IO-KEY  PIC X(nn)


READ INPUT-MASTER-RD NEXT record
at end set ws-done to true.
IF INPUT-DATE < CUTOFF-DATE
    MOVE  IM-KEY TO IO-KEY
    DELETE IO-RECORD
END-IF

JCL
///RUNPGM EXEC PGM=KSDSCLNR   (/// because otherwise Outlook changes
slashslash to HTTP)
///INPUT   DD DSN=xxxx.xxx,
///            BUFNOD=(whatever_comes_closet_to_1_cyl),
///            BUFNOI=(1_or_2_o_3)
///IOFILE  DD DSN=xxyy.yyy,
///            BUFNOD=1,      (RANDOM never uses more than 1 data buffer!)
///            BUFNOI=(1_or_2_or_3)

Will give much better performance on almost any setup than just coding
DYNAMIC.
IBM's COBOL Language Ref used to tell you that 'if the source containing the
'SELECT' does
NOT have any 'READ NEXT' for that SELECT, the COMPILER CONVERTS the DYNAMIC
to RANDOM ANYWAYS' or word to that affect.  What do you want  to bet it
still does?

� Buffering                 � The default is one index (BUFNI) and two   �
�                           � data buffers (BUFND).  Request additional  �
�                           � data buffers for sequential access and     �
�                           � request additional index buffers for       �
�                           � random access.  Specify both BUFND and     �
�                           � BUFNI when ACCESS IS DYNAMIC.  (See your   �
�                           � Access Method Services Reference manual.)  �
�                           �                                            �
�                           � Avoid coding additional buffers unless     �
�                           � your application will run interactively;   �
�                           � and then code buffers only when            �
�                           � response-time problems arise that might be �
�                           � caused by I/O delay.



� File access modes         � For best performance, access records       �
�                           � sequentially.  Dynamic access is less      �
�                           � efficient than sequential access, but is   �
�                           � more efficient than random access.  Random �
�                           � access results in increased EXCPs because  �
�                           � VSAM must access the index for each        �
�                           � request.                                   �
+---------------------------+------------------------------------------

2.5.5.4 Buffers for Direct Access

For a key-sequenced data set or variable-length RRDS, you can increase
performance for direct processing by increasing the number of index
buffers.  Direct processing always requires a top-down search through
the index.  Many data buffers do not increase performance, because only
one data buffer is used for each access.

2.5.5.4.1 Allocating Data Buffers for Direct Access

Because VSAM does not read ahead buffers for direct processing, only
the minimum number of data buffers are needed.  Only one data buffer is
used for each access.  If you specify more data buffers than the
minimum, this has little beneficial effect.

When processing a data set directly, VSAM reads only one data control
interval at a time.  For output processing (PUT for update), VSAM
immediately writes the updated control interval, if OPTCD=NSP is not
specified in the RPL macro.

2.5.5.4.2 Allocating Index Buffers for Direct Access

If the number of I/O buffers provided for index records is greater than the
number of
requests that require concurrent positioning (STRNO), one buffer is used for
the
highest-level index record.  Any additional buffers are used, as required,
for other index
set index records.  With direct access, you should provide at least enough
index buffers
to be equal to the value of the STRNO parameter of the ACB, plus one if you
want VSAM to
keep the highest-level index record always resident.

Unused index buffers do not degrade performance, so you should always
specify an
adequate number.  For optimum performance, the number of index buffers
should at least
equal the number of high-level index set control intervals plus one per
string to contain the
entire high-level index set and one sequence set control interval per string
in virtual
storage.  Note that additional index buffers will not be used for more than
one sequence
set buffer per string unless shared resource pools are used.  For large data
sets, specify
the number of index buffers equal to the number of index levels.

VSAM reads index buffers one at a time, and if you are using shared
resources, you can
keep your entire index set in storage.  Index buffers are loaded when the
index is referred
to.  When many index buffers are provided, index buffers are not reused
until a requested
index control interval is not in storage.

VSAM keeps as many index set records as the buffer space allows in virtual
storage.
Ideally, the index would be small enough to allow the entire index set to
remain in virtual
storage.  Because the characteristics of the data set cannot allow a small
index, you
should be aware of how index I/O buffers are used so you can determine how
many to
provide.

The following example, illustrated in Figure 30, demonstrates how buffers
are scheduled
for direct access.  Assume the following:

   Two strings

   Three-level index structure as shown

   Three data buffers (one for each string, and one for splits)

   Four index buffers (one for highest level of index, one for second
level, and one for
    each string)


                --------------------------------------------    ------------
----------------
                                Index Buffers                        Data
Buffers
------------    ------------    ------------    ------------    ------------
    ------------
 Direct Get                        String 1       String 2
 from CA            Pool            SS              SS            String 1
String 2
------------    ------------    ------------    ------------    ------------
    ------------
 CA2             IS1    IS2         SS2                           CA2-CI
------------    -----  -----    ------------    ------------    ------------
    ------------
 CA3                                                SS3
CA3-CI
------------    -----  -----    ------------    ------------    ------------
    ------------
 CA6-1st CI             IS3         SS6                          CA6-1st CI
------------    -----  -----    ------------    ------------    ------------
   -------------
 CA6 1st CI                                         SS6
CA6-1st CI
------------    -----  -----    ------------    ------------    ------------
   -------------

                                            ISI

+------------------------------+------------------------------+
              �                              �
�
              �                              �
�
             IS2                            IS3
IS4
    +-------------------+         +---------------------+
+-----------------------+
    �      �     �      �         �       �      �      �        �       �
�       �
    �      �     �      �         �       �      �      �        �       �
�       �
    SS1   SS2   SS3    SS4        SS5    SS6    SS7    SS8       SS9    SS10
SS11    SS12
    �      �     �      �         �       �      �      �        �       �
�       �
    �      �     �      �         �       �      �      �        �       �
�       �
    �      �     �      �         �       �      �      �        �       �
�       �
                                                                
       
    CA1   CA2   CA3    CA4        CA5    CA6    CA7    CA8      CA9
CA100   CA11    CA12


IS - Index Set
SS - Sequence Set
CA - Control Area

Figure 30. Scheduling Buffers for Direct Access


Here's what happens:

    Request 1: A control interval from CA2 is requested by string 1.

       The highest level index set, IS1, is read into an index buffer. IS1
remains in this
        buffer for all requests.

       IS1 points to IS2, which is read into a second index buffer.

       IS2 points to the sequence set, SS2, which is read into an index
buffer for string 1.

       SS2 points to a control interval in CA2.  This control interval is
read into a data buffer
        for string 1.


    Request 2: A control interval from CA3 is requested by string 2.

       IS1 and IS2 remain in their respective buffers.

       SS3 is read into an index buffer for string 2.

       SS3 points to a control interval in CA3.  This control interval is
read into a data buffer
        for string 2.


    Request 3:  The first control interval in CA6 is requested by string 1.

       IS1 remains in its buffer.

       Since IS1 now points to IS3, IS3 is read into the second index
buffer, replacing IS2.

       SS6 is read into an index buffer for string 1.

       SS6 points to the first control interval in CA6.  This control
interval is read into a data
        buffer for string 1.


    Request 4:  The first control interval in CA6 is now requested by string
2.

       IS1 and IS3 remain in their respective buffers.

       SS6 is read into an index buffer for string 2.

       SS6 points to the first control interval in CA6.  This control
interval is read into a data
        buffer for string 2.

       If the string 1 request for this control interval was a GET for
update, the control
        interval would be held in exclusive control, and string 2 would not
be able to access it.



+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
�                                                                        �
� Suggested number of buffers for direct processing:                     �
�                                                                        �
�    Index Buffers                                                      �
�         Minimum = STRNO                                                �
�         Maximum = Number of Index Set Records + STRNO                  �
�    Data Buffers                                                       �
�         STRNO + 1                                                      �
�                                                                        �
+------------------------------------------------------------------------+

2.5.5.5 Buffers for Sequential Access

When you are accessing data sequentially, you can increase performance by
increasing
the number of data buffers.  When there are multiple data buffers, VSAM uses
a
read-ahead function to read the next data control intervals into buffers
before they are
needed.  Having only one index I/O buffer does not hinder performance,
because VSAM
gets to the next control interval by using the horizontal pointers in
sequence set records
rather than the vertical pointers in the index set.  Extra index buffers
have little effect
during sequential processing.

+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
�                                                                        �
� Suggested number of buffers for initial load mode processing:          �
�                                                                        �
�     Index buffers = 3                                                  �
�     Data buffers = 2 * (number of Data CI/CA)                          �
�                                                                        �
+------------------------------------------------------------------------+

Subtopics
2.5.5.5.1 Data Buffers for Sequential Access Using Nonshared Resources

releases and modifications until otherwise indicated in new editions.

numerous tests to reduce I-O impact system-wide, pgm run-times and pgm
IO/CPU times.

## Thread: Looking for COBOL/SQL solution — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: Looking for COBOL/SQL solution

- **From:** "Ray Smith" <Ray.Smith@fujitsu.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-09-06T16:41:00+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p4ktp$aak@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`
- **References:** `<39B42A08.CAB08020@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz> <39B42C58.9A4DC573@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz>`

```
Hi Duncan,

We use Acu4GL with Sybase and had heaps of performance problems until
release 4.3.1.

I have heard along the grape vine (second hand news so beware!!!) that the
Acu4GL MS SQL version is still very slow.

My initial tests seem to indicate that using Acu4GL for Sybase is between 2
to 3 times slower than straight vision files.

This was after some small modifications to code to enforce a "where"
constraint with the selects that Acu4GL automatically generate.

We haven't put this release into a production environment yet so I can't
tell you it was a success but we are confident it won't be more than 3 times
slower than vision.

Acu4GL  can be setup to allow some files to remain in vision and some to be
point to your database.  This is the approach we will be using with index /
code type files being left in Vision and the actual data files to be put
into Sybase. This will help with performance a little, and also gives us /
and our clients a backup plan for higher performance systems ... with the
draw back it isn't in there DB (but might be uploaded daily or weekly for
example).

Not having looked at all the options others have suggested here, but I'd be
pretty surprised if any can improve significantly on what we have achieved
here with Acu4GL and Sybase without major rewrites.  If your looking for
performance from a DB anywhere near vision without re-writing I think you
will be dissappointed.

I would suggest you contact Acu support and see if they can give you some
pointers as to how to improve your performance.
Mentis support us here in Oz and have been a great help sorting out our
Sybase performance problems and numerous others.

Another thing to consider is that DB's (doesn't matter which one) will never
achieve the performance of ISAM files.  Maybe your expections are to high?
I'd be interested if anyone could give actual examples of where moving to a
DB increased performance ... any takers?

Good luck,

Ray Smith


Duncan Kinnear <duncan@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz> wrote in message

> databases:

at runtime to SQL-Statements send to your DB.

Software. But at the moment it only supports Merant Micro Focus Cobol.

You may take a look at www.parkway-software.com and ask for AcuCobol
support...

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).

"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".

where available

CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech

<a id="t-dbaf967df7"></a>
## Thread: SYSLIB - Cannot locate copybook — 3 msgs

### SYSLIB - Cannot locate copybook

- **From:** patrick_finnegan@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-06T15:25:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p5nk3$mfi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


We are moving a COBOLII mainframe application to to AIX.  The AIX IBM
compiler cannot find copy libraries unless the file names are qualified
with full extensions.

e.g

 COPY ig14700.         >   not found

 COPY "ig14700.cpy".   >   found

Both statements use the same compile SYSLIB which is defined as:

export SYSLIB=/ultd/app/pfs/r431cib/XTOR/COBCOPY
export SYSLIB=$SYSLIB:/ultd/app/pfs/r431cib/COBCOPY
export SYSLIB=$SYSLIB:/ultd/app/pfs/r431cib/SQLCOPY
export SYSLIB=$SYSLIB:/usr/lpp/cics/include

We can run the code through a rexx script to generate the quotes plus
filename extensions but is this really necessary?

The IBM compiler is 1.1.1.2 running on AIX 4.3.3.13.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: SYSLIB - Cannot locate copybook

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-06T11:49:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p5sip$ung$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8p5nk3$mfi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
What is your COBCPYEXT environment variable set to?  Also, is your file
really defined with a "cpy" and not "CPY" extension?  I know that UNIX-like
systems are often case sensitive, and this COULD be your problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SYSLIB - Cannot locate copybook

- **From:** patrick_finnegan@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T17:20:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p8ioq$26n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8p5nk3$mfi$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8p5sip$ung$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
We had already the checked that the library names matched the
literals.  The problem was caused by the IBM Cobol compiler resolving
literals to upper case e.g  call copy1 gets resolved to COPY1 but the
file name is copy1.cpy.  I changed the copy file names to uppercase and
everything worked ok.  So now call copy1 gets resolved to COPY1 which
matches COPY1.cpy



In article <8p5sip$ung$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> What is your COBCPYEXT environment variable set to?  Also, is your
file
> really defined with a "cpy" and not "CPY" extension?  I know that
UNIX-like
> systems are often case sensitive, and this COULD be your problem.
>

IBM

qualified
> > with full extensions.
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

plus

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> read/write on the COBOL-CICS application trough the WIN32 Emulator.

For what it is worth;

You can always parse through the current windows' on your computer, and
obtain the window handle of your emulator by checking the window title
(presuming there is at least one word in the window title that will identify
the window as a terminal window), then you can have your application send a
print screen command to that particular window and finally you can grab that
print screen from the clipboard. This will however feed you with a graphical
image of the screen, so I don't know if that is suitable. If it is, you
don't have to do anything with the emulator or the Cobol app.

Let me know if this sounds interesting and I can provide you with directions
for how to do a print screen.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-7f45048543"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu + comp — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu + comp

- **From:** Ekky.Panneck@hanse.antar.com (Ekky Panneck)
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T04:53:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d5c_200009070716@antares.antar.com>`

```
Someone there who kindly point me the way to find out
about COMP-Fields with Fujitsu v3 ?

Many TIA,  Ekky
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu + comp

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T21:46:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9tYt5.475$Ej6.108129@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<d5c_200009070716@antares.antar.com>`

```
Sure.

There are three kinds of COMP: (1)BINARY(COMP-4), (2) COMP-5; and
(3)PACKED DECIMAL (COMP-3).
For BINARY and COMP-5, there are two sizes: 2-bytes and 4-bytes

BINARY is just what you think: powers of two by bit. Decimal 1234 = 04
D2 (for 2 byte) and 00 00 04 D2 (for 4 byte)
COMP-5 is the same as BINARY, but the bytes are flipped for
Intel-processor convenience. Decimal 1234 = 04 D2 (2 byte) 00 00 04 D2
(4 byte).

COMP-5 on an Intel machine is much more efficient for arithmetic than
BINARY.

On the other hand, if sorting, use COMP4 (BINARY).

Hope this helps. If not, make your question more specific (like why
you need to know).


"Ekky Panneck" <Ekky.Panneck@hanse.antar.com> wrote in message
news:d5c_200009070716@antares.antar.com...
> Someone there who kindly point me the way to find out
> about COMP-Fields with Fujitsu v3 ?
>
> Many TIA,  Ekky
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu + comp

- **From:** Enojon <enojon@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-08T19:05:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B970A7.C07C0836@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<d5c_200009070716@antares.antar.com> <9tYt5.475$Ej6.108129@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
Decimal 1234 = D2 04(2 byte) D2 04 00 00 (4 byte).

Floating pt is comp-2 and comp-3 but uses proprietary formatting.
and is 4-bytes and 8-bytes respectively, in Little-Endian  (x86 style),
where mantissa occupies first 3 or 5 bytes, and exponent is last.

additional datatypes are BOOLEAN, and (implied) BIT via PIC 1 clause,
POINTER, and INDEX.

> Sure.
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

<a id="t-42021cae14"></a>
## Thread: Looking for COBOL/SQL solution — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: Looking for COBOL/SQL solution

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T01:08:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rhgdrs4hb64f7u387ba5cvmmhssddatkvo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<39B42A08.CAB08020@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz> <39b4430d.85304083@207.126.101.100>`

```
>>Does anyone know of any other methods/systems/etc. for using COBOL on
>>relational databases?  We are prepared to shift away from Acu if we can
>>find a way to achieve this.

>>Is anyone out there actually doing this successfully?

there will, of course, always be middleware you'll need to move to a
relational database, but the big thing is to verify the cobol, when
rewritten, is done according to the principles of indexed io in both
'languages'.

tables will be a problem, as sql does not control the cache size very
well and dual move reads must have the sql statement written in the
correct where order.

the problem with giving users sql access is simple. it will require
more hardware, period. there is no way to do on-the-fly sql statements
without a programmer in between it to make it fast, and every
inbetween layer will take extra hardware, there is no way around it.


reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL/SQL solution

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@cegi.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T00:28:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39b726fe_2@news5.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<39B42A08.CAB08020@mccarthyNOSPAMHEREPLEASE.co.nz> <39b4430d.85304083@207.126.101.100> <rhgdrs4hb64f7u387ba5cvmmhssddatkvo@4ax.com>`

```
That's why you avoid using READ/WRITE syntax all together and use
embedded SQL. :)

It sounds counter intutive of course, but it really isn't. The SQL database
will open a cursor, from which you can fetch all the returned rows you want.
You code your SQL to return them in the desired order and with the desired
data elements.

Updates and inserts are just as simple.

The beatuy of using an SQL Database really shows up when you an push off
doing all the index manipulation and copmlex data retrieval to a system
built just to do that.

-Paul


G Moore wrote in message ...
>>>Does anyone know of any other methods/systems/etc. for using COBOL on
>>>relational databases?  We are prepared to shift away from Acu if we can
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

- **References:** `<KpFt5.25643$p5.906724@newsread03.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8p7oe0$lr5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> writes:

>The manual that you want to look at is at:
> http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEV1000/CCONTENTS

>This is a NON-trivial task, but that manual includes ALL the information that
>you would need.

Following the save area chain isn't hard.  Knowing when you are at
the beginning isn't easy.   I believe that there is a system call that
will give the address of the beginning, though.  For standard linkage
convention (the URL above also inclues FASTLINK and XPLINK),

If R13 contains a save area address then load it into another register,

   LR  R2,R13

   then

   L R2,4(,R2)

Will be the address of the callers save area. 

Register 15 in the save area points to the entry point, and 4(R15)
to the length of the name and 5(R15) is the beginning of the name.

   L   R4,16(,R2)   R15 in the save area pointed to by R2.
   SR  R3,R3
   IC  R3,4(,R4)
   BCTR  R3,0
   EX    R3,MVC 

and an MVC to execute to copy the name to buffer BUF.

MVC  MVC BUF(0),5(R4)

I hope this helps,

-- glen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS call chain

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T23:03:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39B81EBC.ED7B69C4@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<KpFt5.25643$p5.906724@newsread03.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8p7oe0$lr5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8p8u43$qmu@gap.cco.caltech.edu>`

```

glen herrmannsfeldt wrote:
<snip>
> Following the save area chain isn't hard.  Knowing when you are at
> the beginning isn't easy.   I believe that there is a system call that
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> I hope this helps,
<snip>

Actually, this will not work in many cases.  If the program is
one by IBM and certain vendors, the R15 EPA (Entry Point Address)
may not actually be a 47F????? (Branch R15+???) to jump past the
length and literal area that you refer to.  Some times you must
use the CDE chain to figure some of this out, particularly when
the program loaded was actually a module (a group of object decks
pre-linked into one large loadable entity).

Running the R13 chain (RSA - Register Save Area) may not work, as
it may be broken by a program that violates the standard.

One may find this easier to accomplish by taking a dump using
SYSMDUMP and IPCS to read the chains to determine who called
who.  I did not look at the book that was referenced by the other
poster, however, if it is the one I'm thinking of, you will need
it.  You might also try OS Debugging for the COBOL Programmer by
Murach (assuming it is still in print) -- www.murach.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS call chain

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** gah@ugcs.caltech.edu (glen herrmannsfeldt)
- **Date:** 2000-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pp2ef$7kl@gap.cco.caltech.edu>`
- **References:** `<KpFt5.25643$p5.906724@newsread03.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8p7oe0$lr5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8p8u43$qmu@gap.cco.caltech.edu> <39B81EBC.ED7B69C4@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com> writes:

>glen herrmannsfeldt wrote:
><snip>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> 
<snip>

>Actually, this will not work in many cases.  If the program is
>one by IBM and certain vendors, the R15 EPA (Entry Point Address)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>pre-linked into one large loadable entity).

Certainly the name in the entry is optional. 

>Running the R13 chain (RSA - Register Save Area) may not work, as
>it may be broken by a program that violates the standard.

This is harder to believe.  Every program must save the address of the
caller's save area to restore it before returning.  It could store it
somewhere else, but why?

-- glen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS call chain

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pp2ur$qju$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<KpFt5.25643$p5.906724@newsread03.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8p7oe0$lr5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8p8u43$qmu@gap.cco.caltech.edu> <39B81EBC.ED7B69C4@neo.rr.com> <8pp2ef$7kl@gap.cco.caltech.edu>`

```
Check the "history" of PL/I for examples of where IBM "generated" code did
NOT use R13 save areas.  Other software has ALSO done this as well.
```

service) aborted'

The code works fine on the mainframe.

## Thread: MVS Call Chain — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### MVS Call Chain

- **From:** "searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-07T22:22:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3Yt5.4$ju4.3741@news2.mia>`

```
After 20 something years of programing, I've never NEEDED to know
the original 'load' module.  The Jobname/Number sometimes, but never
so far the first pgm.

Whatever is the reason?
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Call Chain

- **From:** "John Cloughly" <jcloughly@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-08T15:18:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cr7u5.31876$C42.1407254@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<a3Yt5.4$ju4.3741@news2.mia>`

```

"searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:a3Yt5.4$ju4.3741@news2.mia...
> After 20 something years of programing, I've never NEEDED to know
> the original 'load' module.  The Jobname/Number sometimes, but never
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Let's see 2000 minus 1979 = 21.  I got you beat by 1 year.  (LOL)

Most often I've seen this tactic used in assembler to fool batch programs
into being used by CICS.  Or, to switch parm areas depending on who's
calling.  In OO land this could be a entrance into the objects private code.

But this time is different...
First it's a Cobol only environment.  This particuar chain structure is used
by numerous root modules in a huge application.  The linkage is max out.
Now the client wants a new root to grab information only a module (x) levels
deep can supply.  They want to make the mod only to the specific module
which has the data.  They want the new code to execute only when the new
root module is used.  AND, they don't want to pass a switch or extra I/O for
a file to pass parms.  The only solution I've figured is to have the root
create a temp storage area.  The new code at module (x) deep will identify
the root and if it's a hit get the temp storage and fill it.  Again this
would be a no brainer in Assembler.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Call Chain

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-08T22:05:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8%gu5.5$ju4.3783@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<a3Yt5.4$ju4.3741@news2.mia> <cr7u5.31876$C42.1407254@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Consider using a data element or file that is 'GLOBAL'.

John Cloughly <jcloughly@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:cr7u5.31876$C42.1407254@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net...

code.
>
> But this time is different...
> First it's a Cobol only environment.  This particuar chain structure is
used

> Now the client wants a new root to grab information only a module (x)
levels
> deep can supply.  They want to make the mod only to the specific module
> which has the data.  They want the new code to execute only when the new
> root module is used.  AND, they don't want to pass a switch or extra I/O
for
> a file to pass parms.  The only solution I've figured is to have the root
> create a temp storage area.  The new code at module (x) deep will identify
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
