- **Message-ID:** `<hWbw5.895$aY1.44662@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<8FAC68F10thomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116> <39bcf9b3.46999410@207.126.101.100> <DAdv5.1021$oz2.74580@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8FAD538DFthomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116> <cmxv5.328$Aj2.49734@nnrp2.sbc.net> <VaJv5.192$IG1.21794@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <22C95AB6F79C4357.CABBE23A0BA68021.6709AF625B0AF110@lp.airnews.net>`

```
More uproar over Amazon's privacy policy:

Public Protest of Privacy Policy: Two Groups Leave Amazon

The Electronic Privacy Information Center (EPIC) and Junkbusters, two
privacy rights advocacy groups, have withdrawn from Amazon's
Affiliates
program in protest over Amazon's recent revision of its privacy
policy,
which reclassified customer information a saleable "asset."

The EPIC Bookstore (http://www.epic.org/bookstore) has been part of
the
Amazon Affiliates program since 1996, making it one of the first
organizations to join the program. Executive director Matt Rotenberg
said,
"Because Amazon announced that it could no longer guarantee that it
would
not disclose customer information to third parties, and in the absence
of
legal or technical means to assure privacy for Amazon customers, we
have
decided that we can no longer continue our relationship." He called
the
Amazon change part of "the slow erosion of online privacy under the
industry's self-regulatory approach."

Junkbusters president Jason Catlett called Amazon's recent revision "a
big
step backwards." He added, "Like most companies, Amazon seems to think
of
the data it holds about its customers as its own property to do
whatever it
wishes with, regardless of the interests of the people what the data
is
about. That's why Americans urgently need legal rights to control
their own
data."

Oddly, Amazon still cites EPIC on its page about credit card safety.
The
text states: "We respect your personal privacy. We do not now sell or
rent
our list of customers to anyone. In fact, the Electronic Privacy
Information Center cites the quality of our concern with customer
privacy
as one of the reasons they became an Amazon.com Associate." As if that
isn't enough, Amazon also indicates "if you would like to make sure we
never sell or rent information about you to third parties, just send
an
e-mail message to never@amazon.com." This information is out-of-date,
apparently.

Across the Atlantic, Privacy International (http://www.privacy.org),
an
advocacy group in the U.K., has objected to Amazon.co.uk's policy of
sending and storing personal data outside Europe, something customers
automatically consent to by using the site.

Apparently, the European Data Protection law specifically limits such
transfers to countries that do not have adequate legal protection. In
a
open letter to Amazon.co.uk, Simon Davies, director of Privacy
International writes, "I believe that your current practices
constitute
unfair processing under the terms of the UK Data Protection Act of
1998. I
am therefore writing to the UK Data Protection Commissioner requesting
an
investigation of Amazon.co.uk's practices, and asking her to issue an
interim order prohibiting these transfers."

In another ironic twist, Amazon.co.uk does not appear to have recently
changed its privacy policy, which still reads:

"Amazon.co.uk does not sell, trade or rent your personal information
to
others. We may choose to do so in the future with trustworthy third
parties, but you can tell us not to by sending a blank e-mail message
to
never@amazon.co.uk."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free-Cobol?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39c386ca.3577735@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<8FAC68F10thomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116> <39bcf9b3.46999410@207.126.101.100> <DAdv5.1021$oz2.74580@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8FAD538DFthomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116> <cmxv5.328$Aj2.49734@nnrp2.sbc.net> <VaJv5.192$IG1.21794@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <22C95AB6F79C4357.CABBE23A0BA68021.6709AF625B0AF110@lp.airnews.net> <hWbw5.895$aY1.44662@nnrp2.sbc.net>`

```
This is really off topic for the COBOL news group, but take a bit step
back for a second and realize this:

These types of lists have been for sale for many many years.  Credit
card companies buy and sell the lists.  State agencies sell lists.
Credit companies sell lists.  Retailers with in house charge accounts
sell lists.  When you fill out a warrenty card, that information is
collected and sold.  The customer base *IS* an asset and if Amazon was
sold the customer list would go with it.  

This is marketing and it is how it has been done for years and years.
The internet does not afford you any MORE or LESS privacy protection
than anything else.  There's nothing new here.

NOTE WELL - I am not supporting the colleciton or sale of personal
information -- I'm just stating the facts as they exist and have
existed for quite some time.  AMAZON did not CHANGE their policy as
far as I can tell  - they just clarified it  -- Bluntly.

I'm familiar with the laws you are speaking of.  They relate only to
government collected information.  "Comsumer" and "credit" information
is not legally protected.  However, these groups do self police and
have instituted VOLUNTARY safeguards.  These include only releasing
data that has been collected privately, but verified from "public"
data.  There is a whole industry group (the name of which escapes me
at present) that the different credit agencies belong to and support.
It was designed to PREVENT legeslation from being necessary.

When it comes to consumer data (Warrenty cards and the like), however
- all bets are off.

>

information

There's an easy way to protect your privacy: lie. Examples:

Occupation: Shepherd/Admiral of the
Fleet/Cartwright/Equestrian/Whittler
DOB: 7/4/1776 (I like 7/4/2002)

- **Message-ID:** `<39C64365.A6070338@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<8FAC68F10thomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116> <39bcf9b3.46999410@207.126.101.100> <DAdv5.1021$oz2.74580@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8FAD538DFthomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116> <cmxv5.328$Aj2.49734@nnrp2.sbc.net> <VaJv5.192$IG1.21794@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <22C95AB6F79C4357.CABBE23A0BA68021.6709AF625B0AF110@lp.airnews.net> <hWbw5.895$aY1.44662@nnrp2.sbc.net> <39c386ca.3577735@207.126.101.100> <EB46F0DA9D33D264.092295D835A0D227.7D0E66A4F5DFF67C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:

> Thane, while you are correct in your assessment of list selling, I
> believe that in rescent years legaislation here in the United States
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> obviously doesn't) apply to the Internet.

Possibly some state laws but nothing on a national level except in specific
fields, e.g. medical records. I believe that some have been proposed at the
national level, but none have yet been enacted. The application of state
laws to corporations doing business in several states is complex and often
questionable. The gathering of information might take place in one state but
the sale/dissemination might take place in another state. (And delivery
might be in yet a third state.) Which state's laws have jurisdiction?
```

thomas.gonschor@vph-bensberg.de (Thomas Gonschor) wrote:

>Hi!
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thomas

Thomas:

The best place to get an inexpensive compiler or to learn how to write
COBOL code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

The book only costs about $20.00 US$ and includes the compiler at no
additional charge.

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.chapters.ca
http://www.indigo.ca
http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com
http://www.amazon.com




Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free-Cobol?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-09-12T09:06:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39BE628F.53530FEA@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<8FAC68F10thomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116> <39bd0cf6.13996186@news.epix.net> <8FAD510BBthomasgonschorvphben@195.20.224.116>`

```
Thomas Gonschor wrote:

> Hi Bob!
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Great! I only need 24 hours to learn it? ;-)

COBOL's kind of like touch typing. I can teach you everything you need
to know about touch typing in under 5 minutes, but you'll need at least
a couple of days of continuous practice to learn it. (And without
*guided* practice, it'll probably take you longer. If, like most
non-typists your fingers get sore after an hour or two of typing, then
you can't practice continuously so that'll also take you longer.)

You can probably learn all you need to know about COBOL in 24 hours, but
it'll take you a while to learn to use what you know.
```

## Thread: MicroFocus COBOL and WIN2000 — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus COBOL and WIN2000

- **From:** "Rich Cullen" <rich@sparksit.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-11T13:13:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pilpv$cg4@dispatch.concentric.net>`

```
We have an application written in MicroFocus COBOL that we are trying to run
on a WIN2000 server system.

From workstations, we can run the application fine with the files on the
server.  When we try to run the application on the server, we get the
message "COBOL run time library not installed - file not found."  Our
application developer is not any help.  They essentially say, "We don't do
windows; run it on NetWare."

We need to be able to run the application on WIN2000 in order to use Windows
Terminal Server for a remote office.

Is there a simple fix for this problem?  We know very little about COBOL.

Thanks for any help.

Rich Cullen
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL and WIN2000

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-11T11:52:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XJ9v5.1938$kB1.3693@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<8pilpv$cg4@dispatch.concentric.net>`

```
Rich,

It sounds like the Run-Time System files are missing.  You might try
installing Micro Focus on the server - this should fix the problem.  You
could also just copy the RTS, but you would have to pay for another
developer license due to MF license constraints - unless you are running
NetExpress v3.0 (not 3.1!), which allows you to ship the RTS free of
charge.

Hope it helps

Todd Lucas
E-Mail:  Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net
Web:   http://www.c-cubed.net

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL and WIN2000

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-09-11T12:08:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pjak2$pf0$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8pilpv$cg4@dispatch.concentric.net>`

```
"Rich Cullen" <rich@sparksit.com> wrote in message
news:8pilpv$cg4@dispatch.concentric.net...
> From workstations, we can run the application fine with the files on the
> server.  When we try to run the application on the server, we get the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> We need to be able to run the application on WIN2000 in order to use
Windows
> Terminal Server for a remote office.
>
> Is there a simple fix for this problem?  We know very little about COBOL.

This sounds like an installation issue. The thing is, when you attach as a
remote user to the Windows terminal server. You get your own separate system
directory, which differs from the standard directory that you get when
accessing locally. This effectively affects the searchpath for your
applications. There are various ways of solving this issue. But first of all
you will have to determine what files are missing for the remote connection,
then you will have to decide where is the appropriate location for the
files. Due to security issues, you can't just give access to all over, and
copying the files around probably ain't a good solution either due to
possible maintenance problems. I guess you should discuss this issue with
Merant, as they sooner or later must target this issue, unless they already
have (likely).

To put it straight, your remote connection is to be considered a computer
that does not have your application installed yet because the initial
installation executed locally on the server did not pay attention to being
installed on a Windows Terminal Server.

Note: Depending on the application, this may not only apply to DLL's, but
also registry settings and other files. It is pretty simple to fix when you
know what you have to fix, but if you don't know what is impacted by the
remote connection, it is hard.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL and WIN2000

- **From:** "Rich Cullen" <rich@sparksit.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-11T21:58:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pjkht$cfe@dispatch.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<8pilpv$cg4@dispatch.concentric.net> <8pjak2$pf0$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle,

Thanks for the help; but I don't think it is really a terminal server issue.
I get the same message if I try to run application from a DOS box on the
server.

I seem to be dealing with a DOS version of this application.  I copied the
files from the old NetWare directory to the new directory on the W2K server
and the application runs fine from the workstations.  It runs just like it
did on the old NetWare server.

Could there be some files on the workstations that are not on the server?
The more I think about it, the more I think that could be the problem.  What
would the files be, and where would they be located?  Maybe this "run time
library" file needs to be on the server in order to run the application on
the server or on terminal server.

Thanks again.
Rich

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL and WIN2000

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-12T09:28:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pkt82$h8d$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8pilpv$cg4@dispatch.concentric.net> <8pjak2$pf0$1@news.cerf.net> <8pjkht$cfe@dispatch.concentric.net>`

```
Rich,

> I seem to be dealing with a DOS version of this application.  I copied the
> files from the old NetWare directory to the new directory on the W2K
server
> and the application runs fine from the workstations.  It runs just like it
> did on the old NetWare server.
>

I don't think you are necessarily missing any files. On old Micro Focus
products eg 2.5 or 3.4, the executables produced could run on either DOS or
OS/2. 32-bit Windows platforms, including Win2000, will default to trying to
run the OS/2 part of the executable and in order to do that you need to have
the directory containing the run-time DLLs eg COBLIB.DLL added to the
Os2LibPath system environment variable. Alternatively, you can use the
FORCEDOS command to run the DOS part of the application.
```

### Re: Help Business Process CM

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-11T12:14:04-04:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8pj0fc$6iq$1@news.hitter.net>`

For any Configuration Management questions, begin with
< http://www.iac.honeywell.com/Pub/Tech/CM/ >. (Been there)

The Configuration Management newsgroup is:
< news:comp.software.config-mgmt  >. (Haven't been there)

Some of these issues are also discussed in:
< news:comp.software-eng >. (Been there, many good people)

FAQs for both groups may be obtained through
< www.faqs.org >.

HTH, good luck!
------------------
Rick Smith

<a id="t-dd3acf126d"></a>
## Thread: An SP2 bug. — 31 msgs

### An SP2 bug.

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-12T11:27:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pl3u5$4io$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

Here is a story about a funny bug in SP2.

My vertical-market POS systems use a three-character
(SECURE) password. People started calling me up and
saying that they could only enter one or two characters
into the three-character password field.

I tried it and it worked fine. Some people had a
problem and some didn't. Some people could type one
character, some people could type two, but they
couldn't type three.

I normally use 'SAM' as my password, and, for no
particular reason, I used 'JOE' instead. Now, all
I could do was type in one character.

Turns out that typing a lower-case 'o' (ASCII 111)
terminates the accept (CONVERSE) of the field.

This is what happened. . .

Programs are selected from a System Menu. The Menu
expects a password for certain programs. For reasons
not worth going into, I had put two pushbuttons on
the Menu panel. Later, I changed my mind and removed
them both and recompiled.

The SP2 manual gives the KEY-VALUES as ranging from 001
to 404. To avoid conflict I always use numbers like 711
712, and so on for the KEY-VALUE of the pushbuttons.

I had made a typo when assigning the value to the first
button and made it 111 (lower-case 'o').

Even though the button was DELETED, the program now
terminates when you type a lower-case 'o'.

So, as soon as you go to type in 'joe', the 'o'
terminates the accept. 'JOE', of course, works fine.

Note to Bob and Andy:  I manufactured a test program
which demonstrates this phenomenon if you want it. . .

Thanks

Tony Dilworth




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: An SP2 bug.

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-09-12T20:33:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8pl3u5$4io$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Tony:

It's actually not a bug.  Here's how it works in sp2:

When you create a pushbutton and assign a function key (ASCII Decimal
key value to return control to the application), THAT specific key
value is added to a list of control keys in the panel definition.

Even though you delete the pushbutton field, we don't want to
necessarily assume that you also want to get rid of the control key as
well, therefore we leave it intact in the control key list.

The reason that the application is prematurely returning control to
your COBOL program from sp2 when "Joe" is entered is because you have
established the "o" as a control key.  sp2 encounters the "o" and
passes control back.

If you access the control key list, you can delete the ASCII 111 key
definition and it will no longer return control to your application
when the "o" key is pressed.

Again, we don't like to assume that you want to get rid of a control
key just because you delete a pushbutton field.

You simply need to modify the control key list after deleting the
pushbutton.

Please let me know if this fixes the problem.

Thanks!


Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>Hi:
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

I'm with you, Thane. We do the same thing. We like the fact that we can
remove a pushbutton without removing the control key functionality.

Seems like someone once said something like "You can please some of the
people all the time and all of the people some of the time but you
can't please all the people all the time." As software developers, we
should all be aware of this.

If Bob and Andy change the way pushbuttons and control keys work in
SP2,I know of at least 5 people that won't like it.

I think that we can sometimes ask too much of a tool. The more it does,
the less control we have. I would rather have a tool that took care of
the basics and let me be creative as needed. We've all thrown away
tools that took control out of our hands, now haven't we?

John Chafin

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: An SP2 bug.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NE5w5.9645$XS1.59645@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<8pl3u5$4io$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net> <8pnjnk$41t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39bfd8f2.65096157@207.126.101.100> <8por0l$ki8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
John, you've touched on a fundamental issue here.  There are two very
different schools of thought on software tools, which I call 'monolith
tools' and 'simple tools'.  The 'monolith tools' folks want their tools
to support every function known to man, with every conceivable complex
function already available, so they only have to 'push a button' to get
it done.  The 'simple tools' folks (of which I am one) want a complete
set of essentially one function tools, which can be combined in many ad
hoc ways to do whatever tasks they want (e.g. "Software Tools" by Brian
W. Kernighan and P. L. Plauger).  What the monolith folks don't seem to
realize, or perhaps don't mind, is that complexity inevitably results
in inflexibility.  If you don't believe it, just look at how long it
takes to do simple things like delete all .BAK files in a subdirectory
with Windows as opposed to DOS.  To my mind, with software tools, the
word 'flexibility' is virtually synonymous with 'power'.  Just give me
the pieces, I'll figure out how to put them together. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: An SP2 bug.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C0EE4F.61649B9D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8pl3u5$4io$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net> <8pnjnk$41t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39bfd8f2.65096157@207.126.101.100> <8por0l$ki8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <NE5w5.9645$XS1.59645@news2.atl>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:

> John, you've touched on a fundamental issue here.  There are two very
> different schools of thought on software tools, which I call 'monolith
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the pieces, I'll figure out how to put them together. :-)

There are compromise positions.  The Windows API seems to me to be too tight
for most use.  And of course, compiled languages have taken over assembly for
most applications.  What we really want is to pick our tools.  Dragging and
dropping a button works fine if we have the opportunity to get behind it and
change it as need be.  Calling a library function works - as long as we can
use alternatives when we want.

As it is, I like command line functions.  It is like typing vs using a
dictaphone - harder to learn, but easier to use.  I hated it when my dos
editor went GUI and it doesn't automatically know where I want my files
anymore.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: An SP2 bug.

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-09-13T14:44:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39bf8f74.6104881@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8pl3u5$4io$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net> <8pnjnk$41t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>In article <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net>,
>  flexus.stop.the.spam at epix dot net wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>As DD pointed out, it's not a bug it's a feature.

It's actually not a feature either.  It was designed to work that way.
There is a good reason for the software to continue working the way it
does currently.  Please read on......

>It may be a feature but it certainly walked like a bug,
>talked like a bug. . .
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>be deleted too.

Tony:

The problem is that the control key value is stored in a single
location and the editor doesn't check other pushbutton or icon fields
to see if the same control key has been assigned to more than one
field.

For example.....You create a pushbutton and assign a key value of
ASCII 317 (F3 key).

Next you create an icon with a bitmap pushbutton, and assign it an
ASCII 317 (F3 key).

The end user can click on a pushbutton or on an icon to accomplish the
same result.

Now the user interface designer decides to scrap the pushbutton
because of the duplicity or perhaps they really meant to assign the F2
key to the pushbutton and accidently assigned the F3 key.

If the F3 key gets automatically deleted from the control key list
when the pushbutton is deleted, the icon field will no longer work
properly.

That's why the elimination of control keys has been separated from the
elimination of pushbutton or icon fields.

It's not a bug or a feature.  I'm sure that it is a headache to delete
the key separately, but it would also be a headache to have to
remember to go back and reassign a control key to the screen.

Perhaps we should pop up a little box when a pushbutton or icon field
is deleted, displaying the message......

"Do you want the Control Key to also be deleted?"

This would solve the problem, but then that annoying pop up message
box would be displayed every time you want to delete an icon or
pushbutton field.

Or we could check all other fields on the panel to make sure that the
control key hasn't been assigned to multiple fields.

I'll pass on the enhancement suggestion.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
Bob,

Why don't you take the enhancement request a little further. I'm sure

John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com> wrote:

>Bob,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>ticket!

John:

All of your enhancement requests above have already been scheduled for
version 73.6 which will be released on June 30, 2572.

The bad news is that it won't support Microsoft Windows anymore.
We'll only be supporting the latest and greatest operating system,
Microsoft Peephole, which allows you to really see what's going on
"behind the scenes."

You'll be happy to know that the newest version will anticipate when
you are ready to work on the software and launch itself.  It will also
include a feature which detects when you are bored and launches a web
browser which automatically takes you to your favorite entertainment
portal.

It can also be configured to automatically switch back from the
browser to your COBOL source code editor, if it senses that your boss
is on his way to your cubicle.

Oh....and at 11:55 AM each day, it will also prepare an excellent
gourmet lunch for you.  Early beta test results show that the chicken
marsala is excellent!

;-)


Bob Wolfe, flexus

##### ↳ ↳ Re: An SP2 bug.

- **From:** "Jason S. Adams" <jsa@kona.homeip.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000915.001901.28819@kona.homeip.net>`
- **References:** `<8pl3u5$4io$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net>`

```
In article <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net>, flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
wrote:

> Tony:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is added to a list of control keys in the panel definition.
 
It appears that the bug here was that the system let Tony assign
a normal letter key as function key on a panel that supported 
alphabetic data entry without even warning him that doing so would
prevent using that key to generate a normal character.  
Allowing such an assignment is fundamentally incompatible with
a data entry screen.

Yes, tony made the typo, but the degree of testing he would have to
do to prevent this bug getting out into the wild (every possible letter
and number keyed into every panel) would seem to warrant a 
check for reasonableness by the editor, and the generation of
a warning message.

Of course, you would want to be able to override the warning if
you had some valid reason not to let "Joe" enter his name in
that particular panel.  

>>I had made a typo when assigning the value to the first button and made
>>it 111 (lower-case 'o').
>>
>>Even though the button was DELETED, the program now terminates when you
>>type a lower-case 'o'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: An SP2 bug.

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39c21dcd.479068@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8pl3u5$4io$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net> <20000915.001901.28819@kona.homeip.net>`

```
"Jason S. Adams" <jsa@kona.homeip.net> wrote:

>In article <39be8fcc.28905401@news.epix.net>, flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
>wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>a data entry screen.

Jason:

In this specific case, the pressing of the alphabetic function key
could indeed serve both purposes...i.e. 1) returning control back to
the COBOL application from the screen handler as well as 2) passing
back the actual key value to the COBOL application.

While I do agree that it is used very infrequently on data entry
screens (only when data validation must be done on a
character-by-character basis), it is used more often on entry fields
for password entry screens.

>Yes, tony made the typo, but the degree of testing he would have to
>do to prevent this bug getting out into the wild (every possible letter
>and number keyed into every panel) would seem to warrant a 
>check for reasonableness by the editor, and the generation of
>a warning message.

The only difficulty is the fact that what is reasonable for one
application may not be for another application.  Judson probably
expressed the philosophy best in his prior post when he indicated that
there are two schools of thought.  One is that the development tool
should attempt to do all things for all programmers in a pushbutton
approach and the other is that the tool should provide all of the
fundamental building blocks and allow the programmer to assemble them
in the desired fashion.  The danger of tools which try too hard to
protect the programmer from a typo is that they tend to grow into
massive applications that become restrictive rather than flexible.

A 15 second check of the panel control key list before saving the
screen definition would have displayed all control keys which have
been established for the specific screen.

The other capability offered is that the programmer has the ability to
check every panel property for each and every panel through the panel
maintenance functions.  Instead of slogging through every panel to
check the control key list, a COBOL program supplied (SP2GLOB) can be
modified to read the control key list (or any other panel
characteristic) and report the control key list back to the programmer
on a panel-by-panel basis for one panel, select panels or all panels.


In other words, the capability is there for the programmer to write
their own customized panel verification, testing and maintenance tools
for any panel property or properties desired....in COBOL.  This puts
more control in the hands of the programmer who may wish to check
every panel (and even modify every characteristic of every panel)
globally for control keys, pushbuttons captions or any other panel
characteristic.

>Of course, you would want to be able to override the warning if
>you had some valid reason not to let "Joe" enter his name in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>type a lower-case 'o'.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: Calling a non-COBOL program from a COBOL program on OS/390 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Calling a non-COBOL program from a COBOL program on OS/390

- **From:** davecow@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-12T17:42:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8plpu7$92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
  I have a desire to call a non-COBOL (compiled REXX) program from a
COBOL program on the OS/390 (IBM MVS/TSO) platform.

  Does anyone have experience with this?

  When I run my COBOL program it gives me an error that basically says
it can't find my REXX program.  I believe the system is looking in the
same PDS as the COBOL program resides.

  The REXX program I have has been compiled, but since the RECFM (Record
Format) of the COBOL PDS is 'U', I can't compile the REXX program to it
(the REXX compiler what's a RECFM of 'FB' [Fixed Block]).

  I tried adding a SYSEXEC DD to my JCL to point to my PDS with the
compiled REXX program, but this didn't work out either.

  I would greatly appreciate any help I can get.  I don't think this is
a REXX specific problem, so if you have done this in another language
(ie. 'C', JAVA, etc).  Please feel free to respond.

TIA,

Dave C.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a non-COBOL program from a COBOL program on OS/390

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-09-12T23:21:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000912192145.26236.00000744@nso-cr.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8plpu7$92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8plpu7$92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, davecow@my-deja.com writes:

>  I tried adding a SYSEXEC DD to my JCL to point to my PDS with the
>compiled REXX program, but this didn't work out either.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm not exactly sure if this applies or not, but we are able to call 
PL/1, Assembler, and COBOL from COBOL programs.  I believe that
all the 'called' modules that we use must be in the 'look-aside' library 
or we point to a specific 'extra' list via JOBLIB/STEPLIB type override 
cards.

One other thing to consider is how was the program compiled?
Was it compiled with static or dynamic linking?
Static linking would imply that the code is bound in during the 
Link edit phase and the Linkage editor must be able to locate
the REXX program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a non-COBOL program from a COBOL program on OS/390

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <djacot@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pqi04$ilg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8plpu7$92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It IS a REXX specific problem. As you remarked, a 'compiled' REXX is not
really compiled, but rather sort of pre-processed. It has not the
load-module format an cannot be called directly.
Call IRXJCL or IRXEXEC providing them with the name of the REXX EXEC you
want to use as parameter.
It does not matter whether the REXX is compiled or not, the call is the
same.
Refer to chapter '12.2.1.2  Invoking IRXJCL From a REXX Exec or a
Program' in the manual 'OS/390 TSO/E REXX Reference', Document Number
SC28-1975-03, Program Number 5647-A01. You can access this manual at:
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IKJ3A330/CCONTE
NTS?SHELF=IKJOSE30

In article <8plpu7$92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  davecow@my-deja.com wrote:
>   I have a desire to call a non-COBOL (compiled REXX) program from a
> COBOL program on the OS/390 (IBM MVS/TSO) platform.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>   The REXX program I have has been compiled, but since the RECFM
(Record
> Format) of the COBOL PDS is 'U', I can't compile the REXX program to
it
> (the REXX compiler what's a RECFM of 'FB' [Fixed Block]).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   I would greatly appreciate any help I can get.  I don't think this
is
> a REXX specific problem, so if you have done this in another language
> (ie. 'C', JAVA, etc).  Please feel free to respond.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol Version 2.4.2 to Fujitsu Cobol conversion

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-22T04:16:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<V2By5.1817$ib.99167@nnrp1.ptd.net>`
- **References:** `<ufLv5.203$P7u.1114206@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca>`

```
Over the lasat 18 months, we have converted a large
legacy RM/Cobol application to Fujitsu PowerCobol.

"Convert" is really a poor word.  The event-oriented GUI
programming really doesn't end up being converted,
it ends up being re-evaluated, and in many cases,
an entirely new algorithm created.

For the first 6 months, the process and learning curve
were very slow.  Since then, creation speed is back
up to my old levels.
```

<a id="t-3683511a78"></a>
## Thread: IBM: CICS - Clearing R15 (revisited) — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM: CICS - Clearing R15 (revisited)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8poepd$7vv$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI (follow-up on an old thread),
  I just received the following from the IBM COBOL dox people,

"We have decided to include in the CICS section information about the need to
clear R15. You will see this update in the next release.

Many thanks for bringing this concern to our attention."

*OBVIOUSLY* this doesn't mean that if you don't clear this, that you will
ALWAYS have problems, but it does mean that "to be safe" that you should do
this.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM: CICS - Clearing R15 (revisited)

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pon9q$fvu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8poepd$7vv$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <8poepd$7vv$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> FYI (follow-up on an old thread),
>   I just received the following from the IBM COBOL dox people,
>
> "We have decided to include in the CICS section information about the
need to
> clear R15. You will see this update in the next release.
>
> Many thanks for bringing this concern to our attention."
>
> *OBVIOUSLY* this doesn't mean that if you don't clear this, that you
will
> ALWAYS have problems, but it does mean that "to be safe" that you
should do
> this.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

One other little item I'd like to post is the fact that COBOL's "RETURN-
CODE" is treated by IBM COBOL as a HALFWORD, S9(04) BINARY. So, if
anyone has ANY ideas of using R15 as a means to pass back a fullword-
value to the Caller, forget it, because only the low-order halfword is
addressable. Therefore, the greatest value for this purpose (and I'm
not advocating this by no means) is H'32767'.

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

## Thread: UNIX COBOL people (especially those with IBM experience) — 1 msgs

### UNIX COBOL people (especially those with IBM experience)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pp6cm$hcn$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I received the following that MIGHT be of interest to some in this NG.

"Due to popular demand, the deadline to submit technical
presentation abstracts for USERblue (http://www.userblue.org)
has been extended until Friday, September 15. Presentation
slots are filling up quickly, however, the USERblue program
committee is still looking for suggested presentations from
seasoned UNIX( professionals that utilize IBM technologies
to round out the agenda.

If you have knowledge and experience that users of IBM
technologies on UNIX systems would find valuable, please
submit an abstract to be considered for this inaugural event.
In addition to significant industry exposure for you and your
employer, session presenters will receive free registration on
the day(s) they present.

USERblue is a brand new education and networking forum
dedicated to those who use IBM products and services on
UNIX systems. This first-time event will take place
February 26-28, 2001, in Long Beach, California.  USERblue
is brought to you by SHARE in close cooperation with IBM.
Among the event highlights already confirmed is a keynote
presentation from Dr. Vinton Cerf of Worldcom, the individual
generally regarded as "The Father of the Internet."

Abstracts can address a wide array of subjects related to IBM
technologies on UNIX systems, including the following planned
technical tracks:
* Architecture
* e-Business
* High Availability
* Security
* Systems Administration
* Trends and Directions

Relevant technologies and IBM product lines include:
* AIX
* Dynix/PTX
* Linux
* NUMA-Q
* RS/6000

The Call for Presentations form is currently available at
http://www.userblue.org.  The extended submission deadline is Friday,
September 15, so be sure to act soon!

Watch the USERblue Web site (http://www.userblue.org) for the latest
event information.  For more information, contact SHARE Headquarters
at (888) 574-2735 or send an e-mail to hq@userblue.org

USERblue
A SHARE Forum for IBM Users of UNIX System Technologies
February 26-28, 2001
www.userblue.org
hq@userblue.org
(888) 574-2735"
```

<a id="t-fafba600a4"></a>
## Thread: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0 — 30 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T02:59:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
I found this helpful:
A Quick Start Guide to Using Fujitsu 4.0 Cobol

1.	Create project folder on floppy or hard drive
   a.	Open Windows Explorer
   b.	Select file->New->folder
   c.	Give folder name such as "Project1"
   d.	Close Windows Explorer
2.	Create/edit Cobol Source Code
   a.	Start->Programs-> Fujitsu Cobol 4.0->Cobol Project Manager
   b.	File->Project Open
   c.	Navigate to your project folder
   d.	Type in "Proj1" or other useful name
   e.	Click "Yes" to create the project file
   f.	Right click Proj1.prj
  g.	Select new file
  h.	Rename "New File.exe" to "PROJ1.EXE"
  i.	Right click on PROJ1.EXE
  j.	Select New Folder->Cobol Source file
  k.	Right click "Cobol Source File"
  l.	Select "New File"
  m.	Rename "New File.cob" to "Proj1.cob"
  n.	Right click PROJ1.cob
  o.	Select "Main Program" (Icon color turns red)
  p.	Double click PROJ1.COB tp begin entering source code
3.	Compile Your Code
  a.	Left click topmost folder to highlight your project
  b.	Click "Build" icon or select Project->Build from menu bar
  c.	If you get syntax errors, double-click on red message to launch editor
and fix error
  d.	Click "Build Start" icon after returning to message window or select
Build->start
4.	Run Your Program
   a.	Click "execute" icon or select Project->execute from menu bar
```

#### ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
Hi:

You must be a beginner because only a beginner with
no prior experience could possibly accept such an
utterly ridiculous sequence of events with equanimity.

This is wot I do. Windows 95 at the DOS PROMPT. No mouse use
except with SP2.

1. Make panel with Flexus SP2.
2. Copy skeleton program to NEWPROG.
3. Edit NEWPROG with BRIEF to manufacture program.
4. Compile and Link by typing 'C NEWPROG'.
5. Type NEWPROG at the DOS prompt to execute NEWPROG.
6. Repeat steps 3, 4 and 5 until happy. (Happiness occurs quickly.)

Using a Macro keyboard, each step is executed with a single keystroke.

(If you don't have a macro keyboard, DOSKEY is helpful in reducing
keystrokes. Why you don't have a macro keyboard is beyond me:))

If any living human can improve on this, I'll eat their source code
in Macy's window.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


> I found this helpful:
> A Quick Start Guide to Using Fujitsu 4.0 Cobol

editor

select

I would not pay too much attention to Dilbert here, as he is well known for
having to be dragged into any methodology that evolved after 1975, when he
started programming.  The approach he uses only works well with single file
source code modules that, by Cobol standards, are minuscule in size, badly
written, and rather mickey-mouse in nature. If you are a student and have at
least twenty years experience, you may be able to con someone into hiring
you using his approach, but it would be short term for sure. You'd be better
off learning to do it right.

Donald

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39c4cb3a.32063740@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com> <8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100> <8q054b$up3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The project file is one that basically lists the programs in a system.
It details which are main and which are called.  It details all the
libraries that are to be linked into the various EXE and DLL's (OR
INT's and GNT's if using a MERANT product).  With the project file, if
a change is made, a "build" of the project can be performed and only
those items that need to be compiled and linked are.  

On Sat, 16 Sep 2000 15:54:57 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>In article <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100>,
>  thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q7hun$60c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com> <8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100> <8q054b$up3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c4cb3a.32063740@207.126.101.100>`

```
In article <39c4cb3a.32063740@207.126.101.100>,
  thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> The project file is one that basically lists the programs in a system.
> It details which are main and which are called.  It details all the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> those items that need to be compiled and linked are.


Hi:

Thanks for the explanation.

This brings me to another of my favorite peeves, i.e., the
complete inadequacy of operating systems and compilers to
provide a decent directory.

Trying to manage hundreds of files of all sorts is not
made easier when all you get in the directory is the
name, size and date.

The invention of the 256-character filename as a means
to improve the situation is another example of Microsoft's
remarkable ability to do things the wrong way.

A decent file-management/directory system would provide
for several items of information about each file at the
time it was created. For example, the author, name of
file, file-extension, category/project to which it belongs,
date created, date last-modified, mode, and so on.

The directory should be listable in several different
sequences with the ability to exclude certain types
from the listing, and so on.

Anyway, I wrote a program to keep track of my stuff years ago
and I still use it. The eight-character plus extension
file name is perfectly adequate if you have a file-naming
convention - and stick to it like glue.

The naming convention I have used for aeons breaks
down each of the eight characters in the name like
this:

TPPNNSSS where;

        T is the Type of file, where:
             'P' is for Program,
             'F' is for Data Files,
             'R' is for a Record Description,
             'S' is for a Subroutine,
             'D' is for Documentation,
			  and so on.

        PP is the Category or Project the file belongs to.

        NN is a number identifying the file within the Project,

        SSS is a suffix to identify the version of the file,

The DOS extension identifies the nature of the file,
e.g., EXE, COB, CBL, CPY, etc.

Between my program for supplementing the directory and batch
files to compile complete systems or sub-systems, I guess
I have the equivalent of your project file.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T01:47:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-LiYTZ9bDXSFB@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com> <8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100> <8q054b$up3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c4cb3a.32063740@207.126.101.100> <8q7hun$60c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 19 Sep 2000 11:16:50, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

> In article <39c4cb3a.32063740@207.126.101.100>,
>   thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The OS/2 Warp operating system from IBM has a set of data
that are associated with a file called "extended attributes".
These can be used to carry additional information about
the file and can be retrieved by application programs
and OS utilities to display and/or sort files according
to the attributes. One major use in the WPS (that's the
OS/2 GUI) is to identify the program(s) that can act on
the file and what the default behavior for file open is.

You can idenify a file as "COBOL Source" and define
the default "open" to be in your COBOL editor program
(which can be defined as any executable). The file extension
has no bearing on how the file is treated (unlike Windows or DOS).

Each file can have up to a total 64 Kbytes of "extended attribute"
information. Each "extended attribute" has a name and a
value for that name. Creating extended attributes like
"Program Type" that has values of "subprogram" "main program"
etc can is verry easy and the values can be used to filter
for desktop folders or "batch" file operations. An attribute
called "Project Name" can be used to group files into
projects.

The "extended attributes" for a file are bound to a file
and are carried with it for backups, file copy operations etc.

The "extended attributes" coupled with the 256 character
path/filename available with OS/2 Warp makes file management
very easy.

This is just to let you all know that some operating
systems do provide the capabilities to provide a
decent directory and project control environment.
Mind you, the only one I know of is OS/2 Warp.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q2nq6$806$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com> <8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100> <MQSw5.149$Y24.82932@nnrp1.sbc.net> <39c4cc0d.32274481@207.126.101.100>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <39c4cc0d.32274481@207.126.101.100>...

>>> There are numerous advantages to setting up a project file.  While
>>> Fujitusu DOES support BATCH (command line) compiles, I don't do
>>them.

Thane, I do both, and it is as handy as heck.  Since Pstaff *creates* make
files, it is trivial to create a batch file that is simply a list of PSTAFF
project names ... IE:

erase \myobject_area\*.obj
NMAKE \myproject_area\PROJECT1.mak
NMAKE \myproject_area\PROJECT2.mak
etc.

Particularly with OOP, a largish system could contain several Pstaff
projects that must be compiled in a specific order so that the REP files are
created correctly.  The batch file provides a nice simple way of doing a
rebuild all from scratch (by answering "Y" to the erase all question), or to
check all possibilities of a needed for re-link (by answering "N" to the
delete all).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T00:15:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000916201551.29365.00000938@nso-ba.aol.com>`

```
In article <MQSw5.149$Y24.82932@nnrp1.sbc.net>, "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
writes:

>
>If you've got a way to compile a subroutine and add it to a .LIB file
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The only way that I know of how to get a .LIB or.OBJ is by not setting 
a MAIN option and comiling the program as a SUB-PROGRAM.
Using the Project manager to create the .DLL also creates the associated
.LIB, .OBJ, and .EXP files. 
I am not certain this is what you were looking for, but it is how I go about
creating a function library of routines that can be 're-used' in several
programs
without the need to 'include' the code in several places.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<68gx5.167$jO6.147237@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<MQSw5.149$Y24.82932@nnrp1.sbc.net> <20000916201551.29365.00000938@nso-ba.aol.com>`

```
As I said, we compile subroutines in a batch file (we have about 200
subroutines), the pertinent parts of which are (COMPILE MYPROG):

COBOL32 -iS:\A-SOURCE\SUBS\DEFAULT.CBI %1.COB
IF EXIST NOASK.TMP GOTO LIBADD

CHOICE /C:YN Add to library?
IF ERRORLEVEL 2 GOTO END

:LIBADD

\FSC\COBOL\LIB /REMOVE:%1.OBJ /VERBOSE SUBS.LIB
\FSC\COBOL\LIB /VERBOSE SUBS.LIB %1.OBJ
IF EXIST %1.OBJ DEL %1.OBJ
GOTO END

We'll probably try using Project Manager to handle the editting and
compiling and a stand-alone batch file to add the resulinting OBJ to
our LIB file (and delete the detritus).

(other

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

associated

go about

several

While Fujitusu DOES support BATCH (command line) compiles, I don't do

Hi Thane:

What do you do when it is necessary to recompile all programs
within a single system, for whatever reason?

I have DOS batch files to compile/link either ALL of my programs
or all programs within a system.

Although it happens infrequently, I have found big clumps
of junk in the middle of source programs - probably created
during one of Window's frequent death throes.

My software is pretty static, and, for peace of mind, I will
periodically recompile all of them just to be sure that nothing has
become damaged in some way.

I also recompile before making backups of source.

I wouldn't want to do any of this without batch files.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C7E1C5.1D584AD5@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com> <8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100> <8q7gch$4d7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c792ef.56731242@207.126.101.100> <39C796DA.D38AEA2C@brazee.net> <39c7de3a.76008486@207.126.101.100>`

```
I think this is the main reason OO CoBOL is nowhere close to being considered
in most enterprise shops.  If we keep programs isolated, we can test them
isolated.  We see how every night Microsoft compiles their next version of
NT, and half of its team tests all day, and the other half apply changes.
Well, enterprise environments can't do that - especially if they are have
large parts of their system based upon running batch programs quarterly or
upon demand.

Of course, OO programs and systems can be made isolated as well, but that
isn't what is preached.

So we have two directions where OO is growing:
We have the huge system which has to be integrated (Windows NT), because it
cannot be created any other way.
And we have the small systems which can be tested as integrated environments
- without effecting working systems.

Most enterprises do not have their business logic in either environment.

the best solution to integration (dare i say it) is to use

Hi,

Since this message shifted into security with the problems it brings, one
such method of solving those problems follows.  I didn't think of it, Adm.
Rickhover (SP) did.  In his lab in a sub of Pittsburgh he demanded these
features.
ALL software built in-house
No communication lines outside of the fences.
Lead lined computer room.
No working at home.
Latest and best people in/out control.
On site "police".

I knew one of the police, and everything went well until the guy who did
the software decided to leave.

Warren Simmons
warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cbass2112@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qajoh$phu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com> <8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100> <8q7gch$4d7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8q7gch$4d7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
> In article <39c38863.3986967@207.126.101.100>,
>   thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> within a single system, for whatever reason?

Generally speaking, the whole point of a project file is to *avoid* such
global recompiles, providing a quicker test/debug turnaround. I cannot
think of any circumstance where a global recompile would be necessary
in a properly maintained project, but all development environments I
have seen do provide this ability.

> I have DOS batch files to compile/link either ALL of my programs
> or all programs within a system.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> during one of Window's frequent death throes.

I think I would move my source *off* of my development machine if this
were a common occurrence. Burn it onto CD-R/W perhaps.


Curtis


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cbass2112@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q7uqv$ljv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com> <8pqi7a$iot$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8q6bg4$rb7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8q7faa$39g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8q7faa$39g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> you edit, compile and test?

Are you suggesting that editing, testing and debugging a 15,000-line
source module is no more difficult than a 1,500-line module? Or, perhaps
a 15,000-line module should never exist; would editing/testing *ten*
1,500-line source modules be no more difficult than editing/testing
*one?*  Remember that the ten source modules must compile into one
integrated suite of executables, or perhaps into one executable.  What
goes on in one module may have diverse effects in the other nine.

Would keeping track of the interactions between ten 1,500-line modules
be just as easy as keeping track of one such module?

> What it runs on shouldn't affect that either, no?

Your development machine is, presumably, one machine. How does "Repeat
steps 3, 4 and 5 until happy. (Happiness occurs quickly.)" work when the
target is a two- or three-tier c/s environment?

> Aren't A/P, A/R, etc. separate systems anyway?

Seperate *modules* perhaps, but the overall *system* which includes
these modules will require smooth interaction between the various
modules, and should also behave well if some modules are missing or not
installed.


Curtis


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Only for COBOL newbies w/ fugitsu cobol 4.0

- **From:** "mike sheehan" <sheehan_mj@iolfree.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RRFw5.2738$44.8595@news.iol.ie>`
- **References:** `<20000913225913.01697.00000012@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
To Deek> it is useful to have "standard" help notes available for new
users...

A previous post of mine to this ngroup shows a similiar kind of info
requested!

Mike

This works for Fujitsu COBOL 4.0, but, it should work the same...

You can select compile in the editor...

To create an exe:
1. Launch the COBOL Project Manager
2. Create a new folder for the project and source
3. Open a new project by giving it a name and selecting the folder
4. In Project Manager select/highlight the project
5. Select menu Edit... new file
6. An empty executable "New File..EXE" is created - you can change the name
7. Select it and select menu Edit... new folder... COBOL source File
8. Select the new Cobol folder - Select menu Edit... New File
9. You can rename the source file
10. Double click the COB file to edit it: type in your program
11. Once a program is typed in - Select tools ... compile in the editors
menu
12. When you are happy with the program - select the project from the
project menu
13. Select project menu ... build. The application should build ok
14. To run the executable ... double click on the executable file

note: an alternative to going to the menu is once you have highlighted a
file - you can right click and select options available to you......

Hope this is of help.
Mike

## Thread: Netexpress cgi and http header — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Netexpress cgi and http header

- **From:** "Carlos Ruivo Ferreira" <mb2s.geral@esoterica.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pqh0v$cqe$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
I have a cgi program running in a web server with no problems. Now when I
try to publish my web site with the same cgi program in another site it
doesn't work and gives the following message:
"The specified CGI application misbehaved by not returning a complete set of
HTTP headers. The headers it did return are:"
the guy from my isp says that everything is ok, permissions and so on, but
the fact is the cgi program works fine in another server

Can someone help me with this?

Thanks in advance
Carlos Ferreira
carlos@mb2s.com
Porto
Portugal
```

#### ↳ Re: Netexpress cgi and http header

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pt03l$f3g$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8pqh0v$cqe$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```

Carlos Ruivo Ferreira wrote in message <8pqh0v$cqe$1@venus.telepac.pt>...
>I have a cgi program running in a web server with no problems. Now when I
>try to publish my web site with the same cgi program in another site it
>doesn't work and gives the following message:
>"The specified CGI application misbehaved by not returning a complete set
of
>HTTP headers. The headers it did return are:"
>the guy from my isp says that everything is ok, permissions and so on, but
>the fact is the cgi program works fine in another server
>
>Can someone help me with this?

I imagine the reason for this is some text from the application is being
output back to the web server before the proper headers which should be
produced by a CGI application.

If you are using the MERANT Microfocus extentions for CGI check you are not
displaying any html code or other text before the first DISPLAY
external-form in your application  or the first EXEC HTML statement which
produces output. As it is when the first of these which when encountered
will automatically produce the appropiate headers.

A method I sometimes use to debug this kind of thing is to place a

  display "Content-type: text/plain" & x"0a" & x"0a"
or
  display "Content-type: text/html" & x"0a" & x"0a"

as the first line in my program. That way I can see everything which is
being passed to the web server and spot any problem areas. This will work if
you are doing all the CGI stuff yourself or using the features provided by
the product.

So if you use the text/plain version and you see something like

Some Text Here from my application
Content-type: text/html

<html>
<head>
...

Then it is the 'Some Text Here from my application' which is upseting the
web server and the other one ignoring it.

If you do the above and don't understand what the problem is, perhaps you
could send me a copy and I'll see if I can help.

Regards,
Steve.

>
>Thanks in advance
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

>hello,
>
>First of all thank's for your time. I have tried to place the display
>"content......etc, and the message appears but with nothing in the top,
only
>the message. The strange thing is that if i try to run with solo works
fine.

>isp) it works. If i do the upload of the very same program at netway
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>ever? In my personal web it works fine too. In my personal web
configuration
>(win 2000 prof) i had to give the permissions of execution and i saw that
>there is a folder, at the cgi properties window of the Internet Information
>Service, about http headers.Is it possible that there is something with
this
>configuration (at my isp) that provoke the error?

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Portugal

Hi Carlos,

Thanks. Works with SOLO, goes now hill with another web server. Well that
helped me remember another possible senario which can cause the problems
you have. That is if the CGI executable is producing a run time error.

One common reason for this is because it can't find part of the COBOL
system.
On your local machine the executable (if linked to use the dynamically bound
run time) can find the  runtime system via the registery system in the
Windows
operating system. On another system the runtime system won't be avaliable.

Check to see how your application is linked together. It wants to use
the static runtime linking (in the IDE check out build settings and the
link tab). Or use cbllink -BSF ... Try this first. If that does not work..

Another debug method, because I'm sure the reason for the http error
is because something invalid is being passed back to the server from
the application, is to catch this using a stub program.

mybat.cmd:

@echo off
@echo Content-type: text/plain
@echo.
@echo Test batch file.
@e:\...\myapplication.exe

So you form tag will need to say
<form ... action=".....\mybat.cmd" ... METHOD="GET">

Using the Microsoft Management Console to look at the properties of
my scripts directory (right click on scripts, select properties, then
clicking on configuration), under the 'application mappings' I see I
have the line

.cmd    c:\winnt\system32\cmd.exe /a /c %s

Now I can't remember if I added this myself or is there automatically
but one needs this present if you want to execute .cmd files in the
<form> tag from your browser. Best to select in the permissions of
.exe and scripts for now.

Hopefully one now can see the text which is causing the webserver
problems.

Note using this stub method the CGI info is not passed to the application
so your application may not output correct results. I only use it to
see if the application is producing unexpected run time errors.

A slight aside, but you may need this depending on the complexity of
your application.

Another alternative to static linking, which I don't know too much about
is the 'application  server' which I think has all the run time files
needed for  distributing your  application. I've heard in theory when
installed it updates the registory so the cobol runtime system can be
found again.

A third alternative is when installing the application server is
updating the system environment variable PATH to point to the
run time system as PATH is also searched when looking for .dll files.

I hope the documentation will explain the application server side more.

Let me know how you get on.

Regards,
Steve.

>
```

---

>I have a quick questions, as i have posted before i am new to COBOL, for class
>we get projects well i got through with Proj1 with no problems but i am
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Deek

What they are asking you to do is define a variable in working storage
and every time you select a record to process, add 1 to your variable.
So, it might look like:

03  WS-CNTR         PIC S9(5)           VALUE +0.

Then somewhere in your program you might have:

READ INPUT-FILE  AT END  MOVE 'Y' TO INPUT-EOF.
IF INPUT-EOF NOT EQUAL 'Y'
   ADD 1 TO WS-CNTR.

This is a very simplistic example, but I hope it gets you thinking in
the right way.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Never raise your hands to your kids. 
It leaves your groin unprotected.
 

Boycott Mitsubishi Industries
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol FAQ Quesiton

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ATbw5.890$aY1.44140@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<mjn0sssb9o7rnfous1gef8cf4tiggpljst@4ax.com> <20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
It gets every COBOL programmer at least once a week.

There are some who swear by the (funny-looking) construct of putting
periods on lines by themselves and only having one period per
paragraph. In my view, this syntax looks weird, but it solves (mostly)
the problem of spurious (or missing) periods.

There are, however, one or two things you can get in the habit of
doing to minimize the problem.

One is to use END-(something) at every possible place. For example,

IF condition
   do something
   END-IF

If you plan on using a period instead of END-IF - and you forget the
period - you're hosed. WITH the END-IF, period or no, you're golden.

Don't EVER use commas or semi-colons as syntax in a COBOL program.

"Deek40" <deek40@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com...
> Thanks, as i stated before i am learning Cobol and still make dumb
mistake oh
> well live and learn.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol FAQ Quesiton

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g0xk5q1.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<mjn0sssb9o7rnfous1gef8cf4tiggpljst@4ax.com> <20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com> <ATbw5.890$aY1.44140@nnrp2.sbc.net>`

```
On Thu, 14 Sep 2000 16:55:48 -0500, Jerry P wrote:

>It gets every COBOL programmer at least once a week.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have come to the conclusion that are two ways to reduce the possibility to
make mistakes.

1. Only use periods when the syntax or the logic of your program requires it.
2. Use periods whenever you can.

I prefer the second, because it's more likely that the compiler catches your
errors.

Anyway, using the END-IF construction is much easier to read, so if your
compiler
supports it, use it (together with END-READ END-PERFORM etc.)

Just my opinion

Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol FAQ Quesiton

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Christopher J Pomasl" <pomasl@uswest.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R0uw5.439$eS1.206945@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<mjn0sssb9o7rnfous1gef8cf4tiggpljst@4ax.com> <20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com> <ATbw5.890$aY1.44140@nnrp2.sbc.net> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g0xk5q1.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
I made a standard to ALWAYS use the END-xx constructs when the code is more
than one line long.  I have eliminated many stray periods this way and the
compiler ALWAYS tells me when I have one.  When you are working with a IF
statement that is several lines long and may even contain some embedded IF
statements, it is easy to place a incorrect period in fray.  By coding the
END-xx where you EXPECT the end to be, the compiler will flag the statement
as not having a corresponding "xx" statement if you put in a stray period.

Hopefully your compiler supports it as it is more valuable than debugging
the results of a stray period.  Use them!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol FAQ Quesiton

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C31B4D.813FFBB1@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<mjn0sssb9o7rnfous1gef8cf4tiggpljst@4ax.com> <20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com> <ATbw5.890$aY1.44140@nnrp2.sbc.net> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g0xk5q1.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <R0uw5.439$eS1.206945@news.uswest.net>`

```
Christopher J Pomasl wrote:
> 
> I made a standard to ALWAYS use the END-xx constructs when the code is more
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the results of a stray period.  Use them!!

I have made it a standard for my happy little band of merry programmers,
telling them to code END-IF, for example, as routinely as they code
END-EXEC. No one seems to feel that END-EXEC is a burden or diminishes
them as a person.

As always, YMMV,
LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol FAQ Quesiton

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C21D02.CB80C26B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<mjn0sssb9o7rnfous1gef8cf4tiggpljst@4ax.com> <20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com> <ATbw5.890$aY1.44140@nnrp2.sbc.net> <G9ew5.1292$Xp5.267642@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <8ps63k$337i$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Terry Heinze wrote:

> What is the advantage of ending every paragraph with "CONTINUE." instead of
> simply a "." on it's own line?  I can see that the disadvantage is coding an
> unnecessary 8 characters per paragraph.

CONTINUE.

is much more obvious and hard to miss than is

.


Also, my shop won't accept period by itself as part of its standards.  Besides
being easy to miss, and non-English language, it is ugly.  We really aren't
worried about the cost of writing storing those extra 8 characters.  If we had
wanted a terse, hard to read language, we wouldn't have picked CoBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol FAQ Quesiton

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T06:06:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000916020644.02532.00000404@ng-fn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
Another Question:
Not to spite anyone here, but could someone explain what COBOL is used for in
every day life?  What little I have saw of COBOL it looks like a database that
takes a lot of extra time to plan.  What type of things can u do with COBOL
that is unique?  Remember I am not expierenced with COBOL or anything else, but
HTML.
Thanks from the newbie,
Deek
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol FAQ Quesiton

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39c389cc.4348468@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<20000914015503.26266.00000423@ng-fu1.aol.com> <20000916020644.02532.00000404@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
Not sure it it's "unique" or not but here is a short list of COBOL
program functions I have written that are a "little off the beaten
path".

Clipboard monitor - anything that lands in the clipboard gets appended
to a file.

Socket Interface
Serial communications fully automated file transfer of statement data
to a statement processor

Pedigree Tracking (Cats)

Fax Server

Then some more mundane:

Bowling center managment
Archery shoot Software
Invoice printing
Tax Lookup (Web Interface)
POS
Data Entry

That's a short list.  It can do virtually anything you want with a
programmng language.

Deek40 wrote:
> 
> Another Question:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Deek

Well, let me see.  IRS has millions of lines of it for processing
tax returns/refunds.

USGov't has millions of lines of it for doing payroll, inventory,
weapons tracking, certain things for NASA use COBOL, USNavy
personnel helped develop the language...

"Private Sector" Payroll, cash management, check processing (aka,
Item Processing for banks), inventory, order entry, Circulation
management (news paper companies and magazines), anything you
want done with/via/for accounting, credit card processing
(merchant, bank, card-holder, embossing), a few games have been
written in COBOL.  Uh, what more would you like?
```

<a id="t-d1caab1ad5"></a>
## Thread: Error Questions — 4 msgs

### Error Questions

- **From:** "GT" <gtroussel@nsbs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fXhw5.775$0j.470278@news.uswest.net>`

```
If I had a program on a network that ran on top of Cobol 6, what would I
look for when I'm getting error like "error code: 104", and "error code:
93,02." ?

TIA

GT
```

#### ↳ Re: Error Questions

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oNbBOSN8XrkIPyS5pKVWPbKEC4bn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fXhw5.775$0j.470278@news.uswest.net>`

```
First of all you should start reading the users manual in section
RUNTIME MESSAGES.

Anyway error 104 is either a subscript error or a program that
tried to reference a linkage item that was not include in the
"USING" part of the call issued by the calling program, 
(and this includes the parameters in the command line).

Error 93,02 is file locked by another user (process).


FF

On Thu, 14 Sep 2000 22:48:18 -0700, "GT" <gtroussel@nsbs.com> wrote:

>If I had a program on a network that ran on top of Cobol 6, what would I
>look for when I'm getting error like "error code: 104", and "error code:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Error Questions

- **From:** "GT" <gtroussel@nsbs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g7rw5.70$Mn3.79061@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<fXhw5.775$0j.470278@news.uswest.net> <oNbBOSN8XrkIPyS5pKVWPbKEC4bn@4ax.com>`

```
I appreciate your quick response to my question, thank you.  When you
explained the "104" error, I feel that I am still a little in the dark on
your answer, would you follow up with example possibly?  Thanks again!!

GT

news:oNbBOSN8XrkIPyS5pKVWPbKEC4bn@4ax.com...

> RUNTIME MESSAGES.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error Questions

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PHbCOU08awjd93aDHhNEwlaxrDgO@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fXhw5.775$0j.470278@news.uswest.net> <oNbBOSN8XrkIPyS5pKVWPbKEC4bn@4ax.com> <g7rw5.70$Mn3.79061@news.uswest.net>`

```
On Fri, 15 Sep 2000 09:15:36 -0700, "GT" <gtroussel@nsbs.com> wrote:

>I appreciate your quick response to my question, thank you.  When you
>explained the "104" error, I feel that I am still a little in the dark on
>your answer, would you follow up with example possibly?  Thanks again!!
You are doing things the wrong way.

As per the following statement (yourï¿½s) you already had the error.

>> >If I had a program on a network that ran on top of Cobol 6, what would I
>> >look for when I'm getting error like "error code: 104", and "error code:
>> >93,02." ?

So you only have to tell US when and what you were doing at the time
it happened.

What I have told you should be enough for you to figure it out.
But just as another clue, and this for the 93,02 it can happen if you
have two instances of the same program running at the same time in the
same machine.

As for the 104.
Either you are starting the program with the wrong number (or type) of
parameters, or there is an "coding" error in one of the programs.
In either case you should speak with whoever coded the program.

FF
```
