- **Message-ID:** `<39D1F5D0.7E168566@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CF6EB7.FEF5A46@brazee.net> <8qo2eb$72$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39CF9D88.4A1D81A7@brazee.net> <8qr6c6$hrh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <htfA5.7813$583.306022@news1.onlynews.com>`

```
keith smyth wrote:

> In the past there have been, and there continues today, to be employers who
> expose their workers to physical hazards, hazardous chemicals, and a
> standard of living only slightly above poverty.

...

> My feeling is we need something more in our profession.  Not at every
> company.  Most companies are run fine.

The best thing we can have is a value which we can easily take away.  As long as
it costs more to hire and train a new worker than to improve conditions to keep
existing workers, companies will be under pressure to improve conditions.  That
is unless it gets cheaper to farm our our work overseas, or if the value of our
work is not greater than our cost, in which case working conditions won't be our
problem.
```

Unfortunately most of these arrogant anti-Union types are young and

Corporations love ignorant populations for exploitation.

have

where

did y2k

A mere

Right on, Keith!!!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Charles Goodyear <cgoodyear@gdyr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D2AEB6.8D9D0C06@gdyr.com>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CF6EB7.FEF5A46@brazee.net> <8qo2eb$72$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39CF9D88.4A1D81A7@brazee.net> <8qr6c6$hrh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <htfA5.7813$583.306022@news1.onlynews.com> <8qtgcm$dei$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


philotsopher wrote:

> In article <htfA5.7813$583.306022@news1.onlynews.com>,
>   "keith smyth" <k17731NO@SPAMMhome.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> combat that.

   ROTFLMAO. You simply want to overlay one level of power-hungry people
with yet another layer of power-hungry weasels. The problem is not a lack of
power-hungry weasels. I've seen zero evidence that having a union does
anyone any good these days. (Look at United. They are OWNED by their
employees and they still are having labor problems.)

> > An imbalance either way is not good for the countries economic
> health, or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> class.

   No, increased productivity through the application of modern technology
and better production methods is the reason why the US has such a large
middle class. Labor unions have traditionally acted to reduce productivity
and so are irrelevant to the economic well-being of our middle class.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> They conveniently leave out what would happen without the unions.

   Given how small a percentage of the workers in America are now unionized
(17% or something like that), it seems pretty obvious that what would happen
without the unions IS what is happening right now. Unprecedented prosperity
and stability.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Unions would disappear.

   That's a stupid argument. Do you mean to say that all the gains you think
laborers have made that you attribute to past union activity would somehow
just disappear if the proportion of union workers fell from 17% to 0%? What
precisely do you think that 17% are doing that is so important to the
welfare of the other 83% and the maintenance of those gains you attribute to
them?

> They only know that the last 7 years have been
> good to them, and they figure the US has always been like that.

   Hell, no. I know it was much worse when unions were more powerful.

>
> Corporations love ignorant populations for exploitation.
>
   So do union propagandists like you.

> Isn't it weird how some of these anti union types see no threat from
> the millions of dollars spent by pro employer lobby groups?

   I hope that the firm I work for gets all sorts of govt breaks and
advantages at your expense, as it will increase the value of my stock
options. Aside from that, on a more serious note, it is important to realize
that most of that money is spent opposing itself, as one group of companies
lobby for what benefits them while their competitors lobby for what benefits
those competitors, thus cancelling each other out. This operates on a far
larger level than you might think too, as what benefits workers who make the
products of one group of firms thereby also benefits the consumers of the
products of another group of firms, and what reduces costs for one group of
firms thereby reduces the prices paid by the consumers of the products
produced by those firms. The idea that only the very very rich benefit from
the activities of firms is just plain stupid. When it comes to money spent
by the entire corporate world as a whole, aside from this internecine
conflict, even that comes around to the consumer. In a competitive
environment, costs get passed on to the consumer and whatever lowers those
costs thereby also lowers the costs that get passed on to the consumer.
   Labor unions stress the alleged benefit that they are seeking for people
in their tole as workers but those same actions work AGAINST those same
people in their role as consumers. Higher wages ARE higher costs and if
productivity has not changed then workers as a whole CAN NOT be any better
off simply because they get higher wages. The higher wages that the unions
got for them do no additional good for the workers because costs went up the
same amount. This game of musical chairs might impress the less intelligent
among our species but nobody who is intelligent is going to think that
merely raising all workers' wages will do ANY good whatsoever. Therefore, a
labor union can only succeed in improving the financial welfare of their
members if they manage to get larger wage increases than other workers in
the economy get. It is a beggar-thy-neighbor policy. In other words, it is
human greed at its worst.
   The only things that will improve worker welfare overall for the whole
economy is whatever will increase productivity or directly increase human
happiness. Unions may improve human happiness without offsetting costs if
they get employers to engage in activities that don't increase costs so much
that they offset the increase in human happiness that results (I think that
getting all firms to offer 4 weeks of vacation a year even to new employees
would be one such measure). This seems to rarely happen in the real world
though, as unions bargain for higher salaries more than anything else, or
they'll bargain for things that actually reduce overall productivity, like a
prohibition of the use of non-union labor.
   Truly stupid people often think that any concessions labor unions win are
a total gain for workers, never stopping to wonder exactly who PAYS for
those increased benefits. The stupidest ones of all think it will come out
of the executives' salaries. These are the ones that are convinced to
agitage for unions by quotations of huge executive salaries, as if the
executives will just be so impressed by union activities that they will open
up their wallets and share the contents instead of merely passing the costs
on to the consumers (who are, surprise, the middle class).

> They seem
> to think that this money is being spent to improve their working
> conditions. What a bunch of sheep!

   That perfectly describes unionheads who think their dues are going toward
improving their own welfare when actually they are just subsidizing
cost-push inflation and liberal pinhead politicians (though conservative
pinhead politicians would probably be no improvement).

> > But at those companies where people are being treated poorly anyway,
> where
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > situations.

   Those places often can't get unionized because they are usually bankrupt
in time, and bankruptcy is a far worse fate than unionization. Unionization
mostly victimizes consumers, unless the union gets really really stupid
(which seems quite frequent). It is such a rare thing for the very wealthy
to be hurt by union activity that it can be considered statistically
insignificant.
```

philotsopher wrote:
> 
> Unfortunately most of these arrogant anti-Union types are young and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Corporations love ignorant populations for exploitation.

Will it be that hard to get unions when they are needed?  If those young
people who are ignorant of history (I am older and a history buff), are
selfish now and decide that they are better off without unions now -
won't they be still be selfish when unions have something to offer
them?  Won't they take the best offer - which may be unions if you are
correct?

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "keith smyth" <k17731NO@SPAMMhome.com>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Karl Menger <kmenger@MtPellerin.au>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:43:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com>`

```


keith smyth wrote:

> Read the presentation at the other end of this link (I didn't write it, but
> sorry it is so lengthy.  It is worth finishing.  You might want to print it
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://heather.cs.ucdavis.edu/itaa.real.html#tth_sEc5

   That link just goes to the same old tired and invalid Norman Matloff paper
that all opponents of H-1B keep trotting out. Nothing new here. Nothing valid
either. That paper is a good example of how NOT to do an economic analysis.

> If people are loosing their jobs because of age anyway, what do they have to
> loose by trying to unionize their workplace?

   Unions don't work when capital is mobile intellectual capital. The whole idea
of a union is to withhold (or more usually threaten to withhold) labor until the
owners of capital give the workers more stuff. The model is totally inapplicable
when the capital is intellectual capital that is contained within the workers
instead of being owned by the business owners.

> Even young people would support unionization, once they are hired on,
> because it makes no sense to study for 4 years to get a career that will
> last only 8 years.

   Another paranoid delusionist. There are many programmers that have been in
the field longer than 8 years. In addition, there are a number of over-40
programmers out there.

> Even without unionization, as a group we are so disinterested in national
> and international affairs.  We need to employ the tactics of  the NOW, the
> Sierra Club, the NRA, and our employers.

  You mean we need to emulate a bunch of nuts?

> We need to recognize that industry groups are employer groups, and they are
> representing interests contrary to our own.

  My employer's interest IS my own. If my employer fell on economic hard times
because a bunch of union assholes increased costs it would reduce the value of
my stock options. Go peddle your old industrial ideas to someone else, like your
dog.
```

Well, on the other hand, let me point out that unions formed in the 1800's
in
response to business practices that would make Malaysian sweat shops
embarrassed.

Let me also point out that while our job requires both technical skill,
ability, and pure raw talent to do, "management" would like
nothing better than to be able to reduce 'Programmers' to
$5.45/hour type positions.

Check out the latest round of silver bullet disease for
confirmation - once again they are on the kick that
you can buy a product (like say, VA/Generator) and
have cheap programmers use it effectively without
having to teach them how to program. (*sigh*)

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <nfJz5.2350$103.15333@news1.atl> <39cf7ebd_2@news5.newsfeeds.com>`

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praul@cegi.no-spam-net> wrote:
>
> Well, on the other hand, let me point out that unions formed in the
> 1800's in response to business practices that would make Malaysian
> sweat shops embarrassed.

True enough.  However, patients used to be blead to 'cure' disease.
Just because there was a reason for doing it, doesn't mean the
'solution' chosen was a good or apt one. :-)

Have you considered what would have happened if those millions of
workers had politically pressed for something like OSHA with their
voting ballots, instead of using coertion, through unions?  For one
thing, there would have been much less needless bloodshed.  Just
as the U.S. Civil War was stupid, because slavery could have been
abolished in 10 or 20 years with little or no bloodshed, by wise
use of graduated taxation of slaves and financial incentives to
free them (in 1860, the Virginia legislature came within one vote
of abolishing slavery in Virginia), unions were a percipitate, ill-
conceived 'solution' that did vast damage and left a continuing
trail of waste and corruption.  Unions are unhealthy by nature.  I
don't mean the right of free people to form unions, that is a given.
I mean the use they are created for.  A free person should not have
the right to coerce another free person to their will.  Nor should
a group of people have such right.  What is the difference in a
union saying "we will strike and harrass and ruin your company if
you don't give us such-and-such" and an extortioner saying "I will
bomb and ruin your company if you don't do such-and-such?"  The
answer is nothing, they are the same.  "I will hurt you if you
don't bow to my demands."  That whole concept is wrong.  We should
give such power only to the government, where it can be checked by
law, and by the will of the citizens through due process.  I have
no problem with employees forming a union and going to management
to say "We are unhappy, and unless you comply with such-and-such
conditions, we will quit."  Fine, that's simple negotiation.  But
if they do quit, they have no business picketing or harrassing the
company.  Those are nothing more than legalized extortion.

> Let me also point out that while our job requires both technical
> skill, ability, and pure raw talent to do, "management" would like
> nothing better than to be able to reduce 'Programmers' to
> $5.45/hour type positions.

True, but that's what free market is all about.  MacDonald's
would no doubt love to sell their hamburgers for ten times the
current price, but they won't get away with it as long as Burger
King, et al are around. :-)

> Check out the latest round of silver bullet disease for
> confirmation - once again they are on the kick that
> you can buy a product (like say, VA/Generator) and
> have cheap programmers use it effectively without
> having to teach them how to program. (*sigh*)

Just because they want to do that doesn't mean they can make it work,
does it?  But if they can, then they have a point, don't they? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CF94BC.4E068B6F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <nfJz5.2350$103.15333@news1.atl> <39cf7ebd_2@news5.newsfeeds.com> <sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>  What is the difference in a
> union saying "we will strike and harrass and ruin your company if
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> law, and by the will of the citizens through due process.

We don't "give" that power to anyone.  The government takes it because it
can.  Unions take it because they (sometimes) can.  "Due process" does
not change this, and a company has a much easier task fighting the union
than it (or I ) does (do) fighting the government.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D0A27E.7B438BC4@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <nfJz5.2350$103.15333@news1.atl> <39cf7ebd_2@news5.newsfeeds.com> <sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl> <39CF94BC.4E068B6F@brazee.net> <39D01032.851CBE96@net.gr>`

```


Petros Economidis wrote:

> It still isn't a legitimate form of power.

What makes some power legitimate?  If someone persuades you to go along with
it?  (Hitler persuaded lots of people to go along with his power grab).
Certainly if you require 100% agreement, then there is no such thing as a
legitimate form of power.

Or is it legitimate if sanctioned by God or Allah?  Do you need 51% approval to
be legitimate?  (Certainly the majority can oppress).
Or is it simply a form of power which Petros Economidis doesn't mind?



Power is power.  And power gained is corrupting.  The best thing for the rest
of us is if the power is spread around so that the biggest powers have to watch
competing powers and respond.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CFA456.E21A81D5@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <nfJz5.2350$103.15333@news1.atl> <39cf7ebd_2@news5.newsfeeds.com> <sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl> <39CF94BC.4E068B6F@brazee.net> <CYMz5.2768$103.18158@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> > We don't "give" that power to anyone.  The government takes it because it
> > can.  Unions take it because they (sometimes) can.  "Due process" does
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> permit it, even if by our inaction and inattention.

That doesn't happen, but politicians do make unpopular decisions.  I watched
PARADE the other day, where a 1915? governor  of Georgia commuted the sentence
of someone who was accused of rape.  This guy was a northern Jew and the people
wanted to hang him.  And they did lynch him and the governor did flee days
before the end of his term (in real life).

Still, if they could, they would make unpopular decisions quietly.

If you gain power by gun point, by voodoo, by compromise, or whether you gain
power by being as charismatic as Hitler, you still have power.  And if I agree
with what those in power do, I am lucky.  If not, too bad.

One reason unions are not popular with computer professionals, is that computer
professionals have enough power to make their own choices.  We generally don't
believe that giving up power to the unions will be more useful than keeping
it.  We believe that we should be able to negotiate with prospective employers
instead of accepting what others decided (there are exceptions - I am currently
working for the state!!).  We believe that changing jobs will pay off better
than gaining seniority.  If there was only one job in town, with low entrance
requirements - our outlook would be different.  The power in town (the
corporation) would likely need a counter power (a union).  I like having the
power spread out without anybody having absolute control.
```

"Mike Grello" <grello@mgrello.columbasc.ncr.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >How many politicians do you know who would vote a particular way,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> provided by a PAC just to save some measily $160,000 year job?

As long as they believe (rightly) that the voters are indifferent,
most of them.  My point is, what would they do if they *knew* the
voters *would* notice and take action at the polls?  Mind you, I'm
not suggesting that this situation is likely. :-)  But if we voters
really did our jobs, then we would have much better government.  In
a sense, people tend to get the kind of government they deserve.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D0CFA8.8B913486@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <nfJz5.2350$103.15333@news1.atl> <39cf7ebd_2@news5.newsfeeds.com> <sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl> <39CF94BC.4E068B6F@brazee.net> <CYMz5.2768$103.18158@news1.atl> <969925708.2033920414@ncrnews.daytonoh.ncr.com> <kl3A5.4491$bD5.29756@news3.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> As long as they believe (rightly) that the voters are indifferent,
> most of them.  My point is, what would they do if they *knew* the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> a sense, people tend to get the kind of government they deserve.

Trouble is, as is obvious from discussion groups such as this - if the
voters DID know, and if the voters WERE willing to take action - we wouldn't
all take the same action.  Some people would support their actions, some
would condemn them.  Some people find other issues to be important enough to
bump this issue to lesser importance.  Some people believe their opponents
would be worse, some people believe their opponents to be better.

We all tend to be elitist enough to start believing that if the people only
KNEW, or if they only CARED, they would see things our way.
```

    One definition of a government is a group or groups that
believe that they have the
right to take away someone's freedom or life.

    A companies ability to fight a union can vary for several
reasons, one
of which is the economy.  If there is a large group of people
that can
do the job available for hire, than the company can fight a
union.  Of course,
the question remains, can they REALLY do the job.  Witness the
Firestone
tires said to have been made during a union strike.


Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:39CF94BC.4E068B6F@brazee.net...
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> >  What is the difference in a
> > union saying "we will strike and harrass and ruin your
company if

will

The

you
> > don't bow to my demands."  That whole concept is wrong.  We
should

the union
> than it (or I ) does (do) fighting the government.

From another forum, we have the effects of employees quitting en
masse. As anybody ever heard of quality and its cost?

JB

"Gov. Bush values the work of state employees and his staff is
concerned about our turnover rate," Jones (Mike Jones, a spokesman for
Gov. George W. Bush) said. 

"If the governor is in office next year, he will look forward to
working with legislative leaders on ideas to retain our best public
servants."

Billy Hamilton, state deputy comptroller, said turnover is causing a
"hollowing-out of the work force." The gap between the newest
employees and the senior workers means those with six to 15 years of
experience are absent.

"I think there's going to be some hard times ahead," Hamilton said.

Although the transportation department only saw a 9.8 percent turnover
rate in 1999, Jarratt said 25 of 30 people who were in his group in
1993 are gone.

He said new employees are asking for changes that they believe save
money but actually leave bumps that his company must pave. 

Then cars and trucks bounce over the road, hammering it to pieces.

"The product they are going to be putting out is not going to be the
same quality Texas DOT has put out over the last 83 years," he said.

Much of the surplus was returned to taxpayers in the form of a tax
cut, rather than being plowed back into maintaining services,
Armbrister said.

"Joe Sixpack wants the money back," he said.

Gary W. Anderson, executive director of the Texas Public Employees
Association, said paying to retain veteran workers is a matter of
priorities.

"What the people of Texas have come to expect will be gone," he said. 

"Once the infrastructure is gone, it may be very expensive to rebuild
it."





From The Fort Worth Star-Telegram, 9/25/00:    
http://www.star-telegram.com/news/doc/1047/1:STATE12/1:STATE120924100.html

 
State employees leaving in droves, cost Texas $250 million 

By CHRIS ROBERTS
Associated Press 

EL PASO, Texas -- 

State employees repair roads, guard prison inmates, administer welfare
and maintain computer systems. 

They oversee agricultural programs, fight wildfires and patrol the
state's highways.

And in 1999, 17.6 percent of them -- 25,421 people -- left the system.

That's slightly higher than the 1998 rate of 17.4 percent and above
the national average of 14.9 percent.

The result is that lost productivity and constant training are costing
the state between $127 million and $254 million each year, according
to a March report from the state auditor's office.

<grello@mgrello.columbasc.ncr.com> wrote:

    There is a disease, fairly common in USA, that bleeding will
treat.


>
> Have you considered what would have happened if those millions
of
> workers had politically pressed for something like OSHA with
their
> voting ballots, instead of using coertion, through unions?  For
one
> thing, there would have been much less needless bloodshed.
Just
> as the U.S. Civil War was stupid, because slavery could have
been
> abolished in 10 or 20 years with little or no bloodshed, by
wise
> use of graduated taxation of slaves and financial incentives to
> free them (in 1860, the Virginia legislature came within one
vote
> of abolishing slavery in Virginia), unions were a percipitate,
ill-
> conceived 'solution' that did vast damage and left a continuing
> trail of waste and corruption

    See first paragraph.  Considering
the conditions in force at the time, terrorism might have been
used.
Unions were (at the time) a reasonable, measured response.  To be
sure, they tend to keep expanding their role as time goes by.  At
least
the southern USA, the union population is dropping

    Least someone misunderstands me, I agree, more or less with
the current rant, right now.  But they were necessary in the
past.

> Unions are unhealthy by nature.  I
> don't mean the right of free people to form unions, that is a
given.
> I mean the use they are created for.  A free person should not
have
> the right to coerce another free person to their will.  Nor
should
> a group of people have such right.  What is the difference in a
> union saying "we will strike and harrass and ruin your company
if
> you don't give us such-and-such" and an extortioner saying "I
will
> bomb and ruin your company if you don't do such-and-such?"  The
> answer is nothing, they are the same.  "I will hurt you if you
> don't bow to my demands."  That whole concept is wrong.  We
should
> give such power only to the government, where it can be checked
by
> law, and by the will of the citizens through due process.  I
have
> no problem with employees forming a union and going to
management
> to say "We are unhappy, and unless you comply with
such-and-such
> conditions, we will quit."  Fine, that's simple negotiation.
But
> if they do quit, they have no business picketing or harrassing
the
> company.  Those are nothing more than legalized extortion.
>
> > Let me also point out that while our job requires both
technical
> > skill, ability, and pure raw talent to do, "management" would
like
> > nothing better than to be able to reduce 'Programmers' to
> > $5.45/hour type positions.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> current price, but they won't get away with it as long as
Burger
> King, et al are around. :-)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just because they want to do that doesn't mean they can make it
work,
> does it?  But if they can, then they have a point, don't they?
:-)

forcing them to pay $150-300 for a 'therapeutic phlebotomy' every other month,

with no ill effects.  In fact, there are some schools of thought that say this
kind of blood may actually help in the body's own blood replacement process.

- **References:** `<39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <nfJz5.2350$103.15333@news1.atl> <39cf7ebd_2@news5.newsfeeds.com> <sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl> <8qp9na$oml$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <moj1tsktb0aqe2r08o9c7g50bj6k9n0881@4ax.com> <39D192AC.1D45494A@optonline.net>`

```
On Wed, 27 Sep 2000 06:24:55 GMT, an orbiting mind control laser caused Liam
Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net> to write:

>> >There is a disease, fairly common in USA, that bleeding will treat.
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>informational meeting I missed.

I forget the formal latin name, but the condition causes these people to store
up iron, which then can reach toxic levels.  Some people with this condition
can store up so much iron they set off airport metal detectors nude.

The simplest and most effective treatment for this condition is bloodletting
as noted above.  About 5-7 years ago there was a stink that didn't make
national news about the situation, as described above.  I am told that the
rules have changed -- unfortunately after the Red Cross came though the office
so I have no way to verify.  But every time I hear the radio & TV say the Red
Cross is crying about blood reserves I call and tell them about this, but for
some reason that side of the story is always suppressed.

The problem being that blood donation is not covered under medical insurance,
even though it is medically necessary with these people, but the Red Cross
wouldn't take blood from people with this condition.  At one point there was
documented proof of Red Cross officials lobbying the FDA to prevent the use of
such blood in transfusions, at which point I stopped donating to the Red Cross
(blood and money).  My disaster relief donations now go to the Salvation Army,
which does a better job off the starting line.


Frank Ney  N4ZHG  WV/EMT-B  LPWV  NRA(L) ProvNRA GOA CCRKBA JPFO
```

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "keith smyth" <k17731NO@SPAMMhome.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T04:57:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i7fA5.7652$583.303307@news1.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <nfJz5.2350$103.15333@news1.atl> <39cf7ebd_2@news5.newsfeeds.com> <sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl>`

```
It seems Jimmy Hoffa isn't dead.  He really left his mark.  One would think
all union leaders were just like him, rather than he being an icon because
he was so far-and-away atypical.  Not to say there is no union corruption,
just that it is no more common that corruption in other forms of governance.

I'd also note, it seems few of you have read much by Charles Dickens.  You
have no idea of the tradition of management oppression of workers.  The game
is to get as much out of the worker for as little input as possible.
Something called efficiency.

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:sALz5.2564$103.17378@news1.atl...
> "Paul Raulerson" <praul@cegi.no-spam-net> wrote:
<<snip>>
> Unions are unhealthy by nature.  I
> don't mean the right of free people to form unions, that is a given.
> I mean the use they are created for.  A free person should not have
> the right to coerce another free person to their will.

So you aren't in managment then.  I've had the course and I've done the job.

Delegation, encouragement, motivation, persuasion, co-opting, coercion,
discipline and sacking are popular topics.  You start with the first, but
have to be prepared to move to the end if need be.  Of course one tries to
avoid humiliation.

>Nor should
> a group of people have such right.  What is the difference in a
> union saying "we will strike and harrass and ruin your company if
> you don't give us such-and-such" and an extortioner saying "I will
> bomb and ruin your company if you don't do such-and-such?"

Some countries bomb each other for no good reason.  That doesn't mean all
countries bomb each other for no good reason.

There are a few examples of unions destroying their employers.  Most of
these in Britain under the Labour government back 20 to 40 years ago.

There have been bitter labor battles in the US, but GM is still around, Ford
is still around.  Chrysler is part of another company.

There have been other bitter labor battles, like the one for migrant
workers, but then they had reason to fight.

There have been industries that have died out, but usually due to
mismanagement.

> answer is nothing, they are the same.  "I will hurt you if you
> don't bow to my demands."  That whole concept is wrong.  We should
> give such power only to the government, where it can be checked by
> law, and by the will of the citizens through due process.

A democratic government is just a bunch of elected officials.  A
contemporary union is just another type of democratic government.

>I have
> no problem with employees forming a union and going to management
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> King, et al are around. :-)

If we aren't worth the money, we aren't going to get the pay.  I agree it is
as simple as that.

But we are worth the money.  There are tremendous differences in the talents
of programmers.  Not the order of magnituide difference in productivity
found in old studies.

There are major differences in the efficiency, operability, realiability and
modifiability of code from one programmer to another.  Many programmers and
many analyst produce cheap junk.  Some produce great stuff.

>
> > Check out the latest round of silver bullet disease for
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> does it?  But if they can, then they have a point, don't they? :-)

Agreed.

> --
> Judson McClendon      judmc@bellsouth.net
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

organization to

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    That link just goes to the same old tired and invalid Norman
Matloff paper
> that all opponents of H-1B keep trotting out. Nothing new here.
Nothing valid
> either. That paper is a good example of how NOT to do an economic
analysis.
>
> > If people are loosing their jobs because of age anyway, what do
they have to
> > loose by trying to unionize their workplace?
>
>    Unions don't work when capital is mobile intellectual capital. The
whole idea
> of a union is to withhold (or more usually threaten to withhold)
labor until the
> owners of capital give the workers more stuff. The model is totally
inapplicable
> when the capital is intellectual capital that is contained within the
workers
> instead of being owned by the business owners.
>
> > Even young people would support unionization, once they are hired
on,
> > because it makes no sense to study for 4 years to get a career that
will
> > last only 8 years.
>
>    Another paranoid delusionist. There are many programmers that have
been in
> the field longer than 8 years. In addition, there are a number of
over-40
> programmers out there.
>
> > Even without unionization, as a group we are so disinterested in
national
> > and international affairs.  We need to employ the tactics of  the
NOW, the
> > Sierra Club, the NRA, and our employers.
>
>   You mean we need to emulate a bunch of nuts?
>
> > We need to recognize that industry groups are employer groups, and
they are
> > representing interests contrary to our own.
>
>   My employer's interest IS my own. If my employer fell on economic
hard times
> because a bunch of union assholes increased costs it would reduce the
value of
> my stock options. Go peddle your old industrial ideas to someone
else, like your
> dog.
>
Your employer's interest is to make as much money as possible and to
pay you as little as possible. You are very confused.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CF96BC.5D80A5AE@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au> <8qo28q$vrr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
philotsopher wrote:

> Your employer's interest is to make as much money as possible and to
> pay you as little as possible. You are very confused.

How much it pays you is not it's direct interest.  It's interest is to make
as much money as possible.  If it achieves this result by paying you very
little, then those both will be true.  If it achieves this by paying you
more and getting a better return, then it will do that.

Now, there are some other goals an employer has as well.  Apparently CEO's
make more money for being big than for being profitable.  Bill Gates would
rather have the power of being the head of a united Microsoft than to be
even richer with by owning most of two Microsofts (look at the net value of
Standard Oil & the phone company after breakups and compare it to IBM which
succeeded in staying together).

But keeping your pay as low as possible is like you keeping the cost your
tools as low as possible.  Yes it's nice to save money, and the money saved
can be used to further its real goal - but having the right tool at the
right time is also useful in furthering its real goal.

"The People" only get and maintain power if they get and maintain value to
those in power.  (If you get power via revolution, you trade leaders - that
isn't sufficient) To help "The People", make them valuable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Petros Economidis <pecon@net.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00

```
You are exactly right and as usual philotsopher is wrong and naive. The idea
that one can sometimes make more profit by paying X+1 for something that is X+2
more productive is beyond his level of economic understanding. Like little kids
trying to do economics, he foolishly assumes that lower costs ALWAYS means
higher profits.

Howard Brazee wrote:

> philotsopher wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> isn't sufficient) To help "The People", make them valuable.
```

philotsopher wrote:
> In article <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au>,
>   Karl Menger <kmenger@MtPellerin.au> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> pay you as little as possible. You are very confused.
Actually, the truth is somewhere in between, or, a combination of both,
rather. Of course, the interests of your employer aren't your own! But
they overlap to a degree. The idea is not to let your employer's side to
brainwash you into voluntarily relinquishing your interests where they
conflict with those of your employer. No one got it good by being
stupid, and, strictly speaking, no, the interests of my employer aren't
my interests. These are two distinct sets with some shared membership.
They're not identical by any means.


> 
> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

- **From:** "keith smyth" <k17731NO@SPAMMhome.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T04:57:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i7fA5.7651$583.303307@news1.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au>`

```

"Karl Menger" <kmenger@MtPellerin.au> wrote in message
news:39CEE603.89F9513@MtPellerin.au...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    That link just goes to the same old tired and invalid Norman Matloff
paper
> that all opponents of H-1B keep trotting out. Nothing new here. Nothing
valid
> either. That paper is a good example of how NOT to do an economic
analysis.
>

Hardly suprising.  It is a human rights and labour relations study.

You'll note, an economic analysis would primarily discuss economics and
economic issues from an analytic perspective.

> > If people are loosing their jobs because of age anyway, what do they
have to
> > loose by trying to unionize their workplace?
>
>    Unions don't work when capital is mobile intellectual capital. The
whole idea
> of a union is to withhold (or more usually threaten to withhold) labor
until the
> owners of capital give the workers more stuff. The model is totally
inapplicable
> when the capital is intellectual capital that is contained within the
workers
> instead of being owned by the business owners.

Thinking is labor.  Why wouldn't the model be inapplicable?  It works for
physicians.  It works for airline pilots.

Where unions do not work is where the labor is easily replacable, or where
the company is easily replaceable.  That is why unions do not work well for
unskilled workers or at MacDonalds.

>
> > Even young people would support unionization, once they are hired on,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Another paranoid delusionist. There are many programmers that have been
in
> the field longer than 8 years. In addition, there are a number of over-40
> programmers out there.
>

If I was a paranoid delusionist I'd be spouting anti-union ad hominum
arguments (that is, I'd be calling you names).

I'm over 40 and have been in the IT field over 8 years.

The existance of people like me doesn't negate my point.  We are having
difficulties.

> > Even without unionization, as a group we are so disinterested in
national
> > and international affairs.  We need to employ the tactics of  the NOW,
the
> > Sierra Club, the NRA, and our employers.
>
>   You mean we need to emulate a bunch of nuts?

The Sierra Club is a bunch of nuts?  Women are a bunch of nuts?  I don't
think so.  Effective lobbying to protect the environment is smart, not a
sign of insanity.  (Now Greenpeace, I'd agree they are nuts.  We don't want
to emulate them.)

Lobbying to reduce violence against women and unfair treatment in the
workplace is a sane thing to do if you are a woman.

Or we could emulate blacks.  Or farmers.  Or the manufacturering industry.
Or the banks.  Most groups in society have organized representation of some
kind.  Pick one you like that is doing a good job, and emulate them.

There is some sector of American life you like isn't there?

>
> > We need to recognize that industry groups are employer groups, and they
are
> > representing interests contrary to our own.
>
>   My employer's interest IS my own. If my employer fell on economic hard
times
> because a bunch of union assholes increased costs it would reduce the
value of
> my stock options. Go peddle your old industrial ideas to someone else,
like your
> dog.
>

What if your employer falls on hard times because his workers are anarchist
a__holes?  Really, foul language.  The last gasp of those who have lost the
argument.

- Keith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00

```
In article <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com>,
  Dio <Dio@dio.com> wrote:
>
>
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> > > There is no shortage of labour. Just a shortage at the salary the
employers
> > > wish to pay.
> >
> > You could say that about any job.  I would be willing to pick
oranges for a
> > million dollars per day.  But orange growers need to find labor
which will
> > allow them to stay in business
>
>    You are probably wasting your time trying to explain to anti-H-
1Bers why they
> are wrong about even the most basic economics.

We believe in the law of supply and demand. Glut the labor force and
you lower salaries due to lower demand. You believe the government has
a responsibility to control the economy. We don't.

They want a world in which all firms
> pay their workers very very very high salaries yet the costs of those
salaries is
> never passed on to the consumers (who are those same workers in the
end).

Heaven forbid if working Americans got high salaries. And worse yet,
what if their salaries were taken out of the bloated pay of corporate
CEOs instead of increased prices.

There's
> is a never-never land of fantasy and illusion, where all you have to
do is tax the
> rich to make up the difference between salaries and prices.

Tax them? Hell no. I say let's put a cap on the corporate raiders
golden parachutes.

The idea that wages ARE
> a production cost that gets passed on to the consumer is a completely
foreign
> concept to them, that I quite frankly think they lack the intellect
to grasp.
>

Uhhhh. Me caveman. Caveman don't understand dio-economics.
```

philotsopher wrote:

> In article <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com>,
>   Dio <Dio@dio.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> you lower salaries due to lower demand.

   Nope, you don't even understand basic supply and demand. If you have a
shortage then increasing quantitied supplied works to lessen the shortage.
If prices are rigid downward, such as in a situation where suppliers will
not accept lower prices, then what happens as supply increases is that the
shortage is lessened but prices don't fall. The fact that prices (in this
case salaries) are still going up, it seems pretty certain that there is no
"glut" of labor.

> You believe the government has
> a responsibility to control the economy.

   Nope.

> We don't.

   So you believe in open borders with the free flow of goods, capital and
people?

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> CEOs instead of increased prices.

   LOL. Do you even know how utterly stupid you are being here? Even if you
confiscated all the executive pay in the entire US and distributed it among
all the rest of the workers the actual difference in salary for the average
worker would be very small and their real (inflation-adjusted) income would
remain exactly the same since the goods and services they could buy would
remain unimproved by such a redistribution of income. It is socialist myth
that you just have to take everything away from the rich to solve all
problems.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> golden parachutes.

  That is not going to appreciably increase worker's salaries.

>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Uhhhh. Me caveman. Caveman don't understand dio-economics.

  Again more proof that philpotsmoker only states the truth when he is
being sarcastic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Woshington Pravda cries for H1b's

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** pwgoss@notsohotmail.com (P.W. Goss)
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39ccb3f5.10183240@news.binc.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <8qg33h$9c1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Fri, 22 Sep 2000 16:58:39 GMT, in comp.software.year-2000 you
wrote:

>Tax them? Hell no. I say let's put a cap on the corporate raiders
>golden parachutes.
>--
>philotsopher

I am starting to wonder if i) lower-rung prospective employees really
check out a company prior to accepting a position and ii) if existing
lower-rung employees really follow what is happening in their company;
especially with the top management.

If I was working on an assembly line I'd sure want to know who was
holding the rudder of the company.  If the powers that be brought in a
"cost cutter" you can bet your bottom buck I'd be looking at the want
ads if I wasn't a major player and it was likely someone was going to
"move my cheese."  For public companies any yay-hoo can get this
information on Yahoo! or even at a local library.

For the whimpy weepers out there who cry about anybody "taking their
job" or being a victim of costs cuts, well, it took more than one day
for this to be decided upon and to be put into motion.  You were
probably too comfortable and happy, all the while looking the other
way.

There are exceptions, I'm sure, but most times the writing is right
there on the wall.  You merely need to take time to read it.

Lastly, sh1t happens.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Woshington Pravda cries for H1b's

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Hubert Humphrey <dems@Minnesota.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39CD131E.E221B90C@Minnesota.com>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <8qg33h$9c1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39ccb3f5.10183240@news.binc.net>`

```


"P.W. Goss" wrote:

> On Fri, 22 Sep 2000 16:58:39 GMT, in comp.software.year-2000 you
> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> there on the wall.  You merely need to take time to read it.

  You are SO right! I have left firms that I could see were about to
undergo a huge bloodletting and after I left, sure enough they did. It is
never the case that a firm is making shitloads of money, being
well-accepted in the market, growing without losing profitability and then
in the middle of all this just starts cutting blindly clear out of the
blue. It is always the case that they are suffering losses, seeing
problems with their marketshare, or have some kind of huge lawsuit pending
against them that everyone knows about.

   A few years ago I could figure out rather easily that my then-employer
was not making any money and was burning through VC money at very high
rates. It was easy to see that the number of orders and their size was
simply way too small and wasn't growing. Another sign was that the firm
was being severely cannibalized by a consulting firm and yet was
unbelievably cheap to the point where they KNEW it was cutting into their
ability to produce. I knew that VC's weren't going to let that last
forever and would soon start requiring cost-cutting or would withdraw
their money. I left. Sure enough, three months later the firm laid off 40%
of their staff and most of those layoffs were the good, competent but more
expensive employees who had been there the longest and had been the most
faithful. I knew that the management there was going to react exactly in
that fashion when the time of reckoning occured.
```

Bill wrote:
> 
> Hi Phil,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> (also Australia and other 'nice' destinations)
Oh, sure. But it would be a bit less "effective" <g> than making those
importees indentured. But you're right, of course you can achieve the
same effect by importing fully-empowered workers, you'll just need more
of them and it will perhaps take longer.

>  In NYC, most projects I've worked on are about 60 - 70% non-us nationals.
> (but so is NYC).
I'm well aware of that.

> Also despite the 'shortage' of workers. Salaries are less then they were 15
> years ago.
> In the early 80's salaries for COBOL/IBM/IMS programmers was about $800 a
> day.  Now they're substantially less.
That's because of technology changes. You can make good money with
current ones, at least as a contractor. 

> There is no shortage of labour. Just a shortage at the salary the employers
> wish to pay.
I'm used to the thinking that under capitalism there can be no
shortages. Shortages belong in the Soviet Union because there the evil
government set arbitrary prices. Now, turns out, when it's time to pay
up, the shortage phenomenon has surfaced under capitalism as well. Some
people are very smart and want to have it both ways.


> Bill
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Why aren't there enough technical graduates was Re: Woshington Pravda cries for H1b's

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers

```
Could it be that a large number of enterprises value their technical
people - programmers, engineers, etc. less than MBA's?  If you were
going for best compensation and lifetime employability would you become
an engineer?  Also does computer science really teach that which is
necessary for business programming as opposed to scientific programming
or operating system programming?  Note that by the latter I mean
programming the components of the operating system be it Unix, OS390 or
the Unisys flavors.

Clark Morris, cfmtech@istar.ca

```
>> The H1B program is pitiful. There should be unrestricted immigration
>> for those with a guaranteed job - particularly high-tech jobs.

unfortunately, that doesn't match reality. even if the immigrant could
speak english, they won't have the technical know-how because their
countries, in general, could not afford it. the only jobs h1b's will
steal in the us are the ones our citizens (cough business majors)
won't take. and do you think citizens who management won't pay wages
will sit around and pay for their technical education?

the h1b is just a sub-minimum wage scheme for immigrant house-cramming
that management will allow in others neighborhoods, but do not want to
put them up in their own home. and quite frankly, if management takes
in a person from another country with no ties here, to me, you might
as well start up a labor camp for orphans.

>> Recent estimates I've seen show US universities will graduate less
>> than 1/3 of the needed engineers for the next decade (400,000 vs. 1.4

true engineering is research and development. the h1b sounds to me 
alot like technician jobs, and no one needs a degree for that.

>> million). If the technical people are not here, and cannot come, then
>> the jobs go there.

in fact, i don't believe management will move, and ya know why?
startup and relocation costs. how many power-lunchers want to move
overseas to a dinky village and learn a new language to save a few
bucks? and then when you've moved, do ya think they are going to be
able to sell it over here? guess what? not gonna happen.

the h1b scheme is basically slavery, and i'm not against that. i am
against management who pretends it isn't and then won't front a one
way back fee. (once they are here, management will simply dump them if
they become unreliable).

"oh, they quit and i can't find them"

don't worry, we'll take the plane ticket fee anyways. :P

That's just plain silly. If the potential H1Bs don't have the language

wetbacks with leaf blowers here. For many, their native language is

superb.

who do so as well as a number in pure science (geology for one). The
vast majority of engineers, architects, critical care nurses, and
other highly educated folk don't do basic research - they just do the
job.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why aren't there enough technical graduates was Re: Woshington Pravda cries for H1b's

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0jVy5.1271$U92.100252@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C7623E.D9687B9E@istar.ca> <hgofssse2p8as7egjtqom9qo6pgtr75mc1@4ax.com> <O9cy5.311$bi.67767@nnrp2.sbc.net> <ak4lssc4jp2m1m3ofk1p36d1hs7ee0uof1@4ax.com>`

```

"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>

> >wetbacks with leaf blowers here. For many, their native language is
> >English (India, Singapore, Ireland, Hong Kong) and their education
is
> >superb.
>
> if their home countries education was so good, why is the industry
> there not flourishing and here it is?
>
A very good question. Liberal (as in left-leaning) governments want to
supply as much assistance as possible. What these governments CAN
provide is education. What these governments CANNOT provide is
wealth-creating jobs. (Governments cannot produce wealth - governments
can only destroy wealth.)

Left-leaning (as in liberal) governments believe that wealth can be
neither created nor destroyed, that the amount of wealth is fixed.
These same governments do believe, however, that the available wealth
can be redistributed. So, they take what wealth they have, train their
citizens, their citizens come to the USA and thereby redistribute the
wealth. Makes sense to me.
```

>> >wetbacks with leaf blowers here. For many, their native language is

>> if their home countries education was so good, why is the industry

>A very good question. Liberal (as in left-leaning) governments want to

it was sarcastic. i meant to imply that paper pushing has nothing to
do with the actual production of physical goods.

>supply as much assistance as possible. What these governments CAN
>provide is education. What these governments CANNOT provide is

it's kinda hard to provide education when you can't afford equipment,
especially when you have to sell your goods to countries which do have
equipment and you have to compete with them.

>wealth-creating jobs. (Governments cannot produce wealth - governments
>can only destroy wealth.)

ahh yes. governments can only destroy wealth and that is why countrys'
entire markets are based on government legal tender.

>Left-leaning (as in liberal) governments believe that wealth can be
>neither created nor destroyed, that the amount of wealth is fixed.

let's pretend i am an oil ceo. if i can produce twice as much oil, but
a person can only drive 1 car, and everyone has a car, can i sell
twice as much gasoline for them to drive? no. clearly the market is
fixed.

>These same governments do believe, however, that the available wealth
>can be redistributed. So, they take what wealth they have, train their
>citizens, their citizens come to the USA and thereby redistribute the
>wealth. Makes sense to me.

no, you got it wrong. they can't make wealth because the capital stays
in the same place and competes more effectively than with local
industry, and at the chance, they leave their country. nothing to
redistribute when you have no goods to begin with (unless you claim
they made more than us at one point, redistributed it and then the
wealth *disappeared*).

- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C7623E.D9687B9E@istar.ca> <hgofssse2p8as7egjtqom9qo6pgtr75mc1@4ax.com> <O9cy5.311$bi.67767@nnrp2.sbc.net> <ak4lssc4jp2m1m3ofk1p36d1hs7ee0uof1@4ax.com> <0jVy5.1271$U92.100252@nnrp2.sbc.net> <0b4rssgs6ajdjsaqd8o4kncpr5r34cbtss@4ax.com>`

"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> >wealth-creating jobs. (Governments cannot produce wealth - governments
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> entire markets are based on government legal tender.

Shut down the commerce in a country and see how long it takes for the
value of that 'government issued currency' to devalue to zero.  Wealth
is goods, services and property.  Currency is simply an abstract
representation of how much goods, services and property you can buy.
Most really wealthy people have only a tiny fraction of their wealth
in cash.  Why do you think currency in Communist countries isn't worth
spit?  Because their 'productivity' is laughable.  Why do you think
countries such as the US, Germany and Japan have much stronger currency?
Production.  Private enterprise produces, governments don't.  Q.E.D.
```

G Moore wrote:

> let's pretend i am an oil ceo. if i can produce twice as much oil, but
> a person can only drive 1 car, and everyone has a car, can i sell
> twice as much gasoline for them to drive? no. clearly the market is
> fixed.

That's silly, I suspect, on purpose - but...

Pretend you were THE oil CEO, John D. Rockefeller.  Nobody had cars.
Cheaper gasoline creates more people buying gasoline.  Cheaper fuel oil
means more people heat with fuel oil, and more power plants use fuel oil -
or more fertilizer and plastics companies use oil as raw materials.
```

In article <39C7623E.D9687B9E@istar.ca>, "Clark F. Morris, Jr." 
<cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

> Could it be that a large number of enterprises value their technical
> people - programmers, engineers, etc. less than MBA's?  If you were
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> or operating system programming? 

Yes, if I were "going for best compensation..." I would pick the 
sciences and engineering.

I studied physics and joined Intel Corporation in 1974. Nearly every 
manager in the company was a Ph.D. in engineering or the sciences or was 
otherwise trained. 

Tens of thousands of multi-millinaires in the Silicon Valley, at many 
hundreds of start-up companies. Ditto for other regions. And most of 
these multi-millionaires came out of the sciences or engineering.

And all but one of the billionaires I know came out of the sciences.


--Tim May
```

necessarily the same thing.  It isn't necessary to be
totally in sync about 100% of the issue.  I went to the
Nader2000 website, and there's nothing there about
confiscatory taxation.  It does say he's in favor of
progressive taxation, but that's different.

positions on most of the core issues that affect our future.

The joke's on you.  Your vote is already diluted.  Multiply the probability
that your vote will make a difference by the value of what you gain by having
your vote succeed, and subtract the cost of voting, including the cost of
your time.  The result is nearly always negative.  Voters are nearly always
suckers.

- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C8093E.FE45A944@blarney.att.net> <8q92m8$24l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39C8326D.FBBA002@dio.com> <8qa00q$7g5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <39C8FAE0.B9F763FC@dio.com> <8qr6h6$i3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <u433ts4r8adr3thjhvf0bgpkcqrvu9speq@4ax.com> <8qtgii$dp9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


philotsopher wrote:

> I have to agree with you. Unfortunately since high-tech sheep are not
> willing to unionize, the vote is all I have.

   What would you have if high tech workers unionized? Another vote. Big
whoop.

> Of course Gore and Bush
> have been bought off, and the corporate media machine has marginalized
> Buchanan and Nader. That makes any of my voting choices irrelevant.

   Unless the election turns on one single vote, your individual vote IS
irrelevant to the outcome, even if you vote for the winner. Unless you are
able to convince large enough numbers of people to vote for your favorite
candidate, not only is your individual vote irrelevant to the outcome, so
is your opinion (as is mine). Even though I think you are an idiot, I
could care less if you vote or who you vote for, even if it were the
socialist candidate. We are both irrelevant to the presidential election.

   That's the strange thing about democracy, it sedates the masses by
granting them the illusion of individual power. Mainitaining that illusion
protects us from anarchy, totalitarianism and despotism. Odd.
```

```
If I vote, everyone like me votes, by definition.

If I write my politicians, everyone like me writes their politicians, by
definition.

This is how I look at it.

Your vote does count.  Have hope.  Be politically active.  Encourage those
with similar view to be active too.

- Keith

<s261102@tso.ozt> wrote in message
news:u433ts4r8adr3thjhvf0bgpkcqrvu9speq@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 26 Sep 2000 22:04:26 GMT, philotsopher <philotsopher@my-deja.com>
> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

probability
> that your vote will make a difference by the value of what you gain by
having
> your vote succeed, and subtract the cost of voting, including the cost of
> your time.  The result is nearly always negative.  Voters are nearly
always
> suckers.
>

## Thread: Another quick question — 25 msgs

### Another quick question

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T06:13:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```

Not to spite anyone here, but could someone explain what COBOL is used for in
every day life?  What little I have saw of COBOL it looks like a database that
takes a lot of extra time to plan.  What type of things can u do with COBOL
that is unique?  Remember I am not expierenced with COBOL or anything else, but
HTML.
Thanks from the newbie,
Deek
```

#### ↳ Re: Another quick question

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8pvhvk$b7a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>,
  deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:
>
> Not to spite anyone here, but could someone explain what COBOL is
used for in
> every day life?  What little I have saw of COBOL it looks like a
database that
> takes a lot of extra time to plan.  What type of things can u do with
COBOL
> that is unique?  Remember I am not expierenced with COBOL or anything
else, but
> HTML.
> Thanks from the newbie,
> Deek

Hi:

Remember first that COBOL is a business language. It is not
intended for streaming media, network routing, or other
complicated stuff. It IS intended for payroll, accounting,
and every other type of ordinary business application.

COBOL by itself is crippled since it does not have a GUI.
However, there are many add-ons to COBOL such as my favorite,
Flexus SP2 which lets you do virtually anything allowed by
the Windows API including pictures and sound. There are
also things which let your COBOL programs be thin clients,
deal with the Windows Print Manager, and so on.

You can do virtually anything with COBOL and you can do it
easier and faster than with any other language.

Don't let anybody turn you off COBOL. You should check out
Flexus at Flexus.com.  You might also buy Thane Hubbell's book
'Teach yourself COBOL in 24 hours' - it comes with a Fujitsu
compiler and SP2. Thane is very active in the forum and can
help you a lot if you get stuck.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

> COBOL by itself is crippled since it does not have a GUI.

Well, I don't know about, 'crippled.'

I'd prefer to think of COBOL as a senior citizen: it's reliable, steadfast,
solid. Maybe it doesn't get the job done as fast or with as much sex appeal
as the younguns (C++ et al) but it *does* get the job done and has a track
record to prove it.

MCM

#### ↳ Re: Another quick question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zlRw5.6127$Ds.40902@news4.atl>`
- **References:** `<20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
"Deek40" <deek40@aol.com> wrote:
>
> Not to spite anyone here, but could someone explain what COBOL is used for in
> every day life?  What little I have saw of COBOL it looks like a database that
> takes a lot of extra time to plan.  What type of things can u do with COBOL
> that is unique?

In general, you can write anything with any general purpose language,
so COBOL isn't 'unique' in that respect.  But COBOL (COmmon Business
Oriented Language) was designed for the common bread and butter business
applications, such as payroll, general ledger, accounts payable, and
any application that handles money.  COBOL has powerful capabilities
in data formatting and printing.  Unlike most programming languages,
COBOL easily supports decimal numbers, including decimal fractions
(e.g. 123.456) exactly.  For what COBOL was designed for, nothing else
is quite as good.  There are COBOL programs still running after more
than 30 years.  COBOL is easy to learn and program, is simple rather
than complex and obscure, is efficient and reliable.  In other words,
COBOL is very cost effective, for what it is designed for, which is
why there is significantly more COBOL code in use today than in all
other computer languages put together.  But COBOL was never intended
for writing system programs, such as operating systems, compilers or
word processors.  Other languages are much better for those purposes.
But newer versions of COBOL can be used for GUI and web applications
very efficiently.
```

#### ↳ Re: Another quick question

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PLSw5.147$Y24.82172@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
The kinds of programs the ordinary person interacts with are (usually)
not suited to COBOL: Games, databases, web browsers, e-mail, e-bay,
etc.

But, if you get a paycheck, 8 times out of 10 your paycheck was
generated by a COBOL program. If you buy a book or a wood screw or an
automobile, 8 times out of 10 the business maintains it's inventory
with COBOL. If you trade stock, pay a mortgage, use your health
insurance, or interact with most of the mundane things that work below
the radar, their foundation is COBOL.

COBOL has been called prehistoric - a dinosaur, with the implication
that its time has passed. Actually, it is a SHARK. It hasn't changed
much since prehistoric times because it's a damn near perfect killing
machine as it is.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another quick question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C6188A.11066AA4@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com> <PLSw5.147$Y24.82172@nnrp1.sbc.net> <La6x5.3600$eo6.611885@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I was going to post the web page which had Peter Coffee's May 13, 1996 PC
Week article, but when I went to my bookmark, it was gone.  But I have the
article on my wall.  One quote from his article:

"  For example, as I noted in a COBOL roundup in 1991, people are flunking
biology when they call COBOL a dinosaur.  COBOL is a shark, a design that
was mature when dinosaurs were new and that still dominates its niche while
the dinosaurs are long gone.
  C++, on the other hand, may be headed for dinosaur status.  It's evolved
from simple beginnings into fantastic, even grotesque variations on its
original theme, and it may not survive the next wrenching change in its
environment."

That said, I am not sure that CoBOL isn't changing too much now.  It is
trying to evolve, but it may be moving away from the simplicity it once
had.  We'll see.

Michael Mattias wrote:

> Jerry P <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
> news:PLSw5.147$Y24.82172@nnrp1.sbc.net...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

#### ↳ Re: Another quick question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C61643.BAA0AA66@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```


Deek40 wrote:

> Not to spite anyone here, but could someone explain what COBOL is used for in
> every day life?  What little I have saw of COBOL it looks like a database that
> takes a lot of extra time to plan.  What type of things can u do with COBOL
> that is unique?  Remember I am not expierenced with COBOL or anything else, but
> HTML.

First, CoBOL is not a database, it is a language.

Second, with any language or project, taking extra time up front to plan isn't
necessarily a bad thing.  In fact studies have shown that the total amount of time
spent in analysis plus the total time spent in testing is a constant.

Third, the main advantage CoBOL has is that it is easy to read and to maintain.
The longer you expect to be using a program, the more advantageous it is to use
CoBOL.  It is easy to see the business rules when they are written in a
pseudo-English syntax.

Fourth, it is used in just about everything in real life.  It has been around a
long time, and the last study I read (two years ago), showed that it was still the
most common language in new code.  I do not recommend using it to write device
drivers and the like.  It was designed for business applications, and that is what
it is used most for.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8qg6o9$e5p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000916021344.17077.00000001@ng-fn1.aol.com>,
  deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:

COBOL compilers are designed to produce really fast write/read input
and output on files with sometimes millions of records.  It is this
ability for fast I/O on Compilers designed for the mainframe working
environment that make it what it is.

While COBOL does not have a GUI as a standard interface, CICS provides
an ability to create an interface for the user.  CICS is capable of
accepting on-line input and building screens.  A Mainframe with CICS
can support hundreds or thousands of on-line users at one time and is
very fast.  Most of all the Mainframe doesnt need to be rebooted
constatly like Windows!  That is worth considering!  Constant Up Time.

We use cobol to read all of our course files and make the html pages
for all of our courses at one time and store them and serve them up
from the mainframe to the web.  HTML is just Text, and COBOL is
extremely fast with text files.
>
> Not to spite anyone here, but could someone explain what COBOL is
used for in
> every day life?  What little I have saw of COBOL it looks like a
database that
> takes a lot of extra time to plan.  What type of things can u do with
COBOL
> that is unique?  Remember I am not expierenced with COBOL or anything
else, but
> HTML.
> Thanks from the newbie,
> Deek
>
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```
