<a id="t-876a572b47"></a>
## Thread: Debate Re-defined — 3 msgs

### Debate Re-defined

- **From:** "Fred Baker" <fbaker@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tWOw5.3421$5m.18193@skycache.prestige.net>`

```
Alright I asked for it and got it... I'll be a little more specific.

Unisys 2200-6  Running  COBOL 7R2F

Getting bogged down and in need of more power.

We were thinking of dropping for another Unisys however seems there are alot
of other platforms out there that might be better. HP, Digital maybe even
Sun. As you can probably tell I am not a COBOL programmer but definately
more on the business end. However I do decided what is purchased and what is
not. So the question re-defined would be if this current system is beggining
to bog down and we were wanting to revamp. Would it be smart to ramp up
horsepower (processor) to make the older software run faster or maybe even
port the current program to a more current version that might take advantage
or the same hardware and run faster.

Thanks
Fred Baker
```

#### ↳ Re: Debate Re-defined

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T02:49:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C43144.D4E90B90@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<tWOw5.3421$5m.18193@skycache.prestige.net>`

```
Fred Baker wrote:
> 
> Alright I asked for it and got it... I'll be a little more specific.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

First, I'd like to know what the MIPS (Millions of Instructions
Per Second, aka by IBM Corp, Meaningless Indicator of Processor
Speed) rating is for this system.  Second, is this system related
to the old U-1122 (AKA 1100-22) running EXEC-8 and how related
(Real, no VIRTual storage, 36 bit word, etc.)?

Are you allowed to migrate to another architecture?  If so, what
are you allowed to migrate to, and what time-frame & budget?

In days gone by, an IBM 3081 would run rings around the U1122
when it came to running multiple jobs simultaneously, while the
U1122 would beat the snot out of the IBM on a one to one run,
particularly if it came to number crunching.
```

#### ↳ Re: Debate Re-defined

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <dgallan1@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:34:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jwyx5.10461$%h6.2663269@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<tWOw5.3421$5m.18193@skycache.prestige.net>`

```
I'm in a large Unisys 2200 shop (ClearPath IX8400 with 8 processors
partitioned 4 and 4) and we have no problems with COBOL performance. I know
I have a lot more horsepower than an 2200-600 but depending on your
application inventory porting to a new platform could be quite expensive.

Based on the code base that we have, a lot of batch programs use
Unisys-specific features, some of which are accessed via assembler routines.
Some stuff could be converted fairly easily but some I think would be more
troublesome. TIP transactions are potentially your worst problem. Since your
screen I/O handling is probably unlike anything you would be moving to, and
the TIP environment is rather unique, it could be prohibitive to do a port.
You might be closer to a rewrite. What about database access? If you use
DMS2200 you're pretty much in rewrite territory again. If using RDMS you
might have a shot at something.

There is a company in Ontario, Canada, (I don't remember the name) which
claims to have a product(s) which would allow you bring much of your code to
NT. I know they support a TIP environment and I think they even do something
for the DMS code. Looked interesting.

You also don't mention whether you are using ACOB or UCOB COBOL. From the
version you mention I'm guessing ACOB. We saw a decrease in execution times,
sometimes significant (25-50%) by moving applications to UCOB. I think your
machine supports UCOB and extended mode but I'm not sure. The amount of pain
involved in that switch is also dependent upon features used in your current
applications.

Douglas Gallant

"Fred Baker" <fbaker@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:tWOw5.3421$5m.18193@skycache.prestige.net...
> Alright I asked for it and got it... I'll be a little more specific.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

alot

beggining

advantage
> or the same hardware and run faster.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

in quanto un'eventuale rete e' molto meno costosa da realizzare
rispetto a Windows.

<a id="t-eea8b67785"></a>
## Thread: EVALUATE vs IF — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I haven't seen any reply to my earlier note, so I wanted to re-post with
slightly different wording.

Consider 3 sets of code:

Sample 1:

Evaluate aField
  When "A"
      Perform A-rtn
   When "B"
      Perform B-rtn
    When "C"
       Perform C-rtn
 End-Evaluate

    ***

Sample 2:

If aField = "A"
      Perform A-rtn
 Else If aField = "B"
      Perform B-rtn
 Else If aField = "C"
       Perform C-rtn
   .

    ***

Sample 3:

If aField = "A"
      Perform A-rtn.
If aField = "B"
      Perform B-rtn.
If aField = "C"
       Perform C-rtn
   .

***

Now the "question" is which two of these pieces of code are LOGICALLY
equivalent and WHY is the 3rd sample "logically different"?

When you "know" (accept) the answer, you will see that NESTED "IF's" can be
coded to be logically equivalent to EVALUATE statements - but consecutive
ones cannot be coded to GUARANTEE logical equivalence.

Then the question becomes "which is more easily maintainable" nested IF's or
EVALUATE.  I certainly would go with the latter - but I do understand that
the former MAY be more maintainable - to the person who uses it regularly and
does not always use '85 Standard compilers with EVALUATE support.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>

- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You pretty much already answered your question here.  The third piece of
code is only equivilant in a guaratee that aField cannot be changed within
the first 2 routines - something that could be true when the program if
first coded, but may change when the first idiot maintains the progam.

> When you "know" (accept) the answer, you will see that NESTED "IF's" can
be
> coded to be logically equivalent to EVALUATE statements - but consecutive
> ones cannot be coded to GUARANTEE logical equivalence.
>

Technically, the nested IF's even have a limit at which they are no longer
equivilant to the EVALUATE, but let's leave that to another discussion....

> Then the question becomes "which is more easily maintainable" nested IF's
or
> EVALUATE.  I certainly would go with the latter - but I do understand that
> the former MAY be more maintainable - to the person who uses it regularly
and
> does not always use '85 Standard compilers with EVALUATE support.
>

I personally use the EVALUATE statement.  That is my preference, as I feel
it is easier to read.  Many others in my company use the IF's since they are
used to them and find them easier.  Six of one, half-dozen of the other --
if most situations....

> --
> Bill Klein
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q5296$88p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi Mr. Klein:

As we all know only too well, if someone invents something for
programmers, it will easily find practitioners. (See Skill-Set.)

I think that COBOL programming should be taught so that
the student learns to program using an elementary subset
(my COBOL 60). This would presumably ensure that they
learned all about IF and the basics.

Then, they should be presented with EVALUATE and other things
as potentially useful alternatives.

My concern is that the student is basically told that
it isn't cool to use IF anymore and they should use EVALUATE
without ever really getting to know IF.

Thus, something becomes dominant with out due cause.

I wonder what percentage of new programmers would
automatically choose EVALUATE over IF?

I am quite sure that there are cases for both.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

> My concern is that the student is basically told that
> it isn't cool to use IF anymore and they should use EVALUATE
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> automatically choose EVALUATE over IF?

I suspect it is low.  The reason is that for very simple cases, they
would use IF.  Since they start off coding very simple cases, the
English Language IF statement would seem natural for these cases.  Then
if they have to write out a decision table, EVALUATE fits better.  There
is a place in the middle where some people pick one, and others pick the
other.

Everybody uses IF, most current programmers also use EVALUATE.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <djacot@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q52js$8ju$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I prefer EVALUATE since there is the statement 'WHEN OTHER'. I would
even like the COBOL compiler to generate an error at runtime if neither
of the WHEN statements are true and no OTHER is coded. Every reasonable
compiler I know of (PL/I, REXX) does that. Why not COBOL?

Your third 'solution' has a quite strange 'logical other':

If aField NOT = "A" AND aField NOT = "B" AND aField = "C" THEN ...

The second piece of code is OK too, the logical 'OTHER' is just an
'ELSE' without an IF.

Let's agree that the first two solutions are equivalent, the first for
programmers with more than 25 years of coding practice, the second for
the more recent programmers &-).

In article <8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> I haven't seen any reply to my earlier note, so I wanted to re-post
with
> slightly different wording.
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> When you "know" (accept) the answer, you will see that NESTED "IF's"
can be
> coded to be logically equivalent to EVALUATE statements - but
consecutive
> ones cannot be coded to GUARANTEE logical equivalence.
>
> Then the question becomes "which is more easily maintainable" nested
IF's or
> EVALUATE.  I certainly would go with the latter - but I do understand
that
> the former MAY be more maintainable - to the person who uses it
regularly and
> does not always use '85 Standard compilers with EVALUATE support.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q5sbr$iuv$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8q52js$8ju$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
COBOL *does* make the "WHEN OTHER" feature optional.  I wasn't part of the
Standard process when this was "decided".  However, it does seem at least
some what reasonable to me - as it does mean that there are "equivalent"
EVALUATE and IF statements, e.g.

 Evaluate aField
    When "A"
        Perform A-rtn
     When "B"
         Perform B-rtn
      When other
          Perform Other-Rtn
   End-Evaluate

is logically equivalent to

 If aField = "A"
        Perform A-rtn
  Else
     If aField = "B"
         Perform B-rtn
    Else
          Perform Other-Rtn
    End-If
  End-If

   ****
While

Evaluate aField
    When "A"
        Perform A-rtn
     When "B"
         Perform B-rtn
  End-Evaluate

is logically equivalent to

 If aField = "A"
        Perform A-rtn
  Else
     If aField = "B"
         Perform B-rtn
     End-If
  End-IF

In other words, either nested IFs or EVALUATE *can* be coded to handle
"OTHER" situations - but it is also true that either can be coded to "ignore"
such.   Of course, the last two examples are ALSO equivalent to:

Evaluate aField
    When "A"
        Perform A-rtn
     When "B"
         Perform B-rtn
      When other
          Continue
   End-Evaluate

is logically equivalent to

 If aField = "A"
        Perform A-rtn
  Else
     If aField = "B"
         Perform B-rtn
    Else
          Continue
    End-If
  End-If

  ***

Once again, which you code may well depend on what you are used to and/or the
complexity of the actual logic and/or the expected maintenance/enhancements
expected for the "life" of the program.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
2000-09-18 16:40:00 MEST

"William M. Klein" schrieb:

...

Same question can be formulated for most other
programming languages also - except one uses
"EVALUATE ... ALSO ...". But why discuss
the respective merrits of IF vs. EVALUATE?
Its a matter of style, preferences, experience,
editor, fatigue, temperature and everything
else. Two programmer will never agree in all
cases which one of the constructs shall
be used.

BTW: In my old Ada-compiler-days, we implemented
the case-statement (that is the Ada-equivalent
of EVALUATE) depending on the density of the
"cases": A sparse case-statement was turned
into some nested if-statements. It just seemed
natural to do it this way.

Herwig
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** "Mike Sheehan" <msheehan@pks.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q5dmo$jrk$1@kermit.esat.net>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
It may depend on the depth of nested ifs... in circumstances where you have
more than three nested ifs... an Evaluate might be considered more
readable... The Evaluate can be more useful when you use the WHEN OTHER as a
catch all... Adding a specific case easy easier than adding another nested
if...

Good sense will tell you usually when to use one or the other....
Mike
Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote in message
news:39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com...
> 2000-09-18 16:40:00 MEST
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C742AD.455A32E9@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com> <8q5dmo$jrk$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
I have to agree.........

I use both EVALUATE and nested IF statements in my code depending on the
complexity of what I'm trying to accomplish and what makes the most sense in the
situation.  One of the things I like about the flexibility of COBOL is that
there are always multiple ways of doing just about anything.  So, it leaves me
the opportunity to choose the best balance between efficiency, clarity and
maintainability!

Dave Bretz

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C7479C.9D01CABD@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com> <8q5dmo$jrk$1@kermit.esat.net> <39C742AD.455A32E9@mescoma.com>`

```
2000-09-19 12:55:21 MEST

"David P. Bretz" & ... & ... wrote:

As in all such cases, oftenly the history
of a piece of software takes the decission
out of ones hands.

Consider a place where a single IF ... is the
clear choice. But then, in the developing
project, a new condition must be observed
at the same place, then another. Always a
small increment in the code, and always the
choice to add another "IF ..." was best and
most economical.

Then, some day, a fellow programmer, or worse,
a manager, views the code and ask: "Why the
hell did you not use an EVALUATE there?"

In such cases, I do not discuss the
merits of IF vs EVALUATE. Rather, I count
to ten, look out of the window and think
of nice things. Hanging, shooting or
electrocuting, for instance.

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q945i02jii@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com> <8q5dmo$jrk$1@kermit.esat.net> <39C742AD.455A32E9@mescoma.com> <39C7479C.9D01CABD@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
Was that a quote from Jack Handy?

I agree though, who cares.  COBOL is very strongly typed.  If one cannot
code with both EVALUATE and IF/THEN/ELSE/END-IF... then maybe they should
brush up on their skills.  A good programmer probably uses both constructs,
depending on the situation.

Jeff

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T00:28:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XwTx5.458$TF3.272851@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com> <8q5dmo$jrk$1@kermit.esat.net> <39C742AD.455A32E9@mescoma.com> <39C7479C.9D01CABD@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
What I encounter is the stuff where the code started out with a situation
where a simple IF is "right" (whatever that means), but after the user adds
a few new rules (or includes the rules he forgot the first time around),
under the new scenario EVALUATE is what feels "right."

Unfortunately, there's usually been about sixteen other mods to the code,
all of it done by lazy programmers who never bothered to change the
"IF..ELSE"s to "EVALUATE..WHEN"s and I get stuck doing it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T03:08:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C82A1F.EFC37357@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com> <8q5dmo$jrk$1@kermit.esat.net> <39C742AD.455A32E9@mescoma.com> <39C7479C.9D01CABD@fujitsu-siemens.com> <XwTx5.458$TF3.272851@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> What I encounter is the stuff where the code started out with a situation
> where a simple IF is "right" (whatever that means),

From my simplistic perspective, IF is properly used for a strict A or B
(or A or nothing) situation. If it's an A, or B, or C, or ... situation
it's a case structure and I code an EVALUATE. This makes it abundantly
clear that the first match terminates the selection process, unlike
Foodman's columnar offering with periods (full stops).

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q9ait$85um$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C62B00.DAB455A@fujitsu-siemens.com> <8q5dmo$jrk$1@kermit.esat.net> <39C742AD.455A32E9@mescoma.com> <39C7479C.9D01CABD@fujitsu-siemens.com> <XwTx5.458$TF3.272851@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39C82A1F.EFC37357@optonline.net>`

```

I use this same criteria as a rule of thumb; I use an IF when there are only
2 outcomes, otherwise I prefer an EVALUATE for 3 or more.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** Jaime Rezola Clemente <rezola@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C667BC.79EFD3@mail.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
It was difficult for me to understand Sample 2 until I noticed the
period at the end. I sometimes work with a very old compiler and have to
use that type of code. However the way it works would be more like this:

If aField = "A"
   Perform A-rtn
Else
   If aField = "B"
      Perform B-rtn
   Else
      If aField = "C"
         Perform C-rtn
      End-If
   End-If
End-If

This way, there is no need for the period, which sometimes makes me
loose a lot of time debuging.

The point is that there is no Else-If in COBOL (at least the version I
am using).

I hope this helps the discussion.

Jaime Rezola Clemente
rezola@mail.telepac.pt

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I haven't seen any reply to my earlier note, so I wanted to re-post with
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q6080$rqs$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C667BC.79EFD3@mail.telepac.pt>`

```
I used the "period" (full-stop) variation instead of the END-IF variation -
because there are still '74 Standard compilers (and programmers using them)
where there is no END-IF available.  However, I would use MULTIPLE END-IF
delimiters if I were coding "nested" IFs with an '85 Standard compiler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C68C40.1B8F4786@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39C667BC.79EFD3@mail.telepac.pt> <8q6080$rqs$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I used the "period" (full-stop) variation instead of the END-IF variation -
> because there are still '74 Standard compilers (and programmers using them)
> where there is no END-IF available.  However, I would use MULTIPLE END-IF
> delimiters if I were coding "nested" IFs with an '85 Standard compiler.

I use END-IF everywhere I can, as I have no anticipation that I will ever go back
to Ansi '74 or Ansi '68 with my code.  But I also use full stop periods.  With my
pre compilers, they are necessary, and I think they solve more problems than they
cause, especially when used with indented code.

It would be nice to have a compiler option to warn with unmatched IF statements
though.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T02:49:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-PpQkH1WJHsep@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 18 Sep 2000 02:07:52, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> I haven't seen any reply to my earlier note, so I wanted to re-post with
> slightly different wording.
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> equivalent and WHY is the 3rd sample "logically different"?

Sample 1 (Evaluate) and Sample 2 (ELSE-IF construct) are logically
equivalent.

In the first two samples the contents of "aField) is evaluated prior
to any code performed that could possibly modify the contents 
of "aField"

The third sample is not logically equivalent because the
performed code code modify the contents of "aField" and
all three routines could be executed. 

The first two samples would only execute one of the three 
possible routines.

> 
> When you "know" (accept) the answer, you will see that NESTED "IF's" can be
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I prefer EVALUATE but that is only a personal preference. 

Do I get an "A" :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q46lr$46lg$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-PpQkH1WJHsep@tcpserver>`

```

I'd say an A- since, if aField is something other than A, B, or C, none of
the 3 routines gets performed!  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE vs IF

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C8E686.C15DBAB4@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<8q3teu$ag2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I haven't seen any reply to my earlier note, so I wanted to re-post with
> slightly different wording.
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The "answer would seem to be that in the third sample, all three IF's are
executed, whereas in the first two, only as many of the three are (i.e., if the
first CASE or IF is executed, then the second two aren't.  Etc.).


>
> When you "know" (accept) the answer, you will see that NESTED "IF's" can be
> coded to be logically equivalent to EVALUATE statements - but consecutive
> ones cannot be coded to GUARANTEE logical equivalence.
>

If "afield" happens to = "C" then the coding is logically equivalent at execution
time.
On a practical level, if the results are invariably the same, then I don't see
that we need to worry about formally rigourous definitions.

>
> Then the question becomes "which is more easily maintainable" nested IF's or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is a completely different issue.  If the code is well written, it will be
easy to maintain.  If it isn't, it won't be - regardless of whether one used
nested IF's or EVALUATE or any other construct.  I doubt if a case could be
invented that would unequivocably demonstrate that EVALUATE is easier to
maintain.

>

Cheers
PL
```

<a id="t-a0df87e883"></a>
## Thread: ASCII Character for the status bar — 4 msgs

### ASCII Character for the status bar

- **From:** "Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39c50898_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I am using GUI Screenio and have written a routine that is used when setting
up the data for the system. I am using an "*" as the status indicator
character. I would like to use the standard character that would be used in
normal windows programming (the one that looks like a solid tire track).
Does anybody know that ASCII code and font I need to use to accomplish this
task?
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII Character for the status bar

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39c6288a.7067506@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<39c50898_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
"Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net> wrote:

>I am using GUI Screenio and have written a routine that is used when setting
>up the data for the system. I am using an "*" as the status indicator
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Stephen:

The ASCII characters you are speaking of include:

ASCII 176 - light "tire track"
ASCII 177 - heavier "tire track"
ASCII 178 - reverse video "tire track"

The only problem is that I've only seen these characters in the IBM
extended character set in an MS-DOS character mode environment and not
in a Windows True Type font.

Perhaps someone else has been able to locate a True Type font
containing these characters.

The other problem you may experience if you do need to utilize a
special font is the fact that you may have to distribute and install
the special font on every machine on which your application must run.
If it is not a font distributed with the standard Windows
installation, you may have to do this.

If I may offer a suggestion, I would recommend using the Wingdings
font set and use an ASCII 112 (this is a lower case "p" in the normal
text, or default font).

That gives you a very nice looking box which can be used to accomplish
the same purpose.  Wingdings is also a font which is distributed with
every Windows installation, therefore you shouldn't have to worry
about distributing and installing any special fonts.

Good luck!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8q59e9$gpl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00

Actually about the only way is (you MUST know the DD) to call some routine
that executes a SVC99 (DYNAMIC ALLOCATION) requesting that SVC99 return
the DSN

OR
you can get someone to code a little 'C' or assembler routine that you pass
the DSN
(base name only), and that routine performs a 'LOCATE' which will expand (if
the flags
are set right) the fully qualified name.

^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
         MVC   CATDSN,DSNAME       INSERT DSNAME
09010000
         LOCATE SRCATLG            EXPAND IN CASE GDG
09020000
(DATA AREA BELOW)
(MACRO)
SRCATLG  CAMLST NAME,CATDSN,,CATLGLST
17520000

^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GDG help.

- **From:** jacksleight@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qlrg3$gte$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8q59e9$gpl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <aoHx5.10$TF3.9298@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <RZSx5.169$5p.1927@news2.mia>`

```
Hi,

Using COBOLII's pointer feature, you can access MVS control blocks to
get to the DSN.

e.g. CVT @ x'10'; CVT +0 => OLD/NEW TCB PTR;ONTP + 0 => TCB; TCB + 16
     => TIOT; TIOT + 24 => 1st DDENTRY. Find the one you want(loop).

That DDENTRY + 8 => JFCB; JFCB + 16 => DSN. GDG @ o/s 45 of DSN.

P.S.

The 1st byte of each entry contains the length of the entry unless
there are no more, then the 1st 4 bytes ore x'00'.

Open the file first, I think that makes the absolute GDG available.
If the DSN you're interested in is in a concatenation, additional
logic is necessary.

Hope this helps.

Jack




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **References:** `<8q59e9$gpl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <aoHx5.10$TF3.9298@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <RZSx5.169$5p.1927@news2.mia> <8qlrg3$gte$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6axz5.974$bK5.4387@news2.mia> <8qmb02$9tq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

On 24 Sep 2000 at 20:49, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
said:

>Which offset changed at which release level?  I could be mistaken, but I
>thought that "these" offsets were pretty stable since S/360 days.

The offsets haven't changed, it's the contents of some fields that may
have changed.  For example, the 24-bit JFCB address becomes an SVA (SWA
Virtual Address) when your job runs with SWA=ABOVE.  You then have to
convert the SVA to a 31-bit adddress, if you know how to do it, of course.

The SWA=ABOVE issue has been mentioned on this list previously.  Accessing
MVS (or VSE, or any OS) control blocks using the POINTER facility is often
doable, but you'd better know what you're getting yourself into before you
start relying on it.

gsf

>> because it relies upon fixed offsets.

## Thread: ENTRY points — 8 msgs

### ENTRY points

- **From:** "Mike Sheehan" <msheehan@pks.ie>

I've found a reference to multiple entry points to a sub-program that is not
part of the COBOL ISO Standard.
It does not look as it will be a part of the next standard either.

You can call an entry point in call statement e.g. CALL "entrypoint1"

Are entry points problematic in some way?

2ndly, how does the calling program know where the entry point is?
The sub-program name is not in the CALL. so does the CALL statement search a
library for a source that has that entry point name.
What if there are two sub-programs with the same entry point name?

I'm looking at how a COBOL 85 program could implement an interface in a
similar way as an Ada package (or Pascal Unit). it probably cannot be done
using COBOL85 syntax?

Mike

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ENTRY points

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39c6505b.8145773@news>`
- **References:** `<8q5e17$k21$1@kermit.esat.net> <39c645c5.54154493@207.126.101.100>`

```
You can define a program with multiple entry points, and it's quite
handy for some applications.  We use it to map our COBOL API calls to
Windows format API calls, like this:

PROGRAM-ID.  GSAPI.
.
.
ENTRY 'GX_CREATEWINDOWEX' USING arguments
.
GOBACK.
ENTRY 'GX'_UPDATEWINDOW' USING arguments
.
GOBACK.

and so on.

The nice thing is that all related entrypoints reside in a single
source (and object) module.  It makes maintenance easier because you
don't need to remember (or find) the one of a gazillion source files
you need, and because you can use the same WORKING-STORAGE and LINKAGE
SECTION data definitions from any/all of the entries in the module.

Another reason to use multiple entry points is that each entry has its
own argument list; because COBOL won't let you vary the number of
arguments you call a routine with, this is a way to have multiple
entries into a routine, with each one having a different number of
arguments.

As for how they're found when you CALL one of the alternate entry
points, if you call the entry point statically; e.g., CALL
'GX_CREATEWINDOWEX' the linker finds and resolves the called address.

If you call the routine dynamically, you must call the main entry
point first (the name of the .DLL), henceforth the OS or the COBOL
runtime will generally have access to other entrypoints in the DLL.

One thing you need to watch out for is that some compilers default to
converting static calls to dynamic calls.  Obviously this can cause
problems if you don't call the main entry first in your application.
Merant is one of these...

Clear as mud?

Kevin

#### ↳ Re: ENTRY points

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000918141637.11994.00000110@ng-fk1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8q5e17$k21$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
Mike writes...

>I've found a reference to multiple entry points to a sub-program that is not
>part of the COBOL ISO Standard.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>using COBOL85 syntax?

You didn't say what platform you're on, but I can answer your questions for the
OS/390 base.

Entry points are not problematic here, they are simply little known and, for
that reason, seldom used. But they are very useful.

In fact, mutliple entry points can accomplish almost everything done by DLLs in
a mainframe, and I think maybe the people bragging about DLLs being available
on the mainframe aren't even aware of alternative entry points.

Now, as far as a CALL making the connection to an alternate entry point, there
are some options:

1) Static calls
  a) if the caller calls the main entry point (program-id name) as well as an
alternate entry point, the alternate entry point can be found at link (bind)
time, since the subroutine load module is brought into memory for linking

  b) if the caller only class an alternate entry point then either:
      i) at link time of the calling program you need to explicitly INCLUDE the
         load module using a linkage editor control statement like
            INCLUDE DDNAME(PGMNAME)
         where PGMNAME is the name in the directory for the load module and
DDNAME
         is a name you make up; (you must supply this DD statement in the link
edit step,
         then; it points to the library (PDS or PDSE) containing the
subroutine's load module)
      or
      ii) at link time of the subroutine, build an ALIAS for the alternate
entry point by 
         including a link edit control statement like this:
            ALIAS DIRNAME(ENTPTNAME)
         where DIRNAME is the name you are specifying in your CALL and 
         ENTPTNAME is the name identified by an ENTRY statement in the COBOL
         program (they are normally the same); this puts an entry in the
directory
         of the load module library that points to the same load module as the
main,
         but indicates where the alternat entry point begins

2) Dynamic calls
    the only way a dynamic call of an alternate entry point can be resolved is
if
    you use option b.ii) above, set up an ALIAS when you link the subroutine

Note that you can have multiple alternate entry points, and multiple aliases.
Also, you can call alternate entry points across language boundaries.

Note also that people coding programs with alternate entry points need to make
sure any logic conflicts are allowed for and that details of what is going on
are clearly documented for subsequent maintenance programmers.

We cover this (and a whole lot more) in our class "Inter-Language Communication
in OS/390". Check http:www//trainersfriend.com for details (follow link to
complete course list to that course (course code M220 in our scheme)).

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: ENTRY points

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q5r1q$1g9$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8q5e17$k21$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
1) The ENTRY statement (and alternate entry points) are NOT a part of any
past or proposed ANSI/ISO COBOL Standard.

2) IBM COBOL has (and continues) to support them.  ENTRY point resolution is
USUALLY handled by implicit or explicit linkage editor/binder statements.
(Many compilers that provide IBM "emulation" also support them)

3) IBM does document some "gotchas" when you use them.  For example, you may
NOT call dynamically and statically the same entry point in the same
run-unit - without causing "potential" problems.

4) J4 and WG4 have consistently REJECTED adding them to Standard COBOL

5) If you are interested in writing "truly portable" COBOL, then you should
avoid their use.

6) If you use an "EVALUATE" (or comparable) statement at the beginning of
your Procedure Division and then use nested programs (or even
paragraphs/sections), then you can provide "comparable" functionality.

  ***
does this answer all the questions?
```

#### ↳ Re: ENTRY points

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <djacot@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q74g3$o3o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8q5e17$k21$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
I will not answer to your first questions since Steve described very
clearly what you have to do.
And I don't know of 'Ada package' and 'Pascal Unit', but if these are
about the same as a DLLs in Windows, then you could think about using
DLLs in OS/390 too. There is a restriction that you cannot mix dynamic
calls and DLL calls in the same program, but at least it is 'standard'
since no 'ENTRY' statement is needed.

So, IF you do not need to mix old and new programs AND you have the most
current compiler available (COBOL for OS/390 & VM Version 2.1) THEN you
can use DLL linkage (and mix COBOL, C, C++ and VA PL/I DLLs).

You produce DLLs like that:

        CBL DLL,PGMNAME(LONGMIXED),EXPORTALL
        Program-Id "First_Entry_of_my_DLL".
        ....
        ....
        End Program "First_Entry_of_my_DLL".

        Program-Id "Second_Entry_of_my_DLL".
        ....
        ....
        End Program "Second_Entry_of_my_DLL".

All the DLLs are in the same source file and they are NOT nested and
they cannot share global variables.

The Prelink-utility or, if you use PDSE, the Binder produces an 'IMPORT'
statement for each Program-Id you used (on a file with DDNAME
'SYSDEFSD').

You compile the calling program with the same compiler-options as you
used in the DLL (except EXPORTALL if the calling program is a main
program). And you add the generated 'IMPORT' commands to the SYSLIN of
the Prelinker- or the Binder-Input. That's all. And yes, you call the
entries like:
CALL "First_Entry_of_my_DLL" USING ....
No more restriction on 8 characters. That's fine, isn't it?


In article <8q5e17$k21$1@kermit.esat.net>,
  "Mike Sheehan" <msheehan@pks.ie> wrote:
> I've found a reference to multiple entry points to a sub-program that
is not
> part of the COBOL ISO Standard.
> It does not look as it will be a part of the next standard either.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The sub-program name is not in the CALL. so does the CALL statement
search a
> library for a source that has that entry point name.
> What if there are two sub-programs with the same entry point name?
>
> I'm looking at how a COBOL 85 program could implement an interface in
a
> similar way as an Ada package (or Pascal Unit). it probably cannot be
done
> using COBOL85 syntax?
>
> Mike
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ENTRY points

- **From:** "Mike Sheehan" <msheehan@pks.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q787t$ca5$1@kermit.esat.net>`
- **References:** `<8q5e17$k21$1@kermit.esat.net> <8q74g3$o3o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
fyi,
Ada packages are used to create Abstract Data Types...

An Ada package allows you to define a public interface that is separately
compilable from the implementation body...

The advantage is that you can have an public interface - stub in your
programs that declares the functions/procedures and the parameters
involved - and you can change the implementation without affecting the
calling program.
The package body is private, hidden, and so gives you encapsulation...

This is a very powerful facility as it allows for information hiding and
modularisation.
It would fall short of a class/object as inheritance is not directly
available, but, where, inheritance is not required, ideal...

My interest is in how COBOL85 could enable (if not support) such a package
concept, however, it should be far easier to implement in the next version
of the standard using OO facilities. The DLL approach seems a suitable
alternative...

Mike

package LeapYearPackage is

subtype year_type is integer range 1600..9999;
subtype day_number_type is integer range 0..366;

type day_of_week_type is (friday, saturday, sunday, monday,
                            tuesday, wednesday, thursday);

function isleap(year : year_type) return boolean;
function days_since_1600(year : year_type; day_this_year : day_number_type)
                     return integer;

function DayOfWeek(year : year_type; day_this_year : day_number_type)
                                          return day_of_week_type;
end LeapYearPackage;


With Ada.Text_IO, Ada.Integer_Text_IO;
use Ada.Text_IO, Ada.Integer_Text_IO;


package body LeapYearPackage is


function isleap(year : year_type) return boolean is
begin
-- Checks if year is a leap year
   if (year mod 4 = 0 and year mod 100 /= 0)
        or year mod 400 =0 then
       return TRUE;
   else
       return FALSE;
   end if;
end isleap;

function days_since_1600(year : year_type; day_this_year : day_number_type)
                                          return integer is
   diff_year, days_to_last_year, total_days : integer;
 begin
   diff_year := year - year_type'first;
   days_to_last_year := (diff_year * 365) + (diff_year / 4) -
                 (diff_year / 100) + (diff_year / 400) + 1;
                          -- 1 for the leap year 1600!!!!!!!!
   total_days := days_to_last_year + day_this_year;
   return total_days;
end days_since_1600;

function DayOfWeek(year : year_type; day_this_year : day_number_type)
                                          return day_of_week_type is

    day_of_week : day_of_week_type; -- 0 is friday
   total_days : integer;
begin
  total_days := days_since_1600(year,day_this_year);
  day_of_week :=  day_of_week_type'val(total_days mod 7);
  return day_of_week;
--  return day_of_week_type'val(total_days mod 7);

end  DayOfWeek ;

end LeapYearPackage;

<a id="t-042ffa57ff"></a>
## Thread: Help: Need to get COBOL alphanumeric (binary) data into SQL Server 7.0 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help: Need to get COBOL alphanumeric (binary) data into SQL Server 7.0

- **From:** chipling@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** microsoft.public.sqlserver.programming,comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.sqlserver.migration
- **Message-ID:** `<39C68BF7.9409FCF3@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<39B02C31.55640E2A@eaglesoft.com>`

```
Jeff,

Different vendor using different way to store their binary data. Two things you
need to know before you can directly load your data to SQL server.

First is to identify the data definition on the COBOL program for that particular
field. I suspect it will be something like PIC 9(?)V99 COMP.

Second, you need to contact the cobol compiler company (or read thru their manual)
and find out how they convert that numeric data to binary form.

Rgds,
Chip Ling

P.S. If your COBOL program can directly read in the data, why not using the same
data definition in COBOL and write out a text file and use that file to import to
the SQL server.

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Help: Need to get COBOL alphanumeric (binary) data into SQL Server 7.0

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** microsoft.public.sqlserver.programming,comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.sqlserver.migration
- **Message-ID:** `<v2vtOUAtOzx5Iw7z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<39B02C31.55640E2A@eaglesoft.com> <39C68BF7.9409FCF3@hotmail.com>`

```
>>   How does one get binary data into SQL using AcuCOBOL and SQL
>Server 7.0?
>> All help is appreciated.
>>

Jeff,

You could write the data directly from your Acucobol-GT program into the 
database if that would be easier. This can be achieved in one of two 
ways. Either through the use of embedded SQL, or by the use of Acu4GL 
which would allow you to use standard COBOL I/O syntax (WRITE, etc.) to 
update the SQL Server table.

Other than that I'm afraid I'm not sure what's causing your problem.

Feel free to contact me at <neaton@acucorp.com> (don't reply to this 
email please, it's my home account and doesn't get checked all that 
often). I'll try to put you in touch with the appropriate person.

Best regards

Nigel
```

array.  I've also written a c program that calls my cobol dll in which my
cobol program returns the address pointer to my array.  Yet when I go to
access that spot in memory I get an error access denied

           ENTRY 'GetStringPassed' dynamicNTStdCallPlus2
           set my-pointer to address of stack-array.
           EXIT PROGRAM RETURNING my-pointer.
           STOP RUN.

C Code:
( Note I'm hard coding the pointer value.)
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void)
{
  long *ptr, i;
 void *d = NULL;

Tom Wright

#### ↳ Re: for the well versed

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q86mr$vq1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<FStx5.352$b64.51633@news1.primary.net>`

```
Tom,

Assuming Win NT as OS you can't access memory allocated from another
program in that way. It's not "shared". NT always keeps a look at who
is the owner of data in mem. That's why you get an access denied error.

It is very easy to code something in that way to get access violation
errors. You will get the same result using "every" language.


HTH - Stefan Meyer

In article <FStx5.352$b64.51633@news1.primary.net>,
  "Tom Wright" <twright@@larimore.net> wrote:
> I'm writing a cobol program.  I'm going to compile it into a dll. In
my
> program I have an array setup.  I also have a pointer set to address
of the
> array.  I've also written a c program that calls my cobol dll in
which my
> cobol program returns the address pointer to my array.  Yet when I go
to
> access that spot in memory I get an error access denied
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

- **Date:** 2000-09-18T00:00:00

- **References:** `<8fgsno$9pb$1@news.mclink.it> <391C63D6.B8A334F4@home.com>`

```
I am mostly familiar with VMS.  There, you can compile with MAP
option, specifying /map=(alpha,declared) which will give two
dictionaries of data elements, the first in alphabetic order and the
second in declared order.  Add /CROSS for a cross-reference, i.e.
$ cob/copy/list/cross/map=(declared, alpha) myprog

The "copy" option expands copylib inclusions.

and did not fix my problem.

Glen

I am trying to add an IF statement to my program for CIS160 class when i try to
do what i think is right i get this error message IF statement located where
imperative statement must be specified.

I am stuck (I think i made a big mess!!!!) here is the section of code:

200-PROCESSING.
     PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = 'Y'
         READ INVENTORY-FILE
	       AT END
		   MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-FLAG
         NOT AT END
         IF IR-QTY-ON-HAND < IR-REORDER-POINT  
         ADD 1 TO WS-COUNT-2
	       ELSE
	       
	   DISPLAY IR-ITEM-NUMBER, ' ', IR-ITEM-DESCRIPTION
	       END-READ
     END-PERFORM


Thanks,
Deek
what would i do without all of you nice people?
```

#### ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000920101451.24107A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
In structured programming a technique that works is to have a read
paragraph that does nothing but read and set the flag to 'N' at end of the
file.  Then perform the read Paragraph once in the initialization routine.
Then have a process that is in a paragraph and perform that paragraph
until end-flag = 'y'.  In the process paragraph you have a perform read at
the end of the process.  when the read changes the end-flag to 'y' then
the process ends and EOJ is run.

mainline logic
  perform initialization-routine.
  perform process-routine until end-flag = 'y'.
  perform EOJ-routine.


Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

On 20 Sep 2000, Deek40 wrote:

> I am trying to add an IF statement to my program for CIS160 class when i try to
> do what i think is right i get this error message IF statement located where
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
On Wed, 20 Sep 2000 10:25:03 -0500, Charles Hammond wrote:

>In structured programming a technique that works is to have a read
>paragraph that does nothing but read and set the flag to 'N' at end of the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
What I always do is to define a FILE STATUS field and check for a status of
'10':
The logic stays the same, but I don't have to initialize and to set the flag
(I don't like flags)

mainline logic
  perform initialization-routine.
  perform process-routine until statusfield = '10' ( or an 88 level)
  perform EOJ-routine.


Just my opinion
Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kcvgss03i2oo5n5oaloj9be8auok7d4rtj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
On 20 Sep 2000 01:32:22 GMT, deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:
Deek,

you have an IF statement inside a NOT END part of a READ.  This is not allowable here,
because you forgot to terminate the IF with an END-IF, that is the conditional IF range
overlaps the READ - END-READ Range.  Adding an END-IF will make the CONDITIONAL IF into an
IMPERATIVE IF

HTH



>I am trying to add an IF statement to my program for CIS160 class when i try to
>do what i think is right i get this error message IF statement located where
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>what would i do without all of you nice people?

     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

```

#### ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C8B6B5.FB9241DC@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
Two possible fixes:
1.  Add an END-IF  (It is not obvious that this changes the code to an imperative,
but try it).
2.  Add another PERFORM containing the IF (This is more obvious).

Of course, if you chose PERFORM, you are getting some serious nesting, and you
might want to move some of this code to its own paragraph.  If the compiler can get
confused (see fix #1), certainly maintenance programmers can get confused.

I would also work on aligning my code to make it easier to see which nested level
you are in.


Deek40 wrote:

> I am trying to add an IF statement to my program for CIS160 class when i try to
> do what i think is right i get this error message IF statement located where
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> what would i do without all of you nice people?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
as the compiler says, you cannot have IF statements
between a READ and an END-READ.

news:20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com...

try to

where

>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q972n$aqq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com> <XRUx5.18049$3y3.234544@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Correct - but misleading.  Change (indentation shows the correction - but
isn't required)

        READ INVENTORY-FILE
        AT END
            MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-FLAG
         NOT AT END
           IF IR-QTY-ON-HAND < IR-REORDER-POINT
              ADD 1 TO WS-COUNT-2
           ELSE
              DISPLAY IR-ITEM-NUMBER, ' ', IR-ITEM-DESCRIPTION
        END-READ

to

        READ INVENTORY-FILE
        AT END
            MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-FLAG
         NOT AT END
           IF IR-QTY-ON-HAND < IR-REORDER-POINT
              ADD 1 TO WS-COUNT-2
           ELSE
              DISPLAY IR-ITEM-NUMBER, ' ', IR-ITEM-DESCRIPTION
            End-If
        END-READ

(adding that END-IF changes the "IF" statement from a conditional to
imperative statement)
```

There are some who don't like ANY homework questions submitted to the NG.
There are others who will give you exact answers (especially when you show
your code and the error message - as you did) and there are some who will try
and "give you a hint" on where to look.

I usually do the latter - but in this case, I couldn't think of where to
"point you to" in your text/course material that wouldn't take me a WHOLE lot
more effort than showing you where your error was.

Hopefully, you now (better) understand the difference between conditional and
imperative statements - and not just what was wrong in this case.

In the future, don't be surprised if some people tell you to "do your own
homework" - however, if that seems to be the ONLY response(s) that you get,
it probably means that you haven't shown us enough "work of your own".
```

#### ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8cissgb5rggt21tuug73s043el1242e32@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
On 20 Sep 2000 01:32:22 GMT deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:

:>I am trying to add an IF statement to my program for CIS160 class when i try to
:>do what i think is right i get this error message IF statement located where
:>imperative statement must be specified.

:>I am stuck (I think i made a big mess!!!!) here is the section of code:

:>200-PROCESSING.
:>     PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = 'Y'
:>         READ INVENTORY-FILE
:>	       AT END
:>		   MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-FLAG
:>         NOT AT END
:>         IF IR-QTY-ON-HAND < IR-REORDER-POINT  
:>         ADD 1 TO WS-COUNT-2
:>	       ELSE
:>	       
:>	   DISPLAY IR-ITEM-NUMBER, ' ', IR-ITEM-DESCRIPTION
:>	       END-READ
:>     END-PERFORM

You have to convert to IF to an imperative statement.

The sequences

      IF .......
      ELSE .....
      END-IF

or

      READ  .....
      AT END  ......
      NOT AT END ......
      END-READ

or
        
      ADD  ......

are imperative in the sense that paths join and this unit of code is one
logical statement.  

The sequences

      ADD  ......
      ON SIZE ERROR

or
     
      IF   .......
      ELSE  ......

are not imperative because the clause is hanging until you get a period for
closure.  
```

#### ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qfdu8$fel$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com>,
  deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:
> I am trying to add an IF statement to my program for CIS160 class
when i try to
> do what i think is right i get this error message IF statement
located where
> imperative statement must be specified.
>
> I am stuck (I think i made a big mess!!!!) here is the section of
code:
>
> 200-PROCESSING.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Deek

Hi:

I don't know why I am the only one to say this, and I will
probably get yelled at for saying it, but:

You really need to test the FILE-STATUS after every I-O operation
in your program. Without the FILE-STATUS test, and appropriate
actions based on the test, your programs can not be totally dependable.
Putting a FILE-STATUS test and appropriate messages in your example
would change it considerably. You also need to be able to determine if
the data file has no records or only one record in it. Why your
instructor does not make you aware of this, I can't imagine.
There are a few dozen FILE-STATUS codes, see your manual. If you
do not make the test, and an error occurs, your program would
probably produce unpredictable results or crash or something.

If you have the time and the inclination, you might try writing
it in a non-structured way (although your instructor would
probably faint).

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qfik4$ueq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com> <8qfdu8$fel$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have NOTHING against adding/using File-status codes, HOWEVER, there are two
things that I would say about their use in this case:

1) There could be a DECLARATIVE, in which case there would be NO need for
file-status code checking "in-line"

2) In *most* cases, if you have checked the file-status after an OPEN *and*
you are doing a sequential READ statement *and* you check for "AT END" - then
it is probably (possible?) that you really DO want your program to "abend"
(die, abnormally terminate) if any other file-status occurs. (I can think of
some unusual exceptions to this, but I also know of many "production"
applications that intentionally work this way.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IF statement question

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T02:31:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000922223155.23666.00000319@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8qfik4$ueq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I personally haven't used DECLARATIVE. Guess I'm one of those that isn't too
clear on its use.  I also don't use FILE-STATUS on QSAM (aka flat files) but do
use them on VSAM (aka ISAM on pc's?).  Also GSAMs (although I haven't used a
GSAM in about 7 years).  

In our shop if a VSAM file doesn't do what you want it to do you display the
status, the key, the operation, and set the return code to stop processing.  In
all other cases yes we want a nice big messy abend so the operators will notice
what is going on :)  (At least we hope they do - I've gotten calls 3 hrs after
an abend has occured because someone wasn't paying attention).

We do have a few programs that use the invalid key clause on a flat file (which
means dang thing ran out of space) that display a message, places a 0 in the
return code, and then a stop run.  This in turn causes all sorts of headaches
in the morning when people start calling asking where the rest of the data is. 
We rip out that coding everytime we have to maintenance one of these cuties or
when we get bit in the posterior.

File-status has it's place as does an abend.  

Eileen
```

A feller just told me a good'un t'other day... just happened last week.
Back in February there was... *something* that happened in one of these
ninety-year old, hundred-and-forty-three step, two-files-in,
VSAM-and-a-report-out, twelve-*billion*-&&TEMPnn-files jobs.

(note to the reader: if that made no sense there's no need to go on.  then
again... if that *did* make sense - gave a kind of 'I know *those*!'
tingle - then you know how the story turns out... and there's no need to
go on)

Anyhow, this feller determined that somewhere, somehow, deep in the bowels
of this mess two files being matched/merged got out of sync because
someone didn't code an error routine for a not-found condition on a
key-read to (another) VSAM for a name-field lookup... or something like
that.  He then coded a *whomper* (a whomper? a gen-yoo-wine *whomper*!) of
an error routine, complete with a full-page-sized 'ERROR' with each large
letter made of identical component letters... the classic, right?  He
codes that baby up, gets it slapped into Prod... and hears nothing about
it.

Seven months later... seven months later he gets called into the Corner
Office where his boss's boss is demanding an explanation as to why this
error is showing up again.  The feller points to the SYSOUT spread across
the desk and shows the 'ERROR' page - which also contains instructions for
fixing the error, whom to notify and restarting the job - and says that
this situation has been encountered and here are the instructions for
dealing with it.

Both his boss and his boss's boss say 'Hmmmmm... is that what that means?
We checked out the SAR (archived) outputs for this job for the past four
months and every one of them had a page like this... we thought it was
normal.'

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IF statement question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

I've noticed the ABEND is far and away the weapon of choice to wake up the
operators.

Ideologically, I'm not nuts about using an ABEND to report problems which
could be handled with FILE STATUS or DECLARATIVES and come to a "soft"
landing; however, unless and until someone comes up with a better way to
"raise the problem flag," I guess we'll be doing this for a long time to
come.
```

##### ↳ ↳ I/O failures, FILE-STATUS & aborts

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CB5F89.55EFFABA@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com> <8qfdu8$fel$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

> I don't know why I am the only one to say this, and I will
> probably get yelled at for saying it, but:

Possibly - but I don't consider this a yell...


> You really need to test the FILE-STATUS after every I-O operation
> in your program. Without the FILE-STATUS test, and appropriate
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> probably produce unpredictable results or crash or something.

In what environments do such errors produce unpredictable results?

There are conditions where a crash is a perfectly acceptable result of a
failed I/O command, as long as the system gives you the information needed
to easily trace down the error.  If the system does NOT give that
information, it is necessary to do it yourself (check the file status,
display the information, and THEN crash on purpose).  Or, as you say, when
such an error would produce unpredictable results.  Or when you have some
alternative desired behavior.

The "crash on purpose" is sometimes necessary to make sure the operators
notice and take appropriate action.  You don't code that way on an
interactive program.  I am unfamiliar with non-interactive CoBOL programs
on personal computers - does anybody do these here?  How do you handle
abort situations?
```

> In what environments do such errors produce unpredictable results?

Hi:

Windows.

>
> There are conditions where a crash is a perfectly acceptable result
of a
> failed I/O command, as long as the system gives you the information
needed
> to easily trace down the error.  If the system does NOT give that
> information, it is necessary to do it yourself (check the file status,
> display the information, and THEN crash on purpose).  Or, as you say,
when

some

Can't you TELL the operator something is wrong and DEMAND their
acknowledgement? BTW, is 'the system' the operating system or
the application system?


 You don't code that way on an
> interactive program.  I am unfamiliar with non-interactive CoBOL
programs
> on personal computers - does anybody do these here?  How do you handle

the user specify the content). If an error is encountered, the user
is notified and MUST acknowledge the error.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I/O failures, FILE-STATUS & aborts

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qjaf7$ksj$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com> <8qfdu8$fel$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39CB5F89.55EFFABA@brazee.net> <8qi30t$h7q$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I don't know about other mainframes, but to explain to those who are NOT
familiar with IBM's MVS (aka OS/390), let me BRIEFLY explain *why* an ABEND
can be "better" than a clean "error report".

In "batch" jobs, it is possible to "group" a bunch of different steps (each
with its own program) into a "single run" (or job).  The way that IBM JCL
works, it is possible to tell the operating system WHAT to do with files
after EITHER a successful or an unsuccessful (ABEND) in that step.   OFTEN
you tell the system to "catalog" a new file if the step is successful but to
"delete" it if the step ABENDs.  Therefore, if you issue a "nice error
report" (even if someone pays attention to it) it will be marked as
"available" to the next Step in the same job (and possibly even other jobs) -
while if you INTENTIONALLY let/tell the application to ABEND (when there is a
problem), then no future step will "try" and work with this "bad" file.

I know of NO "comparable" methodology on PC's (well you could SORT-of emulate
it with batch or command files - but it would certainly be "unusual").
Therefore, what is GOOD methodology in one environment is bad in others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I/O failures, FILE-STATUS & aborts

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CD04DA.C76535B7@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<20000919213222.11198.00000096@ng-fx1.aol.com> <8qfdu8$fel$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39CB5F89.55EFFABA@brazee.net> <8qi30t$h7q$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
One of the major environments where abends make sense as a termination
is main frame batch.  They also make sense in certain database
environments because they trigger rollback procedures (IMS is one of
them).  In batch abnormal termination can trigger certain clean-up
procedures and interface with job scheduling systems.  (One of the major
IBM mainframe utilities is a two instruction utility that sets the
return code to zero and returns to caller thus activating pre-call
allocation services and post-call termination services).  Thus the
system default of abend on I-O error for batch can be adequate.  In the
programs I have written I have defaulted to standard handling for
sequential files and used status code checking for random access and
direct access files.  Note the programs have status code checked all I-O
operations with status code defined.  I also normally have only one
instance of each verb for each file (only 1 OPEN for each type of OPEN,
only 1 READ, only 1 READ NEXT, etc.) and if there was more than 1
logical place to execute the I-O, the I-O was performed.  Status code
checking was also done for sequential files where the standard error
default was not the action desired.

I have not used declaratives in any of the programs I have written for
the past 30 years.  The reason is that DECLARATIVES would allow me to do
anything that status code checking wouldn't let me do.  

For CICS I have inherited programs that established HANDLE conditions
for I-O exceptions and others that used the response codes.  I have
wished for the time and authorization to eliminate all use of HANDLE
conditions except HANDLE program abend and other events not triggered by
CICS calls.  A HANDLE condition is sort of a resetable DECLARATIVE with
all sorts of potential for unexpected consequences if not established
properly for all generic conditions and not fully reset before each
specific CICS call (EXEC CICS verb).

I would like to see a generic DECLARATIVE - USE ON INITIAL ENTRY and I
will look into the exception handling in the next standard.  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, cfmtech@istar.ca    

Foodman wrote:
> 
> > In what environments do such errors produce unpredictable results?
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

Not easily.  Display upon console has been disabled in most shops for
decades.  And standard aborts ARE noticed and acted upon.

> BTW, is 'the system' the operating system or the application system?

Both and more.  Such products as CA7 which schedule jobs with dependencies
also understand standard aborts.


>  You don't code that way on an
> > interactive program.  I am unfamiliar with non-interactive CoBOL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> is notified and MUST acknowledge the error.

That's the main criterion - all such errors MUST be responded to.  In my
environment, the surest way to do this is via a standard abort.  I don't
know how you do this with PC batch jobs.  I suppose it isn't common to have
scheduling software.
```

In article <39D209C1.924FC0FF@brazee.net>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
writes:

>
>How does this guarantee that the next dependent job in the schedule
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am not too familiar with how things would work in the Windows environment,
but I think that the DOS level BAT files were able to check the ERRORLEVEL
which (I think) is the equivalent to the IBM Mainframe RETCODE.
If the people setting up the BAT files were concerned about proper EOJ/ABEND
type closing status , edits would be placed in the BAT file for ERRORLEVEL
==0 GOTO GOODEOJ or fall thru to GOTO :BADEOJ

<bat file>
<drive>:\<path>\<program>
if errorlevel ==0 goto :GOODEOJ
goto :BADEOJ
:GOODEOJ
echo next program step to run
goto :EXIT
:BADEOJ
echo <program> ABENDed : reload and try again
goto :EXIT
:EXIT
exit
```

NOT AT END is an imperative statement....meaning that NOT AT END can
only be followed by some action (ie. MOVE, etc) not a conditional
expression like IF.

## Thread: COBOL Parsers/Compilers — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### COBOL Parsers/Compilers

- **From:** "Fishfan1" <fishfan1@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q8ma4$dsr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi

I am currently developing lexical and syntax parsers for COBOL.  I have
taken the 'Look Behind' approach and have heard that the 'Look Ahead'
approach is better from a performance point of view.  I would like to know
if anyone has knowledge and/or experience with the look behind approach.
Putting aside performance issues, can the look behind approach work?

Also, would like to communicate with anyone having knowledge and/or
experience developing COBOL parsers/compilers.

Thanks!

Fish
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers/Compilers

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ssg00eiduuev93@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8q8ma4$dsr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Build COBOL parsers is hard, with all the squirrely conventions about column
breaks,
continuation marks, literals-split-across "card" boundaries, goofball syntax
rules...
and ... the dreaded preprocessor.

We have a huge amount of effort in ours (commerical offering) to make useful
and reliable.
I'm sure the COBOL compiler boys will say the same.    You sure you want to
do this?

FWIW, we don't use a "look behind technique" (at least, I'm not familiar
with the definition).
Instead, we use a generalized LR(1) parser that forks when it hits local
ambiguities.
Forked parsers that can't make progress silently die.   No remaining live
parsers --> syntax fault.
Implementing this is really a pain, but then coding a COBOL grammer for this
is
much easier.   The performance is fine; local ambiguities are relatively
rare.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers/Compilers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C8B4E5.6BA063C1@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8q8ma4$dsr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <ssg00eiduuev93@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"Ira D. Baxter" wrote:

> Build COBOL parsers is hard, with all the squirrely conventions about column
> breaks,
> continuation marks, literals-split-across "card" boundaries, goofball syntax
> rules...
> and ... the dreaded preprocessor.

I hadn't thought about this.  I bet many pre-compilers will require source code
to keep to a common format for a while.  When a compiler adds a new command,
that usually doesn't break a pre-compiler.  When a compiler has a new way of
implementing comments, or defining bounds, that certainly would require a
pre-compiler rewrite.

My pre-compiler creates a field named RECORD-NAME, causing an informational
message that this is a reserved word.  But it works anyway.
```

```

> I am currently developing lexical and syntax parsers for COBOL
> Also, would like to communicate with anyone having knowledge and/or
> experience developing COBOL parsers/compilers.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers/Compilers

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-09-20T11:52:03+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39C80A33.5DD4A893@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8q8ma4$dsr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I'm not really sure what you mean by "look behind". In
CobolForGCC we are using bison which is an LALR(1) look ahead
compiler. It needs considerable help to parse COBOL, due to the
fact that COBOL is not LALR(1). You can see the results at
http://CobolForGCC.sourceforge.net (browse the CVS source into
the gcc/cobol directory; there is a discussion of the help the
parser needs in the file cobctok.def). We have the grammar for
the COBOL85 nucleus done - the other parts of COBOL do not
present any unique problems.

There was a long discussion about parsing COBOL under the GNU
COBOL project at
http://www.lusars.net/maillists/gnu-cobol/threads.html. Do a find
on "pars" or "yacc". Learn from our mistakes.

I looked at numerous techniques for parsing COBOL. The hacks
needed to make BISON/YACC work are only a few hundred lines of
code, so I gave away notions of backtracking compilers etc, for
reasons which are explained in the mailing list discussion.
Basically backtracking makes creating reasonable error messages
very difficult.

As the GNAT (ADA) compiler crew showed, you can write a fast
parser with good error messages by hand as long as writing
several hundred thousand lines of extra code does not bother you.

But really parsing is a miniscule fraction of building a COBOL
compiler. Code generation and optimisation and the runtime
libraries are a much larger task.

Tim Josling

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

Fishfan1 schrieb:
> 
> Hi
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Putting aside performance issues, can the look behind approach work?

Sire, are you joking?

I never heard of a "look-behind" approach - that reminds
me of the infamous "comefrom" statement of the
Babbage Language ( see http://www.tlc-systems.com/babbage.htm
and many other places on the Web )

Mmh - maybe a Parser which learns from mistakes can
be called a Look-Behind-Parser? - And Look-Behind for
how many Symbols?

> Also, would like to communicate with anyone having knowledge and/or
> experience developing COBOL parsers/compilers.

So am I. However, developing Syntax Analysis for only a
small part of the language may leave one out of touch
with the rest of Cobol. I am, at the moment, implementing
the Conditional Compilation for Cobol2007, and it
has nothing to do with the Syntax Analysis of
expressions already present in that particular
compiler since eons.

But that is a problem common to many pieces
of legacy software which have been in service
for decades.

Herwig
```

<a id="t-0b05e7d034"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu 4 to 5 upgrade warning. (PC versions) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu 4 to 5 upgrade warning. (PC versions)

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8q85ee$5ik$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I just found a bug in one of my systems that came up and grabbed us as a
result of upgrading from Fujitsu 4 to 5.  I though that I would post it in
here and possibly save some people some time.

Fujitsu version 4 will not allow spaces in filenames.  In fact, a space is
considered a delimiter, so assigning the name "C:\XX.DAT YY.DAT" will quite
happily work with the file "C:\XX.DAT".

Fujitsu version 5 will allow spaces in filenames.  The same code running on
the same machine will look for the filename "XX.DAT YY.DAT", including the
space and the two dots.  If opened in output, it will CREATE the file
"XX.DAT".  However, if you then try to open the same file with the same
filename in I/O or input mode, you will get a status 35.  Somewhat
confusing, to say the least.

I got bitten on it through a subroutine call with mismatched parameters.
The main program was seeing an X(40) id, while the subroutine was seeing an
X(80) id, with other data in the second group of 40.  Version 4 did not
care.  Version 5 does.

Donald
```

To convert in ascii try :
runcob recover2 
then 
Index file : <type the name of the file to convert> (es. ARK)
Drop file  : <type a file name> (es. ARK.seq)
Option : NOSUB

In VB you must read ARK.seq.

RC.
