<a id="t-caea0b8078"></a>
## Thread: Merant Server Express File Corruption — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Merant Server Express File Corruption

- **From:** bonniepeel@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qftc4$22d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello.
   We are running Merant (formerly Micro Focus) Server Express 1.0,
fixpack level 4, on AIX, hardware an IBM 12-way S80.  We are using all
IDX8-format files, and have been experiencing periodic index corruption.
Most types of corruption have been recoverable using rebuild, but
several weeks ago we experienced corruption where the file header said
the file was 10 gigabytes when the file was actually 8 gigabytes, and
rebuild could not fix this type of corruption.  Merant has been notified
and is investigating, but I wanted to find out if others are using this
version of COBOL and have experienced periodic file corruption, and if
anyone has theories as to the causes.  So far we have been unable to
recreate the corruption in our testing.
    I'd also be interested in other issues people have found with Server
Express IDX8 files.

Thanks.
Bonnie


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-7b9ed933fd"></a>
## Thread: A simple exercise — 4 msgs

### A simple exercise

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qi5g5$jhg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```



Hi:

With FILE-STATUS versus DECLARATIVES being the warm topic of the
day, I thought people might do an exercise.

Take any old program which reads a file.

Recompile the program changing the record format somehow.

Remove DECLARATIVES and all FILE-STATUS checks.

Run the program and see what happens. And tell us about it.

THanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: A simple exercise

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qi7hc$lpe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8qi5g5$jhg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

> Run the program and see what happens. And tell us about it.
>

Hi:

OK, I've got nothing better to do.

Since I have never written a program without FILE-STATUS in it,
I didn't know quite what to expect.

It terminated with a file-status of 139. (Inconsistent format)

On the other hand, I have experienced strange things with
inconsistent formats which did not produce any errors - program
appeared to run yet produced no output.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: A simple exercise

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CF6101.754FE4F7@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8qi5g5$jhg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

> Hi:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Run the program and see what happens. And tell us about it.

Typically nothing would happen (provided you have appropriate AT-END
logic).  These checks are to allow you to handle abnormal conditions.
To test, one needs to supply those abnormal conditions, but one can't
test EVERY abnormal condition.

Sometimes the environment handles most abnormal conditions just nicely -
in this case it might be appropriate to let it.  Sometimes the
programmer wants to handle particular conditions (and then making sure
it aborts normally with unanticipated conditions).

For instance, in a mainframe environment, if a file is protected when a
batch program tries to write to it, or if it has used up the space
allocated for it when you try to write - we usually abort - which is
sufficient to notify the operator and stop dependent jobs from running.
The operator looks at the abort message, then corrects the problem and
follows restart procedures.

In an on-line environment, the user needs to get a message explaining
what is wrong.  If the user can't fix it himself, he  typically makes a
screen print or calls system support with the information required.  If
a Windows based CoBOL program gets a "file not found" error, is it
difficult to pull up Windows routines to locate the appropriate file and
retry?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A simple exercise

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qpv2i$6gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8qi5g5$jhg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39CF6101.754FE4F7@brazee.net>`

```
If a Windows based CoBOL program gets a "file not found" error, is it
difficult to pull up Windows routines to locate the appropriate file and
retry?

Hi:

I think that with SP2, you can get access to the directory. Presumably,
you could then go through the file-names provided looking for
what you wanted. I have never tried this.

In a real application, I don't think you would want to go
searching for a file with the right name, it might have the
right name but the wrong content.

BTW, the reason I made this thread was primarily for Deek, et al.
My impression is that newcomers don't get taught much about
File-Status checks and what can happen in the real world.

I spent some time playing around doing things like moving the
.DAT file from one file to the .DAT file for another. Sometimes,
you get an AT-END, sometimes, the program would read the file
with no problem although the application might bomb out with
a 163 (non-numeric data).

My point is that file-handlers, although very good, are not
totally infallible and that newcomers should be aware of that
fact.

In addition to my obsession with checking file-status, I also
sequence-check sequential files. Years ago, I remember asking
someone to always sequence-check the files they were reading.
One guy refused to do this saying that the file just got sorted
so why bother sequence checking it. Anyway, (this was in the
days when JCL was on cards) one day, they had a jam and a
card containing the sort parameters was incorrectly made over.
This resulted in the sort being incorrect. It also resulted
in the guy's program breaking control on every record with
the result that it skipped to a new page each time. The
oprerators finally realized something was wrong after they
loaded the eleventh box of three-part paper.

Newcomers should not assume that things will be the way that
they are supposed to be because the time will come when they
are not.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-507b72c62e"></a>
## Thread: File Buffers on MVS — 11 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T01:12:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
IBM COBOL / OS390 / IBM 9672 CPU.

I have a COBOL program that has been running in PROD for over 20 years.  It
just failed the other day with a S0C7.  Here's what the code looks like:

FD  THIS-FILE
    RECORDING MODE F etc etc.
01  THIS-JUNK     PIC 9(1).


OPEN THIS-FILE.
READ THIS-FILE
...do some more stuff.

DISPLAY THIS-JUNK.
CLOSE THIS-FILE.
DISPLAY THIS-JUNK.

MOVE THIS-JUNK   TO... a comp field.



After putting in the displays, I found the record buffer was filling with
junk after the CLOSE.  The first display showed the correct value.  Now, I
know all about how this is bad code, but regardless, it's been running for
20 years and just now decides to fail.  The load module has been untouched
for almost a year.

What is wrong?  Something in MVS?  Did the Systems group change something?
What changed?

Help!
Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qj9td$31e$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
I agree with all the comments about NOT accessing the "record area" after a
CLOSE, but isn't there ALSO a rule about the *minimum" size of a file under
MVS (OS/390)?  I couldn't find it quickly, but I have some VAGUE memory that
IBM doesn't support files (or some types of files) with LRECL=1.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CD6C50.DD734CAA@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com> <8qj9td$31e$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I was surprised that they didn't get a S0C4 - protection exception or an
addressing exception with access of a file buffer after CLOSE.  At one
time either tape or disk files had to have at least 18 bytes worth of
data per block if my admittedly faulty memory is correct.  Since this
presumably was working, I wonder if the number of records in the file
changed so that a data area that used to have valid format data left
over from something else no longer contained valid format data.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca 

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I agree with all the comments about NOT accessing the "record area" after a
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Jeff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qmgvj02lds@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com> <8qj9td$31e$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <39CD6C50.DD734CAA@istar.ca>`

```
The PIC X(1) was just for example.  The record contained more bytes than 
that.

I moved the CLOSE to the end of the program and it works again.

I always use the READ INTO and WRITE FROM constructs.  That is one of the
first things I learned about coding in COBOL.

So I guess we've just been lucky up to this point.  The job doesn't run
every day, but a few times a year.  But still, that's quite a bit of luck...

Thanks,
Jeff

----------
In article <39CD6C50.DD734CAA@istar.ca>, "Clark F. Morris, Jr."
<cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:


> I was surprised that they didn't get a S0C4 - protection exception or an
> addressing exception with access of a file buffer after CLOSE.  At one
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g1ci4d0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
On Sat, 23 Sep 2000 01:12:08 -0400, Jeff Baynard wrote:

>IBM COBOL / OS390 / IBM 9672 CPU.
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jeff
Jeff,

There is nothing wrongand nothing has changed, , but before an open of a
file, and after a close, the contents of a file buffer (the 01 in the FD) is
UNDEFINED. That means that everything is possible. Sometimes you'll have the
same values as before the close, BUT YOU NEVER CAN BE SURE. So don't touch
the buffer before open and after close, and you are safe. If you want to
maintain the last record, just use READ INTO and WRITE FROM and you always
can see your record in WORKING STORAGE.

Hope this helps

Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1g2z5.832$N86.31024@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com...
> IBM COBOL / OS390 / IBM 9672 CPU.
>
> I have a COBOL program that has been running in PROD for over 20 years.
It
> just failed the other day with a S0C7.  Here's what the code looks like:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> What is wrong?

Data in the buffer of a closed file has been officially 'undefined' and
inaccessible since at least the Johnson Administration. (Lyndon, not
Andrew). It's one of the reasons READ ..INTO .. is popular.

What's wrong is that for the past twenty years you have failed to purchase
lottery tickets, because you have been damn lucky.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o46z5.6076$5p.35596@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com> <1g2z5.832$N86.31024@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
LOL!!!

You've been d..mn Lucky!

IBM has with OS390 cleaned up some things (witness R15 problems)
because with the OO functions 'RETURNING', this was required.

Unlike WANG and I think Honeywell, at 'CLOSE' execution, IBM trashes
the record contents.  They don't just leave it alone, it is purposefully
trashed.
I haven't checked whether they point the Record  Buffer off into space
or move garbage into the Buffer, but its whacked at end of SUCCESSFUL
CLOSE.

On Wang, even tho' the manual says after CLOSE, the record contents are
unreliable, its still available, BUT I've never risked it.

My advice to you,  either use a READ INTO,
or if that is not good (program size, etc)
then do NOT close the file until you're DONE.


Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:1g2z5.832$N86.31024@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
> Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
> news:8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > After putting in the displays, I found the record buffer was filling
with
> > junk after the CLOSE.  The first display showed the correct value.  Now,
I
> > know all about how this is bad code, but regardless, it's been running
for
> > 20 years and just now decides to fail.  The load module has been
untouched
> > for almost a year.
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CD2A8A.1E227A8B@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com> <1g2z5.832$N86.31024@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <o46z5.6076$5p.35596@news2.mia>`

```


mangogwr wrote:
...
> Unlike WANG and I think Honeywell, at 'CLOSE' execution, IBM trashes
> the record contents.  They don't just leave it alone, it is purposefully
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> CLOSE.
...

It's not that they "trash" the record contents, but that the buffer
storage that was allocated for the file at OPEN is released at CLOSE,
making it available for other uses (after all, the semantic meaning of
"CLOSE" is "I'm no longer going to use this file or its contents").  
One would hope that any pointers to the buffers would be cleared at that
point, but in any event whatever pointer value you might have at that
point can't point to the original data, because that storage is no
longer owned by the program.
```

#### ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qmchg$g3on6$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```

Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com...
> IBM COBOL / OS390 / IBM 9672 CPU.
>
> I have a COBOL program that has been running in PROD for over 20 years.
It
> just failed the other day with a S0C7.  Here's what the code looks like:
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

I don't think anything has changed.  The buffers have been unreliable for
quite a while now.  That is why READ INTO is so popular.  I think you have
just gotten very, very lucky, or that move wasn't being executed after close
(code change during maintenance???).
```

```
has available for the program -- or in what else is running at the
same time.

<union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h_Rz5.2561$bK5.13491@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com> <39cecaeb.21977805@207.126.101.100>`

```
Yes, we had a routine (written before I got there) for a DataGeneral COBOL
to
MVS conversion.  Of course DG Like Wang has all these NIFTY
HighLevelInterface
(HLI) callable routines to do thinsg like delete files, get command line
args,
get file name, get Job Name, etc.

Conversion was started on OS390 V2.2 or so (97/98).  Someone
in another group wrote an assembler sub that was called and returned
the Job No, Job Name, etc.  Set a Return Code.

About three months ago it started failing? not failing with
the main Program reporting no errors, correct output, etc.
But the Step COND CODE = 7888 or something similar.
Program was called by main routine which was executed in
about 350 Jobs a day!

I researched it and it turned out that some MORON or CRETIN
didn't know wat to do!  The Retrun Code field was a PIC S9(7) COMP-3.
The assembler sub, got the data at the RC address, then used that as a
pointer
(the value was COMP-3 0 (X'0000000C') or address 12 (address of a CC1 or
CC2 - used
to translate 24 bit to 31 bit addresses for I-O), then whatever was  at
address X'0000000C'
in memory was moved to R15!

I knew things were screwey when I saw the caller passed a COMP-3 RC field
and the
sub had no 'PACK' instruction, since the system service being called set R15
to a BINARY
value.


Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:39cecaeb.21977805@207.126.101.100...
> I've seen something like this suddenly bite (even though it should
> have long ago) from a simple change in the amount of memory the region
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I have a COBOL program that has been running in PROD for over 20 years.
It
> >just failed the other day with a S0C7.  Here's what the code looks like:
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >junk after the CLOSE.  The first display showed the correct value.  Now,
I
> >know all about how this is bad code, but regardless, it's been running
for
> >20 years and just now decides to fail.  The load module has been
untouched
> >for almost a year.
> >
> >What is wrong?  Something in MVS?  Did the Systems group change
something?
> >What changed?
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

## Thread: cobol lesson in Hong Kong — 3 this month / 4 total

### cobol lesson in Hong Kong

- **From:** "cheng" <cheng@taifuji.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qi6of$ni31@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```
Hello,

Can anybody tell me where can I learn COBOL
lesson in Hong Kong?

Thanks for your help.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol lesson in Hong Kong

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39ce08c6.1348125@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8qi6of$ni31@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```
"cheng" <cheng@taifuji.com> wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Cheng:

The best place to get an inexpensive compiler or to learn how to write
COBOL code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

The book only costs about $20.00 US$ and includes the compiler at no
additional charge.

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.chapters.ca
http://www.indigo.ca
http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com
http://www.amazon.com




Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)

## Thread: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please? — 17 this month / 37 total

### any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

- **From:** email@address.com
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T02:58:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39cd6d92.1167854@news.sprint.ca>`

```
Here it goes.. I just typed from the book and run it. It's should
work, but it didn't. any idea? Thanks.
______________________________________________________________________

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.       SENOR.
AUTHOR.           ROBERT GRAUER.

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT STUDENT-FILE ASSIGN TO SYSIN
         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
    SELECT PRINT-FILE   ASSIGN TO SYSOUT
         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD  STUDENT-FILE
    RECORD CONTAINS 43 CHARACTERS
    DATA RECORD IS STUDENT-IN.
01  STUDENT-IN.
    05  STU-NAME             PIC X(25).
    05  STU-CREDITS          PIC 9(3).
    05  STU-MAJOR            PIC X(15).

FD  PRINT-FILE
    RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS
    DATA RECORD IS PRINT-LINE.
01  PRINT-LINE               PIC X(132).

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH      PIC X(2)      VALUE SPACES.

01  HEADING-LINE.
    05  FILLER               PIC X(10)     VALUE SPACES.
    05  FILLER               PIC X(12)     VALUE 'STUDENT NAME'
    05  FILLER               PIC X(110)    VALUE SPACES.

01  DETAIL-LINE.
    05  FILLER               PIC X(8)      VALUE SPACES.
    05  PRINT-NAME           PIC X(25).
    05  FILLER               PIC X(99)     VALUE SPACES.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
PREPARE-SENIOR-REPORT.
    OPEN INPUT  STUDENT-FILE
         OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.
    READ STUDENT-FILE
        AT END MOVE 'NO' TO DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH
    END-READ.
    PERFORM WRITE-HEADING-LINE.
    PERFORM PROCESS-RECORDS
        UNTIL DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH = 'NO'.
    CLOSE STUDENT-FILE
          PRINT-FILE.
    STOP RUN.

WRITE-HEADING-LINE.
    MOVE HEADING-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
    WRITE PRINT-LINE.

PROCESS-RECORDS.
    IF STU-CREDITS > 110 THEN WRITE STU-MAJOR = 'ENGINEERING'
        MOVE STU-NAME TO PRINT-NAME
        MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PRINT-LINE
        WRITE PRINT-LINE
    END-IF.
    READ STUDENT-FILE
        AT END MOVE 'NO' TO DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH
    END-READ.
______________________________________________________________________
```

```
The problem (as explained in one of the earlier notes) is not that your code
is "wrong" - it simply is NOT what this book is trying to do in this case.
They wanted a combined conditional.  Unless you have this book, I don't
think you can correct the original code - to do what the book was trying to
do.
```

#### ↳ Re: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
email@address.com wrote:

> Here it goes.. I just typed from the book and run it. It's should
> work, but it didn't. any idea? Thanks.

...

>
> PROCESS-RECORDS.
>     IF STU-CREDITS > 110 THEN WRITE STU-MAJOR = 'ENGINEERING'

Yes, this line doesn't make sense.

But the reason I am posting is to tell you that when you ask a question
such as this, you should tell us your compiler, your OS, your error
message, and what you have tried to do to solve your problem.

Look at your compile listing - find where it lists errors and where it
tells you whether it successfully compiled and linked.  Does it point to
the above line?  What does it say?

Check your manual.  What do you think that above line should try to do?
What would you like it to do?
```

<a id="t-44cca4240b"></a>
## Thread: Book on programming: Cobol to Java — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Book on programming: Cobol to Java

- **From:** emtinc@flash.net (emtinc)
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T02:52:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60ez5.7001$864.392341@news.flash.net>`

```
Here is a book that you might be interested in:
Learn Java by Association with C Cobol Fortran Pascal Visual Basic
Dictionary of Programming Languages

More info (including sample chapters)  at http://www.TechEdNet.com

This book is for anyone who knows C, Fortran, Cobol, Pascal or
Visual Basic and  wants to quickly learn Java.

When trying to learn a new programming language like Java you
will often say to yourself "Gee, I know how to do that in Pascal",
or whatever language you are currently fluent in, " but I wish I
knew how to do that in Java". This book is an answer to that wish.

The book is setup like a dictionary but is much more than a dictionary.
Like a dictionary it helps you with the translation of words (syntax),
in addition, it also tells you how you build "sentences" in various
programming languages.

The CD includes JDK, documentation and several Java tools.

```

#### ↳ Re: Book on programming: Cobol to Java

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qkp6u$cl2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<60ez5.7001$864.392341@news.flash.net>`

```
In article <60ez5.7001$864.392341@news.flash.net>,
  emtinc@flash.net (emtinc) wrote:
> Here is a book that you might be interested in:
> Learn Java by Association with C Cobol Fortran Pascal Visual Basic
> Dictionary of Programming Languages
>
> More info (including sample chapters)  at http://www.TechEdNet.com


Hi:

Although I am VIOLENTLY opposed to using anything but COBOL
for commercial applications, I have a mild interest in other
languages and have actually used about 14 different ones, I even
had a brief affair with PL/I in the sixties, when it first came
out.

The idea of a book which shows a COBOL programmer how to do
the things they are accustomed to in another language makes
sense to me.

I went to the site given and went to check out the sample
chapters.  The first was a broken link, the second caused
by AOL connection to terminate and it did it TWICE! The
third, just gave a blank page.  All I did was click on
the links.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: Indexed File Template — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Indexed File Template

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T04:57:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CD8616.4CB6C62A@home.com>`

```
The folowing is related to OO, but probably applies equally to
Structured. Based on a tip Bill Klein gave me I have come up with a
generic class template for handling SEQUENTIAL and LINE SEQUENTIAL
files. The latter is used to read or write some 20 separate ascii
delimiteds

I would like to do the same - create a template for indexed (ISAM)
files. Even though my indexed files are fixed length I could still use
the "record varying clause" to 'fool' the compiler - however the problem
is the ISAM PrimeKey and Alternate Keys, which could be located anywhere
in the record; of course their positioning in the record is determined
the first time the file is created. Any ideas how to resolve the
Prime/Alternate Key problem ?????

The following is the File Class for Ascii-Delimited/Line-Sequential :-

Jimmy, Calgary AB
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
       *>-----------------lsqfile.cbl-----------------------------------

       Class-id.	  LineSequentialFile
			  inherits from FileMethods.
	..............

       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY................
       End FACTORY.
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT.
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
	    Select		 Data-File
	    assign		 Data-filename
	    organization	 line sequential
	    access		 sequential
	    file status 	 ws-filestatus.
	
	*> I would need references here to Prime/Alternate Keys

       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  Data-File
	   record varying from 1 to 1000 characters
	      depending on ws-record-size.

	*> Again a reference required for Prime/Alt Keys
	
       01  Data-record		 pic x(1000).
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       copy "LinkErr.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==. 
       copy "wsresult.cpy".
       01 Data-filename 		     pic x(100).
       01 ws-record-size		     pic x(4) comp-5.
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 os-record.
	     10  occurs 1 to 1000
		 depending on ws-record-size	pic x.
       *>----------------------------------------------------------
       Method-ID. "access-file".
       *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-result				pic x(4) comp-5.
       Linkage Section.
       copy "fileactn.cpy".
       01 lnk-result				pic x(4) comp-5.
       Procedure Division Using     file-action
			  returning lnk-result.
	  set file-result-ok to true
	  initialize ws-Error-Data

	  if   dont-initialize
	       continue

	  else initialize os-record(1:ws-record-size)
	  End-if

	  Evaluate TRUE

	    When read-next
		 Read Data-File next record
		   At End
		     Set file-finis to True
		   Not at End
		     Move Data-record  (1:ws-record-size)
			  to os-record (1:ws-record-size)
		 End-Read
	    When write-record
		 Move os-record     (1:ws-record-size)
		      to Data-record(1:ws-record-size)
		 Write Data-record
	    When close-file	    Close Data-File
	    When open-input-file    Open input Data-File
	    When open-output-file   Open output Data-file
	    When delete-file
		 call "CBL_DELETE_FILE" using	  Data-Filename
					returning ls-result
		  move "00" to ws-filestatus
	   When other
	     invoke super "invalid-action" using file-action
	     set file-error to true
	  End-evaluate

	  *> Code below is not moving a 'key' but the first 50
	  *> characters of the line-sequential record

	  if	  ws-filestatus <> "00"
		  move os-record(1:10)	to ws-error-key
		  move os-record(11:40) to ws-error-name
		  invoke super "check-file-status"
			 using	   ws-ErrorFields
				   file-action
				   ws-file-result
			 returning lnk-result

	  else	  move ws-file-result to lnk-result
	  End-if
       End Method "access-file".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "initialize".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-Parent			    object reference.
       copy "makefile.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
       Procedure Division using     lnk-Parent
				    lnk-makefile-params.
	 initialize		  ws-ErrorFields
	 move lnk-filenum      to ws-filenum
	 move lnk-filename     to Data-Filename
	 move lnk-record-size  to ws-record-size
	 invoke super "set-window-title" using lnk-Parent
					       lnk-makefile-params
       End Method "initialize".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "get-file-object".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *> Gets the data object after a READ NEXT
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-record.
	  10  occurs 1 to 1000
	      depending on ws-record-size    pic x.
       Procedure division returning lnk-record.
	Move os-record(1:ws-record-size)
	  to lnk-record(1:ws-record-size)
       End Method "get-file-object".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "set-file-object".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *> Sets the data object prior to a WRITE
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-record.
	  10  occurs 1 to 1000
	      depending on ws-record-size	     pic x.
       Procedure division using lnk-record.
	Move lnk-record  (1:ws-record-size)
	     to os-record(1:ws-record-size)
       End Method "set-file-object".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       End OBJECT.
       End CLASS LineSequentialFile.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed File Template

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-09-24T18:48:28+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qk8fk$9ur$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<39CD8616.4CB6C62A@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

it's time you dropped this archaic ISAM nonsense and got into real
databases..<G>

I did exactly what you're trying to do in the early 1970s (as disk drives
became readily available), with VSAM KSDS on system 360.

We started off by writing a general purpose VSAM file handler (incorporating
all the Methods you posted) which became a "template". This then had certain
symbols substituted into it , and could be used to quickly generate a
specific file handler by editing these symbols.

You ended up with a module which did everything.

However, we had one of these modules for every file.

Just as you are experiencing, the problem was "knowing" where the keys were
located.

Reference modification was not available at the time, so I wrote an
assembler routine which we called VARMOVE. It allowed you to reference
fields with an offset and a length. It lasted me for many years and later I
translated it into Intel Assembler so I could use it on the PC.

Nowadays, (we all have to move with the times, Jimmy...<G>) I have replaced
it with reference modification in Standard COBOL.

So, to solve your Key problem make sure that when the Method is invoked it
is passed the offset and length of the required key (or keys). Like this...

RECORD KEY IS os-record (offset:length)

The first time you write such a record, the necessary index(es) will be
created. You will need to recognise and differentiate between individual
files.

 (This does not preclude having a generic solution to handle them (you will
instantiate a different object for each file, with the appropriate file name
and key data as outlined above.)

And ensure that the correct offset and length for the key(s) is passed when
you invoke your generic solution to access them later.)

OO allows a much better solution than our original one of generating
individual modules (one for each file, and then compiling them) although the
effect is identical.

And NO, I am NOT back in CLC...I was bored on a Sunday afternoon because
everything is working and there are no outstanding problems to be looked
at... <G>

This NG is still desperately in need of moderating (just look at the thread
on H1b...[and you edited my use of "horseshit" once, remember..??]) and
until this happens it is unlikely to be of any real use to people who need
it.

Not only that, but it is actually propagating bad  practice in some cases
(...move  "Y" to end-flag...Yucccckkk!!! Looks like a society for the
preservation of ugly COBOL [SPUC ?!])

I have had several students sending me this rubbish lately. The latest was a
girl who said (get this...!) her course was TEACHING them to do this...!!

The same people are saying the same things they were saying a year ago.
Foodman, DocDwarf, YukonMama, nothing changes... Nobody looks forward, or
wonders, or speculates or imagines or innovates,  just  pedantic little
arguments about outmoded and obsolete dinosaur technology.

The same "voices of reason" (I won't embarrass them by naming them) pour oil
on troubled waters and try to get some semblance of balance and sanity with
simple, down to Earth advice, genuinely trying to be of help.

Hands up all those who have been helped by CLC? Now hands up all those who
NEEDED help AFTER visiting CLC... ?!

If it was a pub, I'd drink somewhere else...<G>

Pete.
```

```
OOps!

I got it wrong.

The solution I proposed to Jimmy cannot be implemented.

COBOL does not support Reference Modification outside of the Procedure
Division.

In case anyone else here is interested in trying to do what Jimmy's trying
to do, write to him and ask for the solution.

I have posted a less elegant, but nonetheless effective solution to him.

Pete.
```

## Thread: working storage addresses — 11 msgs

### working storage addresses

- **From:** cd <cdolny@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi folks,

COBOL is not my native language, so I'm hoping someone can point me in
the right direction.  I need to call an Assembly language program from
a COBOL program.  The Assembly language program takes one parameter, a
structure containing three pointers.  I have the following in my
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  ASM-PGM-PARM.
        05  P1        USAGE IS POINTER.
        05  P2        USAGE IS POINTER.
        05  P3        USAGE IS POINTER.

These pointers need to point to other items in my WORKING-STORAGE
SECTION.  For example, I have the following in my WORKING-STORAGE.
    01  INTERFACE-STRUC-1.
        05  OPERATION        PIC X(8).
        etc. etc.   More Level-05 fields...

The following gives me an error message at compile time:
    SET ADDRESS OF INTERFACE-STRUC-1 TO P1 OF ASM-PGM-PARM.
The following doesn't work either:
    SET P1 OF ASM-PGM-PARM TO ADDRESS OF INTERFACE-STRUC-1.
Pointer P1 needs to be set to the address of different items depending
on parameter values that this COBOL program was called with.

I am using COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2.

What is the correct way to set a pointer to an address in working
storage?  Where is this documented in the manuals?

Thanks much.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KcSz5.2579$bK5.13682@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Here's the manual:
COVER Book Cover
--------------------------------------------------------------------------
COBOL for OS/390 & VM
COBOL Set for AIX
VisualAge COBOL

Language Reference

Document Number SC26-9046-01

And here's the text:
A data item defined with USAGE IS POINTER is a pointer data item.  A pointer
data item is a 4-byte
elementary item,

You can use pointer data items to accomplish limited base addressing.
Pointer data items can be compared for equality or moved to other pointer
items.

A pointer data item can only be used:

   In a SET statement (Format 5 only)
    In a relation condition
    In the USING phrase of a CALL statement, an ENTRY statement, or the
Procedure Division header.

The USAGE IS POINTER clause can be written at any level except level 88. If
a group item is described with the USAGE IS POINTER clause, the elementary
items within the group are pointer data items; the group itself is not a
pointer data item and cannot be used in the syntax where a pointer data item
is allowed.  The  USAGE clause of an elementary item cannot contradict the
USAGE clause of a group to which the item belongs.

Pointer data items can be part of a group that is referred to in a MOVE
statement or an input/output
statement.  However, if a pointer data item is part of a group, there is no
conversion of values when the
statement is executed.

A pointer data item can be the subject or object of a REDEFINES clause.

SYNCHRONIZED can be used with USAGE IS POINTER to obtain efficient use of
the pointer data item.

A VALUE clause for a pointer data item can contain only NULL or NULLS.

A pointer data item cannot be a conditional variable.

A pointer data item does not belong to any class or category.

The DATE FORMAT, JUSTIFIED, PICTURE, and BLANK WHEN ZERO clauses cannot be
used to describe group or elementary items defined with the USAGE IS POINTER
clause.

Pointer data items are ignored in CORRESPONDING operations.

A pointer data item can be written to a data set, but, upon subsequent
reading of the record containing the  pointer, the address contained can no
longer represent a valid pointer.

Note:  USAGE IS POINTER is implicitly specified for the ADDRESS OF special
register.  For more information  see the IBM COBOL Programming Guide for
your platform.


So to use it:

6.2.33.5 Format 5: SET for USAGE IS POINTER Data Items

When this form of the SET statement is executed, the current value of the
receiving field is replaced by the address value contained in the sending
field.

+--- Format 5--SET (USAGE IS POINTER Data Items) ------------------------+
�                                                                        �
�          <------------------------------+                              �
� >>--SET------identifier-4--------------------------------------------> �
�            +-ADDRESS OF--identifier-5-+                                �
�                                                                        �
� >--TO----identifier-6----------------------------------------------->< �
�        +-ADDRESS OF--identifier-7-�                                    �
�        +-NULL---------------------�                                    �
�        +-NULLS--------------------+                                    �
�                                                                        �
+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
identifier-4
    Receiving fields.

    Must be described as USAGE IS POINTER.

ADDRESS OF identifier-5
    Receiving fields.

    identifier-5 must be level-01 or level-77 items defined in the Linkage
Section.  The addresses of these
    items are set to the value of the operand specified in the TO phrase.

    Identifier-5 must not be reference-modified.

identifier-6
    Sending field.

    Must be described as USAGE IS POINTER.

    Cannot contain an address within the program's own Working-Storage,
File, or Local-Storage Section.

ADDRESS OF identifier-7
    Sending field.

     PICTURE 302 Under AIX, OS/2, and Windows, it must name an item in
either the Linkage Section or the
    Working-Storage Section of level 01, 77, or 02-49. PICTURE 303

     PICTURE 304 For OS/390 and VM, it must name an item in the Linkage
Section of any level except 66 or
    88.  ADDRESS OF identifier-7 contains the address of the identifier, and
not the content of the identifier.
    PICTURE 305

NULL
NULLS
    Sending field.

    Sets the receiving field to contain the value of an invalid address.


Table 49shows valid combinations of sending and receiving fields in a Format
5 SET statement.

+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
� Table 49. Sending and Receiving Fields for Format 5 SET Statement
�
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
�
�               �                      Receiving Field
�
� Sending Field
+-----------------------------------------------------------�
�               �  USAGE IS POINTER �     ADDRESS OF    �     NULL/NULLS
�
+---------------+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------
�
�    USAGE IS   �       Valid       �       Valid       �         -
�
�    POINTER    �                   �                   �
�
+---------------+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------
�
�   ADDRESS OF  �       Valid       �       Valid       �         -
�
+---------------+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------
�
�   NULL/NULLS  �       Valid       �       Valid       �         -
�
+-------------------------------------------------------------

SO TO MAKE A LONG STORY SHORT, you've got it BACKWARDS.!!
You want the POINTER to hold the Address of  INTERFACE-STRUC-1.
Not the other way around.

Think of it just Like 'C' or BASIC, put the address of 'INTERFACE-STRUC-1'
into
PTR-1.
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CFD93D.7139DAB8@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
cd wrote:
> 
> Hi folks,
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks much.

The correct syntax should be:
SET pointer-name TO ADDRESS OF linkage-section-record

IBM mainframe COBOL does not allow you to take the address of a
working-storage item (you already have addressability to it, so why do
you need it's address?).  At least this was true in VS COBOL II, and I'm
not sure that it's different even in COBOL for OS/390 & VM.

This topic has been discussed in comp.lang.cobol before, but not for
several months.

One creative solution I have seen is to write a very short nested COBOL
subprogram.  Pass it the working-storage item and a pointer variable. 
In the nested subprogram, SET the pointer variable to the ADDRESS OF the
linkage section item (your original working-storage item in your calling
program) and return.

The nested subprogram might look something like this:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  WS-ADDR.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
DATA DIVSION.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01  WS-RECORD   PIC X(80).
01  WS-POINTER  USAGE IS POINTER.
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING WS-RECORD, WS-POINTER.
GET-ADDRESS-OF-WS-RECORD.
    SET WS-POINTER TO ADDRESS OF WS-RECORD
    GOBACK.
END PROGRAM WS-ADDR.

The nested subprogram would be embedded inside your main COBOL program
right before its END PROGRAM statement.

Then you should be able to get your pointers in the main program by
calling the subprogram:

CALL "WS-ADDR" USING INTERFACE-STRUC-1, P1 OF ASM-PGM-PARM
CALL "WS-ADDR" USING INTERFACE-STRUC-2, P2 OF ASM-PGM-PARM
CALL "WS-ADDR" USING INTERFACE-STRUC-3, P3 OF ASM-PGM-PARM     

This gets around the restriction in IBM COBOL that prevents you from
directly saving the address of a working-storage item.  I haven't
verified the syntax of the nested subprogram, so you may have to do some
experimentation on your own.  

I hope that helps!
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qomji$l45$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You can do it one at a time and then you can expand this example. Each time
the first parameter must change. There is no "SET ADDRESS OF" for WS fields
in IBM COBOL2 and greater. After returning from the caller, WS-POINTER will
contain the address of the first parameter, in this example, WS-DATA.

In ASSEMBLER, it would be:

GETADDR    CSECT
                        USING    *,15
                        LM           7,8,0(1)
                        ST            7,0(,R8)
                        BR            14
                        END

In COBOL, it would be:

PROGRAM-ID. GETADDR.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01    LS-PARM-AREA        PIC X(01).
01    LS-PARM-POINTER POINTER.
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LS-PARM-AREA, LS-PARM-POINTER.
        SET LS-PARM-POINTER TO ADDRESS OF LS-PARM-AREA.
        GOBACK.

In both cases, the syntax would be:

CALL "GETADDR" USING WS-AREA, WS-POINTER.

The WS definitions in the calling program looks like this:

        03    WS-DATA        PIC  X(1024).
        03    WS-POINTER  POINTER.

You can get into redefining the pointer as a fullword, then you can
arithmetically increment/decrement the pointer, but I'll leave that as an
exercise.

HTH....

Cheers,

Bill,
Atlanta
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

```
WOB wrote:
> 
> You can do it one at a time and then you can expand this example. Each time
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cheers,

My apologies for misspelling DIVSION in my earlier example!

In my normal working environment, I use the following COBOL compiler:
PP 5688-197 IBM COBOL for MVS & VM   1.2.2    

I verified by experimentation that this compiler will not allow you to
SET a pointer TO a working-storage address.  So I coded up an example
program named "GETADDR" with a nested subprogram named "WS-ADDR":

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                      
 PROGRAM-ID.     GETADDR.                                      
*AUTHOR.         ARNOLD J. TREMBLEY.                           
*DATE-WRITTEN.   MON-25-SEP-2000.                              
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                                         
 DATA DIVISION.                                                
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                      
 01  200-RECORD  PIC X(80)  VALUE                              
     'THIS IS A TEST DATA RECORD'.                             
 01  800-POINTERS.                                             
     05  800-POINTER     USAGE IS POINTER VALUE NULL.          
     05  800-POINTER-N   REDEFINES 800-POINTER PIC S9(9) COMP. 
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                           
 0000-MAINLINE.                                                
     DISPLAY '800-POINTER-N BEFORE = ' 800-POINTER-N           
     CALL 'WS-ADDR' USING 200-RECORD, 800-POINTER              
     DISPLAY '800-POINTER-N AFTER  = ' 800-POINTER-N           
     GOBACK.                                                   
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                      
 PROGRAM-ID.     WS-ADDR.                         
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                            
 DATA DIVISION.                                   
 LINKAGE SECTION.                                 
 01  WS-RECORD   PIC X(80).                       
 01  WS-POINTER  USAGE IS POINTER.                
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING WS-RECORD, WS-POINTER.  
 GET-ADDRESS-OF-WS-RECORD.                        
     SET WS-POINTER TO ADDRESS OF WS-RECORD       
     GOBACK.                                      
 END PROGRAM WS-ADDR.                             
 END PROGRAM GETADDR.

It compiled cleanly, and when executed gave the following results:

800-POINTER-N BEFORE = 000000000     
800-POINTER-N AFTER  = 210768784     

The length of WS-RECORD in the WS-ADDR subprogram does not matter if the
only thing you are going to do is SET the pointer and return.

I hope this example is more helpful.

With kindest regards,
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qrkul$geopo$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

cd <cdolny@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi folks,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
How exactly is your assembler program accessing the pointers?  It sounds to
me like it is expecting a standard call area.
I.E.

        L   R2,0(R1)       ADDRESS PARM AREA 1
        L   R3,4(R1)       ADDRESS PARM AREA 2
        L   R4,8(R1)       ADDRESS PARM AREA 3

(or, of course, any version of this same concept).

If this is the case, then it is a simple call from the COBOL program...

CALL asm-program USING INTERFACE-STRUC-1, INTERFACE-STRUC-2,
INTERFACE-STRUC-3.

As for setting a pointer to an address, you can do the following:

SET WS-PTR1 TO ADDRESS OF LINKAGE-AREA-ITEM where ws-ptr1 is in your working
storage (or linkage area, for that matter, as long as it is addressed and
defined as usage pointer) and linkage-area-item is in your linkage area.

Hope this helps....

Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** "Armin Zerfas" <A.Zerfas@telekom.de>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qpjjp$s8r3@mailgate1b.telekom.de>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You are looking in the wrong direction: you need a simple call to your
assembler-program like
call CALL 'ASM-PROG' USING INTERFACE-STRUC-1 INTERFACE-STRUC-2 ...
and COBOL will be happy to create the Parameterlist and pass its address.

regards
A. Zerfas
cd schrieb in Nachricht <8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi folks,
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lf41tssvnjpi0fjp5du67dt8us18a6mks5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Mon, 25 Sep 2000 15:20:28 GMT cd <cdolny@my-deja.com> wrote:

:>COBOL is not my native language, so I'm hoping someone can point me in
:>the right direction.  I need to call an Assembly language program from
:>a COBOL program.  The Assembly language program takes one parameter, a
:>structure containing three pointers.  I have the following in my
:>WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
:>    01  ASM-PGM-PARM.
:>        05  P1        USAGE IS POINTER.
:>        05  P2        USAGE IS POINTER.
:>        05  P3        USAGE IS POINTER.

:>These pointers need to point to other items in my WORKING-STORAGE
:>SECTION.  For example, I have the following in my WORKING-STORAGE.
:>    01  INTERFACE-STRUC-1.
:>        05  OPERATION        PIC X(8).
:>        etc. etc.   More Level-05 fields...

:>The following gives me an error message at compile time:
:>    SET ADDRESS OF INTERFACE-STRUC-1 TO P1 OF ASM-PGM-PARM.
:>The following doesn't work either:
:>    SET P1 OF ASM-PGM-PARM TO ADDRESS OF INTERFACE-STRUC-1.
:>Pointer P1 needs to be set to the address of different items depending
:>on parameter values that this COBOL program was called with.

:>I am using COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2.

:>What is the correct way to set a pointer to an address in working
:>storage?  Where is this documented in the manuals?

Somehow I doubt your ASM subprogram wants a structure like that. It is more
likely that it wants a standard parmlist where R1 points to a list of
addresses, i.e.,
      LM   R3,R5,0(R1)  
at entry would load the addresses of the three parameters.

If my assumption is correct, you would use
       CALL 'subroutine' USING STRUCT-1, STRUCT-2, STRUCT-3

Of course, if the ASM subprogram does use a second level of addressing, you
will need to write another subprogram to get the addresses.
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** cd <cdolny@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qq84u$gqt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Many thanks to everyone who responded.  I have a better understanding
of pointers in COBOL now.  We still cannot find our paper manuals for
COBOL for MVS & VM here, but at least we have Book Manager...

Thanks again.

     cd
```

#### ↳ Re: working storage addresses

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r38ml$ona$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8qnqf4$ls8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  cd <cdolny@my-deja.com> wrote:
> Hi folks,
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I wanted to mention that if you pass a single WS parameter to a given
subprogram and this subprogram attempts to get fancy and return the
ADDRESS of this parameter in R15 (COBOL RETURN-CODE Special Register),
the Caller will be in for a surprise. Regardless whether the subprogram
is COBOL or ASSEMBLER, COBOL treats this Special Register internally as
a HALFWORD, S9(04) BINARY. Therefore, you WILL lose the high-order (2-
bytes) of R15 and have a bogus address. The use of a Special "Register"
does NOT necessarily translate to a FULLWORD value, so be careful.

Just wanted you to be aware of this if it is ever suggested.

Cheers,

Bill,
WOB Consulting, Inc.
Atlanta


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: Woshington Pravda cries for H1b's — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Re: Woshington Pravda cries for H1b's

- **From:** "Margaret Bartley" <REMOVETHISmargaretbartley@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<8qrtm0$nt9$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C7BA1C.346875E@jay.jay.org> <8qb322$cro$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39C984E4.C618BFF8@dio.com> <8qe6up$4gk$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <h2Hy5.1571$2l.39945@news.shore.net>`

```

<eskwired@SPAMBLOCK.shore.net> wrote in message
news:h2Hy5.1571$2l.39945@news.shore.net...
> In comp.software.year-2000 philotsopher
<philotsopher@my-deja.com> wrote:

> It seems to me that as a country, we should benefit by the
cheap labor,
> but that we need to retrain our workers who lose their
jobs to unskilled
> foreign workers.
>
> What I see is us reaping the benefits of these cheap
foreign workers in
> the form of cheaper consumer goods, while at the same
time, having a
> skilled workforce right here at home, so we can do jobs
here that can't be
> done overseas.

And just what jobs might those be?

Practically the entire manufacturing sector of the US
economy has been lost.  When I was in school, I learned that
the reason the British had such a poor economy was that
investors had invested in the US, and let the British
industrial sector go to seed, and thus the British economy
was uncompetitive, and down the tubes.  Now, of course, the
investors have moved their investments to Asia, mostly, and
the American industrial jobs are pretty much gone.

That didn't happen overnight, it took about fifteen years.
The Lima Accords were signed in 1974, signifying the US's
intention to ship manufacturing jobs overseas.  In the 80's,
the US Dept of Commerce was actively helping American
businesses set up shop overseas.  The rationale was that the
tariffs on the goods coming in would be greater than the
taxes lost through loss wages.  Of course, as soon as the
facilitites were up and running, GATT was signed, and the
tariffs removed.


I've seen lots of posters on this ng making fun of H1-b
workers, but they are missing the point.  When the Japanese
first starting manufacturing, they weren't very good at it.
"Made in Japan" was synonamous with poor qualifty for many
years.  But eventually they learned, and almost put the US
auto industry out of business.  Right now, China makes toys
and crappy electronic goods.  But the money and know-how is
being poured into China, and soon they will become a world
powerhouse of high-tech manufacturing.

Right now, the US is in the same position vis-a-vis the
information economy as it was in the 80s vis-a-vis the
manufacturing economy.  The same thing is now happening with
the high-tech sector that you seem to feel that only
Americans are capable of handling.  The whole global
corporate culture has been moving for years now to a labor
model of outsourced technical help. (Where do you think that
outsourced technical help will be located?)

Meanwhile, large US and European companies have been
establishing training centers in India, teaching them
high-tech materials.    from http://www.iiit.net/:

"Located in Hyderabad, the future Silicon Valley of India,
Indian Institute of Information Technology combines the
freedom of an academic institution with the strength of the
corporate sector. IIIT is an autonomous, self-supporting
institution started in 1998 with seed support from the
Government of Andhra Pradesh and the participation of major
national and international IT companies. "

(There are several campuses, use any search engine).  They
graduate hundreds of thousands of graduates a year, and they
are learning the latest materal that is taught only at very
few American schools.

Twenty years ago, you could go to a local community college
and learn to do computer programming.  Try to go now, and
learn the latest technology.  It's not being taught to
Americans.


The global corporate interests, who have paid off the US
Congress and both Presidential candidates, and who do NOT
have the well-being of American workers at heart, is fast at
work getting rid of all decent paying jobs, and they are
just hoping that American IT workers will be as ignorant,
and arrogant, as the manufacturing workers were.

And it looks like they are correct.
```

```
"Margaret Bartley" <REMOVETHISmargaretbartley@hotmail.com> wrote:

>The whole global

this is definitely not true. yes, you can call up a phone number when
your computer breaks, but if it's the hardware, or you don't have two
phones lines so you can test your dialup isp, or you can't describe
the problem adequately, there is nothing like the shop down the
street. i suppose eventually the hardware will move away, and the
software will be based more on the client server model, and maybe even
go the way of remote control software for technical support for the
dunces who do stupid things. there might eventually be a password for
the control panel, but currently, the best technology has is plug and
play (which in customer terms is, if the driver is included with
windows, it will detect it on bootup, which is sort of circular in the
first place). that is hardly installation by remote or tech support by
remote. and what i find funny is, the thing which would lead to the
best globalization (cough modem and online code updates) have managed
to create voice-modems whose drivers are nonstandard, so they have to
be installed locally anyways. on top of that, the software must be
installed locally.

on the other hand, win me is going to change alot of things. rollback
(basically drivers being stored) , 28.8k modem updates, plugnplay and
safe mode will monopolize alot of tech support.

>Meanwhile, large US and European companies have been
>establishing training centers in India, teaching them
>high-tech materials.    from http://www.iiit.net/:

and then those indians can sell them to other rich indians? and those
us universities won't have student loans, right?

while it's possible to move overseas to save on labor, and then move
those techies back so they can do hardware support here, it's
improbable, highly. you think the BAs who can't bother to learn the
tech themselves will learn a new language, move to a new country, all
in hopes of saving a few pennies on labor? what if once they get
there, those workers unionize? i guess you'll move back, right? who
wins in the end? the shipping, maps and real estate industry. because
they provide the houses, and ensure the goods get from the newest
source of cheap labor to the newest source of rich clients.

follow the money is nothing new.
```

```
but the hardware is someone else's, and i get the shaft if something
breaks. hehe, even the local parts might not work sometimes and it's

plug in the drive into a new machine.

yea, and then i get to fix it with local parts anyways. managers may

restart wouldn't work. got it to work on one of them and had to
duplicate the harddisk, and resize the partition for each other
prebuilt, because i wasn't sure if i remembered the steps to get the
restart to work on the other machines. it's not the nicest way to fix
it, but at least i didn't try installing windows on each machine again
and hope that i might get the right number of steps to get them to
work.

100% is better than a gamble even if it takes longer.
```

```
learning version of cobol?

or vb or c++ 6.0

the teacher mean a new version was just released.

my time trying to learn a cobol cobol compiler not taught
in class?

Thanks for your help
Jim
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol language

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qop4r$5fd$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<39CFF2BB.36AE9FE8@slic.com>`

```
Cobol is a long standing language,standardised by international agreement, and not  owned by any company. (like Delphi or C++) There are dozens of Cobol's by various vendors, for all different platforms. I would suspect that a course would be taught on PC's, but you could be learning on a mainframe.  Regardless, all the Cobol that you learn will be transportable from your PC to a mainframe.  The differences lie mainly in the screen-interfacing methods.

One place to start is www.adtools.com    , Fujitsu's webpage.  The sell one of the bettor PC Cobol Compiler's.  Another would be     www.flexus.com  .  Flexus makes a generic Cobol/OS screen product that allows many different compilers to interface to many different
operating systems (including the net) from standardized screen definitions. The web page has links to many Cobol vendors.

  Eoyore wrote in message <39CFF2BB.36AE9FE8@slic.com>...
  Can someone please tell me the current standard or 
  learning version of cobol? 
  Example- delphi 5.0 or access 2000 
  or vb or c++ 6.0 

  I wanted to get a head start on a class I might be taking in the 
  spring.  The teacher said he was changing compilers so 
  he couldnt tell me which text he was going to use yet.

  the teacher mean a new version was just released? 

  If there is more than one compiler, could I  be wasting 
  my time trying to learn with a cobol compiler not taught 
  in class? 

  Who makes Cobol? 
  Example Borland/Inprise makes Delphi. 

  Thanks for your help 
  Jim
```

<a id="t-dd2762d02a"></a>
## Thread: TinyCOBOL release 0.2 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### TinyCOBOL release 0.2

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39CF828A.AF79FEAB@arvotek.net>`

```

The TinyCOBOL compiler version 0.2 is available for download at the TC
home page (1) and Metalab (2).

This release contains some bugs fixes, and some code enhancements,
including updates to the IF/ELSE relational, class, name, sign
conditionals statements. The CALL BY VALUE has been added, as an
extension to the standard COBOL grammar.

Please report any bugs and/or problems to the TC mailing list. 



David

1) TinyCOBOL Home Page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/

2) Metalab
   http://metalab.unc.edu/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

```
In article <st09iee49unc2f@corp.supernews.com>, Karim <karims40@hotmail.com>
writes:

>> LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
>> Linking files ended.
>

I'm not 100% but I think that the FAQ may have something on this.
It is a common problem when people first get started using the FujiCOBOL
compiler.   Be sure that you have defined a MAIN program module.
Either in the IDE by selecting MAIN|CONSOLE or MAIN|WINDOWS from
the project manager or in the Compile Options definitions Add MAIN to
your compile options stored in DEFAULT.CBI
```

### ACU: Push-button with bitmap

- **From:** "Erlend Moen" <erlend.moen@disys.no>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qq8jg$a27$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`

```
Compiler: Acucobol 5.0 or higher.
OS: Windows

Is there a way to make a push-button with both a bitmap and text on it?
I want to make a 'cancel-button' with a cancel-icon at the left and the text
'Cancel' to the right.
```

#### ↳ Re: Push-button with bitmap

- **From:** "Matteo" <lena_sas@iol.it>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lf4A5.13754$3R6.273586@news.infostrada.it>`
- **References:** `<8qq8jg$a27$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`

```
working
           copy "acucobol.def".
           copy "acugui.def".
       77  bitmap-button                       pic  s9(9) comp-4.


screen section

           03  push-button                  line ??
                                            col ??
                                            size 16
                                            lines 15
                                            bitmap-handle bitmap-button
                                            bitmap bitmap-number = 5
                                            title "Cancel"
                                            self-act
                                            exception-value = ??.


procedure
             call "w$bitmap" using wbitmap-load,
                  "bmp\barra03.bmp",
                   giving bitmap-button.



by
matteo


"I computer sanno contare solo da 0 ad 1.  Il resto e' illusione."

"Erlend Moen" <erlend.moen@disys.no> wrote in message
news:8qq8jg$a27$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no...

> OS: Windows
>

text
> 'Cancel' to the right.

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Push-button with bitmap

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rZjcFXALK805IwhQ@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8qq8jg$a27$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <lf4A5.13754$3R6.273586@news.infostrada.it>`

```
The code that Matteo provides will produce a push-button with a bitmap, 
and the text "Cancel" as a 'tool-tip', which will pop-up (unless you 
configure it not to) after the mouse has been over the button for three 
quarters of a second, and will then disappear again (unless you 
configure it not to!) after four seconds.

The only way I am aware of to get the effect Erlend wants is to include 
the word 'Cancel' in the desired font within the bitmap.

regards

Nigel

In article <lf4A5.13754$3R6.273586@news.infostrada.it>, Matteo
```

```
What I do is to compile a CoBOL program with the compile listing to show
displacements on the side.  Then I save that in a file, and start
editing it by hand.
```

```
In article <meaA5.3396$Jm5.386170@news.total.net>,
SIJFG <sijfg96@total.net> wrote:
>How can I convert cobol copybook into easytrieve automaticaly?

No way that I know of; if someone else is aware of a utility they'd have
to decide to speak.  I use FileAid; I make an empty file of appropriate
LRECL, 1 track in size, and FileAid Edit it with the COBOL layout as a
map.  I then VFMT it and swap screens a lot to type it all in.

DD
```

## Thread: IBM: Announces new OS/390 (and VM) compiler — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM: Announces new OS/390 (and VM) compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qqt48$2n8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The "long expected" IBM announcement of their new release of their mainframe
compiler came out today.  See:

 http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_200-331

Some "high lights"

 * 31-digit numbers
 * TRUNC(BIN) performance improvements
 * integrated DB2 source "precompiler"
 * Unix System Service compile-time support
```

#### ↳ Re: Announces new OS/390 (and VM) compiler

- **From:** "Roland Schiradin" <schiradi@tap.de>

- **References:** `<8qqt48$2n8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
don't forget the very nice requirement
Display under CICS is now supported, even it works in a LE-environment.
Good job IBM
My special "Thank you" goes to Tom Ross.

Glad to see that Cobol doesn't follow the naming game!

I'll add support to CobAnal as soon as this  release is ready, expect at
least a on month period

Roland
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Announces new OS/390 (and VM) compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qtc9j$8es$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8qqt48$2n8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8qr6v5$2ns6$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de> <39d16d2d.8003352@207.126.101.100>`

```
No, It does NOT support screen section (under CICS, under ISPF, under IMS,
or even under Unix System Services or on the Workstation). IBM has been
"pretty clear" on the fact that they don't support this now - and PROBABLY
will not EVER support it.

It does add support for ACCEPT from DAY, DATE, etc but NOT from "sysin"
```

<a id="t-b73482bf00"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus Workbench/CICS option — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### MicroFocus Workbench/CICS option

- **From:** David Bretz <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D0CD20.B5A9727D@mescoma.com>`

```
I am having a problem with Microfocus WB and the CICS option.

I have recently received the 4.0 version of WB and CICS.  I have
installed WB, and tried to install CICS, but get an error.  I told
Merant, and they tell me that it's a y2k problem.

They send me a document that is supposed to contain instructions on how
to solve this problem.  It tells me to look on the Update CD for a
certain directory and download the information and run an update.  Well,
the directory does not exist on any of the Update CD's they sent.  

Then they tell me I can get into the FTP and download these 9 zip files,
unzip them and run an update.  But, the 9 files do not exist on the FTP.

Needless to say, I'm a bit frustrated....since I need this stuff
installed in order to do my job (I'm a consultant).

So....my question is this.......has anyone else had a similar
experience, and do you have a way to correct it??

FYI....I have the WB 4.0 CD, the CICS 4.0 CD, and the 4.0.38 update CD.

Thanks

Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Workbench/CICS option

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qqvg8$jut$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39D0CD20.B5A9727D@mescoma.com>`

```
From one of my usally reliable sources,

"The Workbench updates are still available on the FTP site at
ftp://ftp.merant.com/updates/ (which is the top level FTP directory), and at
ftp://ftp.merant.com/updates/cobol-workbench-v4.0/non-security-keyed/windows
-nt-95/
for the Non Security Keyed Windows 32 bit version of Workbench
update directory.  The Y2K problem with the Setup program is on all of the
CDs created up through the end of last year.  There is a newer CD that is
available that has corrected that Y2K problem, which has both the corrected
Setup program for the base v4.0 product and includes the v4.0.38 update on
it as well.  In the directory above are two files, fix2kset.txt &
fix2kset.zip, which include the correction for the Setup Y2K problem for the
previously delivered CDs.  The instructions for using the correction are in
the fix2kset.txt file.  Customers who wish to obtain a copy of the corrected
CD can do so by requesting a copy of that CD on the Update Request page on
our web site located at
http://support.merant.com/websupport/upgstore/mfcdupgrade.asp, by calling
the SupportNet Answerline group (1-800-443-1601, voice prompt 3) and
requesting one be shipped to them, or through asking their sales person
(1-800-547-4000, voice prompt 3) to have one shipped to them.  Obviously,
prior to sending a copy of the new CD out, we will need to check to make
sure that they had purchased the product to start with, as the Workbench
product is not license keyed (except for the security keyed versions sold
mostly outside of North America).

I'm not sure why Dave was not able to find the update on the FTP site, as it
has been there for quite some time.  We are looking at moving all of the old
product updates, however, to make it easier to find in the future.  We are
in the process of adding a new facility to the SupportNet Knowledgebase,
where we will incorporate both an Update Locator in its function, as well as
include links from the relevant Knowledgebase issues that are corrected in
product updates and have those entries have direct links to the product
updates (and patches, etc.) that correct those problems.  Once we have this
fully up and running (probably not until late October or early November),
then we will be removing the updates from the FTP site, with replacing them
with a notice to look to the Knowledgebase Update Locator from that point
forward."

"David Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com> wrote in message
news:39D0CD20.B5A9727D@mescoma.com...
> I am having a problem with Microfocus WB and the CICS option.
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dave
```

<a id="t-7495cd3d9d"></a>
## Thread: OT: Computers, and why they suck. — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Computers, and why they suck.

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qrmvs0dck@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
Sorry, had to get this in here.

I am still an intermediate programmer, but pretty strong on the mainframe.

We had an outage of one our company's main file servers.  The corporate
office suffered a 24 hour outage that was supposed to be 1 or 2 hours.  When
they brought it back up, the goddamn rights data was corrupted.  So all our
mainframe FTPs started failing.

I thought, just how many man hours of productivity are being lost here?  At
least Lotus Notes still worked.

We had a meeting with the director today about the outage and stuff.  I
didn't have anything to do with it.  So I just played dumb or whatever.  I
wanted to get some comments in there.  One of the systems people and myself
reminded the room of the strengths of the mainframe, and that such an outage
would be, shall we say, laughable?

So what I learned today was:

--->>> Data is the most important piece of any computer system.

--->>> The mainframe is not dead, and neither is COBOL.

--->>> Novell blows.

OK, outta here...
Jeff
```
