<a id="t-77239409ae"></a>
## Thread: Dear Mr. Dashwood — 2 msgs

### Dear Mr. Dashwood

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qq5lj$dm2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Dear Mr. Dashwood:

First, I respect Mr. Gavan for posting his elaborate OO thing.
I don't care about OO, but there are some who do.

I find it a little annoying that you spoil his thread with
descriptions about what you did in the 1970's and your low
opinions of the CLC and its denizens. Instead, you should have
made serious comments about his post, and, if you wished to
release steam you might have done it in a separate thread.
For this reason, I am not responding in Mr. Gavan's thread.

>And NO, I am NOT back in CLC...I was bored on a Sunday afternoon
>because everything is working and there are no outstanding problems to
>be looked at... <G>

I visited your website:
 (http://ourworld.compuserve.com/homepages/pete_dashwood/homepage.htm)
where it says Mr. Dashwood "devote(s) much of his time to participation
in the Compuserve and Internet Forums on the COBOL language,
particularly providing guidance and help to student programmers.".

Don't you think you should update this inaccurate statement?

>This NG is still desperately in need of moderating (just look at the
>thread on H1b...[and you edited my use of "horseshit" once,
>remember..??]) and until this happens it is unlikely to be of any real
>use to people who need it.

The best thing about the CLC is that is is NOT moderated.  I think it
is of 'real use' right now and you can see daily examples of that.

>The same people are saying the same things they were saying a year
>ago. Foodman, DocDwarf, YukonMama, nothing changes... Nobody looks
>forward, or wonders, or speculates or imagines or innovates,  just
>pedantic little arguments about outmoded and obsolete dinosaur
>technology.

Speaking for myself, the reason I use 'outmoded and obsolete'
technology is because it works. Earlier this year, you had the
opportunity to demonstrate your superior techniques in 'The
Great Programming Contest'. As I recall, you declined. Rather
than knocking things of which you disapprove, why not make a
genuine contribution to the CLC by posting samples of your
work, in particular, the time-clock thing talked about in the
GPC. I think you said you could do it in a few hours. I
did enter the contest and you can see my work using 'outmoded and
obsolete dinosaur technology' at foodman123.com/tcs.htm; now,
let's see yours.

>The same "voices of reason" (I won't embarrass them by naming them)
>pour oil on troubled waters and try to get some semblance of balance
>and sanity with simple, down to Earth advice, genuinely trying to be
>of help.

The best thing you could do would be to return to the CLC
and provide "guidance and help" to set an example for the
others.

>Hands up all those who have been helped by CLC? Now hands up all those
>who NEEDED help AFTER visiting CLC... ?!

I don't think there is the slightest question that people get real
help from a lot of very conscientious people.

>If it was a pub, I'd drink somewhere else...<G>

Sprinkling things with <G>s does not lessen the sarcasm
of your comments nor does it dignify them.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth






Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dear Mr. Dashwood

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T02:41:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qqcvp$jo6$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8qq5lj$dm2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
OK Mr Dilworth,

I have nothing better to do, so I'll stop for a (very quick) drink in the
"CLC and Chequebook".. your round?

Foodman wrote in message <8qq5lj$dm2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Dear Mr. Dashwood:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>opinions of the CLC and its denizens.

I didn't spoil his "thread"; it was a single post (actually, I didn't really
expect my post to be published as I have experienced  problems with posts to
this NG before...still, it was, so that's life.)

I like "denizens" ...that's a better word than I would have used...<G>
(BTW, when I use <G>, it does not indicate sarcasm, it indicates genuine
amusement (something I note is sadly lacking in your own posts...))

If you are annoyed, ask yourself "who's annoyed over this?" So, whose
problem is it?
(note the adroit sidestepping of an apology...I only say "sorry" when I
really am...)

> Instead, you should have
>made serious comments about his post
That's very fair comment, Tony, and I did.

I have posted a complete solution (including code) to Jimmy and if you are
really interested in his "thread" I'm sure if you ask him nicely, he'll give
you a copy.

>>
>, and, if you wished to
>release steam you might have done it in a separate thread.
>For this reason, I am not responding in Mr. Gavan's thread.
>
Do I detect note of petulance here? Why should ANYTHING I post have impact
on YOUR decision to respond? (why not just treat my comments with the
contempt you believe they deserve? I can certainly live with that...)

>>And NO, I am NOT back in CLC...I was bored on a Sunday afternoon
>>because everything is working and there are no outstanding problems to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, while you may consider my comments unpalatable (if not mischievous) I
don't knowingly tell lies... The statement stands. (And thanks for
increasing traffic to my site...<G>)

Ask people who are currently getting direct one-on-one help from me;
currently there are 3 and in the past 2 years the total is 24. (I do
actually spend many hours a week assisting these students. Why? Because I
love COBOL and it has been good to me. And it is refreshing and stimulating
to interact with people who have fresh viewpoints and actually want to
know.)
 BOTTOM LINE: I have nothing to prove. If the statement really WAS untrue
I'd delete it like a shot.

Actually, the Web Site IS alittle out of date because it says I am in the UK
and I'm writing this from New Zealand...but I'll be back next year and so
then it will be true again. I'm also planning a major update to it as soon
as I can find an ISP who'll let me run server-side code so it can be much
more interactive, and as soon as I can find some time from "providing
guidance and help to student programmers."...<LOL>
>
>>This NG is still desperately in need of moderating (just look at the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I respect your opinion, and your right to it.

I disagree, but perhaps, not totally and maybe I did overstate the
case...there is SOME good here, but there is also a lot of nonsense and
off-topic horseshit which dilutes the underlying merit. I know you don't
like what I'm saying but I call 'em like I see 'em, and it IS just one man's
opinion.

>>The same people are saying the same things they were saying a year
>>ago. Foodman, DocDwarf, YukonMama, nothing changes... Nobody looks
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>Speaking for myself, the reason I use 'outmoded and obsolete'
>technology is because it works.
Excellent! I have no problem with ANYBODY using what works for them (I do it
myself all the time...<G>)

I WOULD like to see some exploration of new ideas though...what is your
reaction to .NET for example? How do you feel about the new proposed COBOL
standards? What impact will it have on your existing applications? Why
should young people learn COBOL instead of JAVA? Do you see a need for COBOL
to offer Web Enablement? Why? Why not?

> Earlier this year, you had the
>opportunity to demonstrate your superior techniques in 'The
>Great Programming Contest'. As I recall, you declined.
Nope. I didn't decline. I offered to put my money where my mouth is if
anyone was prepared to call it. No one did. In retrospect, I'm kinda
glad...I used the time to develop a Windows application (in COBOL) which is
now generating revenue. Besides, it was a pretty futile exercise anyway.

>Rather
>than knocking things of which you disapprove, why not make a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Do you know ANYONE who DOESN'T knock the things of which they disapprove?
It's kind of fundamental to showing disapproval, isn't it?

BTW, I am amazed at your memory (even though it isn't completely
accurate...I said "5 days") you obviously considered the GPC to be a career
landmark...

>did enter the contest and you can see my work using 'outmoded and
>obsolete dinosaur technology' at foodman123.com/tcs.htm; now,
>let's see yours.
Hahaha! Just because you showed me yours DOESN'T mean I'll show you
mine..<G>
>
>>The same "voices of reason" (I won't embarrass them by naming them)
>>pour oil on troubled waters and try to get some semblance of balance
>>and sanity with simple, down to Earth advice, genuinely trying to be
>>of help.


>
>The best thing you could do would be to return to the CLC
>and provide "guidance and help" to set an example for the
>others.
>

Now, I suspect that THAT, is sarcasm...It's academic anyway...I'm not coming
back unless I see something of interest. Pissing contests like this are fun,
but at the end of the day, everybody just gets wet and the time could be
better spent.


>>Hands up all those who have been helped by CLC? Now hands up all those
>>who NEEDED help AFTER visiting CLC... ?!
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
"Conscientious" is not in question. "Effective" is. SOME people DO get help
and that is commendable. It's a question of "signal to noise" ratio. For me,
the noise level is too high (and I have to admit, this post is not
diminishing it...<G>)
>>If it was a pub, I'd drink somewhere else...<G>
>
>Sprinkling things with <G>s does not lessen the sarcasm
>of your comments nor does it dignify them.
>
I sprinkle things with <G> because I have a sense of humour (I realise this
might be a foreign concept to you <sorry, that WAS sarcasm...see the
difference...Oops! that was sarcasm too..oh Christ! NOW you've got me doing
it <G>) As for dignity, I abandon it on entry to this forum...

>Thanks
>
Don't mention it...

Summing up:

My opinions are just that...opinions. If you let them upset you, then ask
yourself why you would want or need MY approval. If anything I say actually
causes you to THINK (rather than just rankle) then the time I spent saying
it was well spent.

I know there's going to be lots of further posts to this "thread" because
some people here will take the same attitude that Tony has, and respond to
something  which was actually said with tongue in cheek, as if the fate of
nations depended on it.

I won't be responding.

Pete.
```

```
Well,

You see, the true difference between someone such as yourself and myself is
the maturity level.  While you may be immature and "high on yourself"

should add in my 0.02cents. :)
```

<a id="t-256a3399b0"></a>
## Thread: Maintenance - top 10 tasks — 40 this month / 82 total

### Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** richere@sympatico.ca (Robaire)
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```
Hello, Group

I have been offered to teach a COBOL course at the college level.
The emphasis is to be on maintenance.
I taught COBOL for several years in the early eighties, but I have
never done maintenance - I worked a lot in Pascal, and then in VB.

To be brief ( too late )  I would appreciate your help in identifying
the top 10 ( or more, or less ) tasks that occupy maintenance
programmers the most.
Knowing the percentage of effort dedicated to each tasks, or its
frequency of occurence,  would be a bonus for me.
I would then use that information as a basis for exercises in the
course.

Were you to  direct me to any sample code snippets or complete
programs that might be of use, or provide examples from your working
experience, I would be thrilled.

Of course, the results will then be available to anyone who needs
them.

Thanks for your help 

Robaire
```

#### ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zjxA5.1579$zG1.88026@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```

"Robaire" <richere@sympatico.ca>

Just on a single report:
1. Add a column of data not already present:
   a. When the requested additional column won't fit on the page
(possible solutions: 2 lines per detail, change orientation of the
paper, 2 different reports, abbreviate contents of one of the fields,
maybe others).
   b. When the requested data are not available from the file being
read (such as number of transactions that made up the total),
necessitating access to a second file.
2. The existing report has the title on the upper right, page number
on the upper left. Switch these locations on even numbered pages so
that the report can be printed on both sides of the paper.
3. The existing report's sub-total line sometimes appears at the top
of a page. Fix the report so that all the details appear on the same
page with their sub-total. Bonus points for handling the case where
there are more than one page of details per sub-total.
4. Report has details and subtotals. New report has subtotals only.
Vice-versa. Optionally either.

One we've had to deal with lately.

A field has grown (from 5 bytes to 6). The record layout cannot be
changed, but 4 slack bytes exist in the record. Your mission is to use
one of the four spare bytes to augment the existing 5. Modify an
update program to access this new field (which now lives in two
non-contiguous locations). Extra credit if the field is the record's
key. Bonus points if the solution is generally invisible to the next
programmer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qu9cj$kj7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <zjxA5.1579$zG1.88026@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
For this or any of the other "report" maintenance assingments, give them a
program that uses Report Writer - and have them modify it there.
(Particularly the case of having odd/even page headers).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qv2ma$65r$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <zjxA5.1579$zG1.88026@nnrp3.sbc.net> <8qu9cj$kj7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39d2b87d.4626637@news.cableamericahsa.com>`

```
As discussed previously in this NG, the COBOL "world" is divided into those
sites/programmers/applications that use RW all over the place and those that
never use it. It is PROBABLY more common (particularly in the PC world) NOT
to use it, but there is a SIGNIFICANT legacy set of applications with it
(and it will be required in the next Standard - not Optional - which MAY
increase its use).
```

```
will not look completely dumbfounded when asked about it in an

Robaire
```

#### ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D22AA1.EB543D38@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```


Robaire wrote:
> 
> Hello, Group
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> never done maintenance - I worked a lot in Pascal, and then in VB.

Hmmm.  That would vary by site.

Here are some ideas.

Have a program which prints a form, and change the form.

Include a structured and an unstructured program and have them do the
same maintenance on them both.

Have them create a program according to some specs one week - then tell
them what you REALLY want the next week.

You just discovered that that summary report gets hand copied into an
Excel workshop.  You mentioned that this can be automated - the user
wants you to automate.

Lots of maintenance is debugging.  Create a program which occasionally
truncates because an intermediate calc field is too small.  The user
finds our totals and his totals don't quite agree.  Or maybe the user
wants your total of percentages to equal 100%.

Your program is doing a pre-Y2K date compare with no centuries in its
data.

You decide to use an existing program for multiple uses, but it has a
hard-coded address in it.  That address needs to vary from user to user.

Have them change from using an internal table to a sorted file (data
amounts are big enough to make this a better solution than originally
designed).

You have a text based PC program.  The users want GUI.

You want a program to send e-mail when it aborts.

Have one of your programs which uses a PIC 9(9) SSN.  The user now wants
it on the report with XXX-XX-XXXX format.  Talk about why that picture
was bad only after they work on the problem.

Have a program which occasionally writes table records past it's table
size.  It used to work fine when the company was smaller, now it
sometimes aborts.
```

```
Robaire
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r26qq$gk0$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D22AA1.EB543D38@brazee.net> <39d27892.443921990@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D34DC2.6E6780DF@brazee.net> <39d493ba.581966863@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```

"Robaire" <richere@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:39d493ba.581966863@news1.qc.sympatico.ca...
> On Thu, 28 Sep 2000 07:55:14 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
> wrote:
>
 <snip>
> I'm glad that you clarified that - for a moment, I thought that COBOL
> programmers were taking a clue from C++ programmers, and using
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Extracting sub-strings" (if I understand what you mean by that) should be
refered to as using "reference modification" - and it is QUITE common in
current COBOL applications (but not in shops that still use '74 Standard
compilers). (Of course, you always could use the "POINTER" option of the
UNSTRING statement - but I don't think that is what you are talking about.)

Unfortunately (???), the use of POINTER data-types (and SET ADDRESS OF) is
also getting to be quite common.  It is a (fairly common) extension to the
'85 Standard - but it will be included in the next COBOL Standard (along
with such "traditionally un-COBOL" things as bit-manipulation and direct
memory allocating/freeing)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Pointers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D4A2E4.E7F4D327@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D22AA1.EB543D38@brazee.net> <39d27892.443921990@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D34DC2.6E6780DF@brazee.net> <39d493ba.581966863@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```
Robaire wrote:
> 
> I'm glad that you clarified that - for a moment, I thought that COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> all prevalent in the real world?

Pointers can be used in CoBOL.  They are primarily used to reference
things outside of the program (they can be used to find a DD name in
JCL, they can be used by subprograms written in C).  I believe PC
programmers use them more frequently due to memory constraints.  Someone
here will chime in who uses them more frequently than I do.

Extracting substrings can be done using reference modification.  This is
done in two places - where the original variable is not available for 05
levels:

display              ' Started ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (5:2)
                     '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (7:2)
                     '/' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:4)
                     ' ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (09:2)
                     ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:2)
                     ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (13:2).

The other time is when we are walking through a string:

PERFORM VARYING MY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
          UNTIL MY-INDEX > STRING-LENGTH
             OR MY-STRING (MY-INDEX: MY-INDEX + 1) = '**'
END-PERFORM.
```

```
> I'm glad that you clarified that - for a moment, I thought that
COBOL
> programmers were taking a clue from C++ programmers, and using

term
> now refers to a feature for extracting sub-strings - is their
use at

misleading.
In this case, it refers to an index or subscript.
```

```
a bunch of excellent problems, here is one more
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** richere@sympatico.ca (Robaire)
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Thu, 28 Sep 2000 00:00:11 GMT, Susan Allen
<susan.a.allen@boeing.com> wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>	I have the scars to prove I have solved them all ;-)

I definitely will include t. What I find particularly interesting is
that I can easily give different students different versions, and
therefore different problems, which they can then present to the
class.
I believe that they should learn to explain their work verbally to a
group as well as document it in the comments. This might help them
realise that the comments detailing their changes may not be as
obvious as they think they are.

Thanks for the idea, Susan.

Robaire
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u%1B5.11084$bD5.69266@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D22AA1.EB543D38@brazee.net> <39D28A0B.A72DFD6@boeing.com> <39d4985b.583152399@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```
"Robaire" <richere@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
> I believe that they should learn to explain their work verbally to a
> group as well as document it in the comments. This might help them
> realise that the comments detailing their changes may not be as
> obvious as they think they are.

There is another reason.  I suspect that most experienced programmers
can remember lots of times they asked for help on some bug, and while
describing the code to the other person, realized where the bug was.
It is interesting to see the expression on a newbie programmer's
face the first time an old warhorse programmer grabs them to help find
a bug. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Dc0B5.10929$bD5.68217@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39d39235.57317184@207.126.101.100>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
> I'll add one more item to Howard's excellent list.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> OWN code make them enhance someone elses.

Consider the programmers who did a good job on the initial program,
then had to revise a horrible program.  They hardly profit from their
good job.  Then the programmers who did a poor job and modified a well
structured program hardly realize the pain their poor job caused. ;-)
I can see basis for some inter-class hostility there. :-)

Actually, it's probably a good idea, it certainly would give them a
taste of what the 'rel world' is really like. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-09-30T04:24:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000930002451.00837.00000246@ng-fu1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Dc0B5.10929$bD5.68217@news3.atl>`

```
>From: "Judson McClendon" judmc@bellsouth.net 
>Date: 9/29/00 8:49 AM Eastern Daylight Time


>, it certainly would give them a
>taste of what the 'rel world' is really like. :-)
>--

Kind of reminds me of what came up at work today.  A new programmer was hired
about 2 weeks ago.  So far she's been doing small project to get used to the
tools available and procedures etc.  I think she said she has about 2 year
experience.  Anywho...

Today she was given part of the UGLY project.  Seems our invoice numbers are
turning over too fast for some of our customers who require the number be
unique in a rolling 12 month period for their own systems.  Since our customers
are bigger than we are (every say no to any of the Big 3 automakers?) we are
changing our invoice numbering scheme from 7 positions numeric to 7 positions
alphanumeric.  The first 3 positions indicate the shipping plant and the next 4
are a counter.  The counter is going from 0 thru 9999 to A000 thru Z999.

The crux of the matter is in a lot of programs this field is defined as numeric
9(7) and in others it is packed!  

We are also taking this oportunity to 'clean house'.  That is remove obsolete
code etc and make the jobs more efficient etc.

Today she received a program where the programmer used the same variable name
in several file layouts and coded by reference in the Procedure Division.  That
is:

MOVE INVOICE OF THE-RECORD TO INVOICE OF THE-OUTPUT
MOVE INVOICE OF THE-RECORD TO INVOICE OF THE-OTHER-OUTPUT 

and so on.  She was aghast.  She had heard of it but never seen it.  Welcome to
the real world.  You ain't seen notin yet.

Eileen
```

```
In article <20000930002451.00837.00000246@ng-fu1.aol.com>,
YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>Today she received a program where the programmer used the same variable name
>in several file layouts and coded by reference in the Procedure Division.  That
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>and so on.  She was aghast.

Hee, hee, hee... hack, *coff*... why, I, I too, am aghast. Firstly the
code is redundant to:

MOVE INVOICE OF THE-RECORD TO INVOICE OF THE-OUTPUT, THE-OTHER-OUTPUT.

... and secondly... how did that manage to get into Prod?  Wasn't there
*anyone* in that shop who could design things to use a Proper, Decent,
Righteous MOVE CORR?

(Today I saw, in the Prod source library, in Y2K-remediated code last
compiled with IGYCRCTL:

IF IN-SSN IS EQUAL TO 989898989 THEN READY TRACE.

It also contained the line:

DATE-WRITTEN.  DECEMBER 14 1976.)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d_lB5.107$Hs3.49381@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<Dc0B5.10929$bD5.68217@news3.atl> <20000930002451.00837.00000246@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000930002451.00837.00000246@ng-fu1.aol.com...
>
> Today she received a program where the programmer used the same variable
name
> in several file layouts and coded by reference in the Procedure Division.
That
> is:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and so on.  She was aghast.  She had heard of it but never seen it.
Welcome to
> the real world.  You ain't seen notin yet.

Ms. Preston, some of us (e.g., moi) prefer explicit qualification like this
to MOVE CORRESPONDING.

I do a lot of work for a large healthcare insurance company, writing code to
process electronic claims. The record format for these claims (NSF) changes
every couple of years, but generally by the addition or redefinition of a
small number of fields. We always end up with a bunch of conversion-type
stuff to do, and I find it makes future maintenance a lot easier when I use:

  MOVE field-1 of record-v3 to    field-1 of record-v4
  MOVE field-2 of record-v3 to    field-2 of record-v4
  STRING SYSTEM-CC DELIMITED BY SIZE
               date OF record-V3  DELIMITED BY SIZE
     INTO
               date of record-v4
  MOVE field-4 of record-v3  to field-4 of record-v4
  ...

(Typical example when a date field is expanded from YYMMDD to  CCYYMMDD)

. than to do a MOVE CORRESPONDING and then tinker with the changed fields.

Maybe it takes more typing the first time, but damn if this isn't worth its
weight in gold to the next poor schmuck (also, often moi) has to put in the
next round of changes.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T01:41:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D2A19F.654F90C2@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```
Robaire wrote:
> 
> Hello, Group
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> course.
<snip>

You might try something that I did when I was teaching COBOL.  I
explained very carefully to the students that they should use
meaningful names.  Their logic should be straight forward and the
program should include documentation to explain why they were
doing something different from what had been explained in class.

Now, to enforce this mantra (they heard it before each
programming assignment was given), here's what you do:  Give the
students a program to write ("read a card, write a line").  Next
week make the same program due, with headings and page numbers
and the source file (on diskettes since there are no card decks
these days).  Pass the respective source files out to the class
so that no one gets their own back and tell them that they must
fix this program [the source they've just gotten] to handle
control breaks -- this programming project will have two grades -
one for being able to make it happen, and the other to the
original writer as to how easy they made it to maintain their
code.

Now, *you* must be ready to read the code handed in by the
students and check it against the copy you handed out so you can
give the 2 grades.

Now, repeat the mantra prior to making program assignments, with
the idea that they won't know when you will repeat the exercise
(typically on the "final" program which included Report Writer
when I taught the class).

You will be amazed at how they write COBOL after that -- that is,
it will be easy to tell who will fail the class and who will pass
it.
```

```
Robaire
```

### Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

- **From:** DPH <kgclg15@kg.hsanet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net>`
- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com>`

```
Assuming the reports are true concerning age discrimination, then it is possible
that most or all of the employers would treat you badly when you reach a certain
age.  What do you do about that without a union / guild?  Open your own
programming shop?

What if you don't live in the top 100 population centers?  How would you feel
about moving away from family and friends, possibly repeatedly, to chase jobs
that last a few years until some pointy haired boss gums up the works again?  Is
it worth the sacrifices demanded by a nomadic life just to avoid joining a labor
organization that might have the power to change things to what they ought to be?

DPH

> Can employees in IT not do that?
```

```
Unions have a pretty good record of successfully opposing unfair labor
practices.  These things ought to be opposed.  Age discrimination is one such
practice that ought to be opposed.  <you name it> discrimination ought to be
opposed.  Big business has always been good at <you name it> discrimination.  IT
people can pretty much take care of themselves in the relm of salaries, but the
IT guy that gets thrown out on his ear just for having one too many birthdays
can't help himself at all.  That's were unions / guilds / somotherorganization
shine(s).

DPH
```

```
DPH wrote:
> 
> Unions have a pretty good record of successfully opposing unfair labor
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> shine(s).

Surely you don't believe the guilds came into being to suppress
discrimination & to insure 4 weeks vacation for their members? They came
into being to restrict entry into their specialty, e.g., silversmith, by
requiring a lengthy apprenticeship with a master, finding a master as a
sponsor, etc. It was a way to create a monopoly at the labor end of the
economy, which enabled the guild, of course, to control the price paid
to their members.

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** DPH <kgclg15@kg.hsanet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39D34CDB.6641BF11@kg.hsanet.net>`
- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <Kd9B5.1468$QF1.222186@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39D2B62D.8F2515E@kg.hsanet.net> <39D57BE6.26AF23A9@optonline.net>`

```
I don't know the history of unionism, but it would appear from your description that
the guilds were doing 2 of the functions that some unions still do - ensure excellence
in the work and keep the salaries high.  If not every Tom, Dick, and Harry is able to
set up shop as a silversmith, the ones that have proved they know what they're doing
are more likely to provide a superior product.  The public learns that the wares
produced by a real silversmith are of high quality, and so they can command a higher
price.

It seems to be a lot like some of the unions, such as the IBEW, which fosters
education amongst its members and, I believe at least among the electricians and some
other trades, still has an apprenticeship program.  These union members are known for
their higher knowledge of the job.

I think all this is as it should be.

DPH

Liam Devlin wrote:

> DPH wrote:
> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> LiamD
```

```
DPH wrote:
> 
> What if you don't live in the top 100 population centers?  How would you feel
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> organization that might have the power to change things to what they ought to be?

So people go to a lot of hardship to keep from having unions help them? 
I don't think so.  Given free choice, people either join or avoid unions
on for selfish reasons.  If they think a union will significantly
benefit them and their future, they will pay the money and accept what
the union has to offer.  If they think the union will hamper them, they
will fight the union.  If they believe the union will have no real
effect, they will choose to keep the money.

If your pointy hair boss follows union rules, your union isn't going to
make your job pleasant.  But if you move to a different job with its
union, you won't have seniority anymore, and will have to start at the
bottom rung.  Having a set of advancement rules which are based upon
service time rather than being innovative and learning new skills and
which apply to everyone is an improvement over cronyism, but it is not
an improvement over what we have in the computer industry today.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> So people go to a lot of hardship to keep from having unions help them?
> I don't think so.

Agreed.

> Given free choice, people either join or avoid unions
> on for selfish reasons.  If they think a union will significantly
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> effect, they will choose to keep the money.

Personally, I would avoid joining a union at virtually any cost, for
reasons of principle, just as I would avoid joining the Communist
Party.  As far as I'm concerned, there is little difference.  Both do
almost precisely the reverse of what they claim, and both stifle any
initiative, and therefore productivity.  People, such at the members
of the truckers unions who shoot at men they don't even know, who are
simply trying to work and to support their families, have absolutely
no concept of what 'personal freedom' and 'free enterprise' mean.
Does anybody remember the union fool who shot from an overpass into
a semi loaded with dynamite on an Interstate highway in the U.S. a
number of years ago?  It blew out a huge section of the Interstate,
and of course they never found anything of the poor innocent driver.
To see the end result of union thinking, just watch as members in
picket lines throw bricks at cars and firebomb people's homes, for
trying to do get to work.  Talk about tyranny!  I never heard of a
company forcing violence on anyone who didn't want to work for them,
but many are forced to join unions, or forced away from their jobs
against their will at threat of violence.  What kind of 'freedom'
or 'benefit' is that?  Shades of Joseph Stalin and the KKK!  Anybody
ever heard of Jimmy Hoffa?  How many times have any of you had to
sit for hours at an airport, or miss flights, because of strikes?
How many of you have had trouble getting to work, or getting a
package delivered because of strikes?  Millions and millions of
people, totally uninvolved with union issues, have suffered again
and again because of union greed and stupidity.  If something is
good, it bears good fruit.  Just look what wonderful fruit unions
produce!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

- **From:** DPH <kgclg15@kg.hsanet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39D2B90D.1926787D@kg.hsanet.net>`
- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net> <qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl>`

```
So you picked one of the most violent, corrupt unions around for your
example.  Why not choose instead another well-know union, the Screen Actors
Guild.  Have you seen Arnold or Sly shoot at the CEO of Paramount or MGM?  Do
the old guys with seniority make the most money in show buisness without
regard to anything but their longevity?

Even skilled workers need unions to keep the big business hacks (PHBs?) in
check.  Without Unions, factory workers would still be earning pennies per
hour.  They don't like it, they can quit and go to another factory that pays
2 cents more per hour?  Big whoop.  There wouldn't be a middle class without
unions - that's the way it was heading until unionization hit the country.
Virtual slave labor, child labor (because the familes had to contribute the
kids to the workforce just to eat), and so forth.  Gordon Gecko's "Greed is
Good" motto lives in corprate boardrooms, and without unions, would have seen
America go the way of Mexico, with a small, rich elite class and a huge,
impoverished remainder.

DPH

Judson McClendon wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> >
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

```
losers) milling about with their printed picket signs (they are too lazy to
make their own) expecting people to honk in support I feel like flipping the
assholes off or disposing of my garbage in their direction. If there is ever
a conveniently placed mud puddle by a bunch of strikers I plan to make good
use of it. If I see "Union-Made" on a piece of merchandise I don't buy it but
head to the nearest known import.

Unions are hypocritical parasites. Nothing more.
```

```
> Personally, I would avoid joining a union at virtually any cost, for

> Party.  As far as I'm concerned, there is little difference.  

"I consider that every workman is well advised to join a trade union. I
cannot conceive how any man standing undefended against the powers that
be
in this world could be so foolish, if he can possibly spare the money
from
the maintenance of his family, not to associate himself with an
organization to protect the rights and interests of labour." 
-- Winston Churchill.

Churchill is not known to have held similar opinions about Communism.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Party.  As far as I'm concerned, there is little difference.

Communists believe in central control.  Unions take away from central
control.  However, the Soviets did support unionization in the west, to
create instability in the west.  This probably causes the confusion.
Neither communism, nor any other totalitatian regime, can tolerate multiple
independently elected groups and associations.  To exist, totalitarians have
to subvert or destroy unions.

The communists also supported the ecology movement in the west, while their
own countries endured outrageous levels of pollution.  The soviet communists
were very hypocritical (and so are the Chinese ones as far as I'm
concerned).  The Soviets supported movements in the west that would not have
been tolerated had communists come to power here.  They supported disruptive
movements here solely in order to bring disruption to the west.

> Both do
> almost precisely the reverse of what they claim, and both stifle any
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> and of course they never found anything of the poor innocent driver.


Does anyone remember the anarchist, NRA supporter, born-again-christian guy
who blew up the Federal Building in Oklahoma?

Are you saying that we should assume that all members of a group support the
nutbar actions of any single member?

> To see the end result of union thinking, just watch as members in
> picket lines throw bricks at cars and firebomb people's homes, for
> trying to do get to work.

At which GM plant or which Toyota plant did that happen?  What TV channel?
Which country?

Few union members support violence.  It can only occur in times and places
where the union is not subject to democratic elections, or where thugs are
allowed to move in and take over ignorant poorly educated people.

The Screen Actors Guild has never engaged in violence.  None of the many
airline pilots associations in the US has ever engaged in violence.

The Teamsters Union engaged in violence back in the 50s and 60s.  But
independant truckers have engaged in violence too.

Furthermore, nobody supports the inaction of Firestone in its SUV tire
recall, and the many dozens of deaths that ensued.

That doesn't mean we lump all companies in with the actions of a few.

We don't knock capitolism even when a major company commits a major wrong in
this country this week.

We look and we say that, that specific company did that specific thing
wrong.

And that is how unions should be treated.

>Talk about tyranny!  I never heard of a
> company forcing violence on anyone who didn't want to work for them,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

Today Jesus would be a union leader.

>
>
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> Personally, I would avoid joining a union at virtually any cost, for
> reasons of principle,

So would I.

> just as I would avoid joining the Communist Party.

Interesting comparison:-) Again, so would I.

>  As far as I'm concerned, there is little difference.  Both do
> almost precisely the reverse of what they claim, and both stifle any
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> company forcing violence on anyone who didn't want to work for them,

You mean beating the crap out of someone who turned down a job offer?
Odd point here, IMHO.

Ever hear of a company using violence to suppress union activity? Henry
Ford Harry Bennett who hired goons in the 30's to fire machine guns into
crowds of striking workers to break up the strikes (Ford was
pro-management <g>). Killed & maimed a lot of people, plus terrorized
the rest, which was the whole point. I suspect management is more
cautious in recent times because unions have legal recognition and
shooting into crowds is something even Charlton Heston *might* disagree
with. If you're interested, you might also look into what happened to
the IWW (the "Wobblies" back in the late teens). 

I do not disagree, BTW, with your point about how useless & bloodsucking
the unions are, just another hierarchy to bleed the workers. 

LiamD
```

## Thread: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos — 4 msgs

### Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

- **From:** "keith smyth" <k17731NO@SPAMMhome.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T03:20:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<QHdA5.6985$583.294276@news1.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com>`

```
(One day I'll remember to cross post correctly.  Sorry again.)

"Mark Johnson" <mjohnson@sectech.com> wrote in message
news:39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com...
>
> >  Do you folks think the Programmers Guild is going anywhere?
>
>    No, of course not. They are just a bunch of racist yahoos that keep
shooting
> themselves in the foot online.
>

I dislike racist yahoos too.  I don't know if they are fellow professionals
or a bunch of hangers on, but I see too many embarassing red neck postings
with ridiculous generalizations and stereotypes about people the writer
knows little about.

So you are saying the Programmers Guild is a bunch of people who are all
like that?

Good thing folks like us don't make racist generalizations.

I don't know how you feel, but I feel that racist actions that cause damage,
personal loss, family breakdown, and pain are more serious than casual
remarks (or even serious statements) by dummies, even if it is a large group
of dummies.

To me it is more racist to bring someone in on an H1B, tell him or her that
you will get them a green card, never give them a pay raise even though they
are progressing from junior to senior, deliberately string them on for six
years, and then send them back.

And yes, you and I both know a few Indians this didn't happen to.  That it
doesn't always happen proves nothing.

I know several that it did happen to, and that it happens proves it happens.

I also know one person who has a relative in the USA on an H1B for computers
that does not have a 4 year degree in a computer related field, and the
relative did not do any falsification.  So in at least one case, the
educational credentials were falsified, presumably by the agency.  I have
read that this is not infrequent.

This doesn't mean we should or should not have H1Bs.  I have no opinion on
that.

It means *some* agencies and/or sponsors are commiting fraud and they should
be stopped.

I can tell you that the popular press in India is unhappy with that
countries top brains being drained off.  India is not a wealthy country and
it cannot afford to subsidize the educations of so many people who are just
going to leave.  It is a popular topic in their papers apparently.

But the way things stand, many of those top brains are going to get good
training and experience, and then return home.

So the concerns of their press may be unwarrented.

I'd like to see everyone, American, Indian and anything else, treated fairly
and with respect.

H1Bs have nothing to do with unionization.  That is why I posted my reply to
you on this issue in this new thread.

- Keith
```

<a id="t-f44451578e"></a>
## Thread: EXTFH and BTRIEVE — 5 msgs

### EXTFH and BTRIEVE

- **From:** "Markus Carl" <carl@ics-khatib.de>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qsa7t$qug$1@news.online.de>`

```
Hello,
I have a problem reading a BTRIEVE file by calling EXTFH. When I call EXTFH
with special code "06" (return file information) i get the information that
the file is BTRIEVE / ANSI format. This works.
But when i want to open the file calling code "00" (open input) or "02"
(open I-O) i get status 9/141 (already open).
I tried to close the file --> status 9/142 (not open) !
The error occured with NetExpress 3.1.11. The program works with workbench
4.0.32.
BTRIEVE version is 6.15.

Thank you for your help,

Markus
```

#### ↳ Re: EXTFH and BTRIEVE

- **From:** Maurice Hickey <mhickey@dtn.ntl.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D1B383.E5E0F701@dtn.ntl.com>`
- **References:** `<8qsa7t$qug$1@news.online.de>`

```
Make sure you set the FCD-OPEN-MODE to 128 before the call to EXTFH to
open the file.

Markus Carl wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Markus
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXTFH and BTRIEVE

- **From:** "Markus Carl" <carl@ics-khatib.de>
- **Date:** 2000-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qso0i$vjv$1@news.online.de>`
- **References:** `<8qsa7t$qug$1@news.online.de> <39D1B383.E5E0F701@dtn.ntl.com>`

```
Yep, now it works !
Thanx,

Markus
```

<a id="t-2ecb1f198d"></a>
## Thread: GO TO and THROUGH — 20 this month / 21 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** "Floyd Johnson" <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
I suspect that this question may be one that may start a religious war, but
I want to ask.  I am using Thane Hubbell's book.  In chaptes 10 and 11 he
introduces the GO TO and THROUGH keywords.  Though my training in COBOL goes
back to 1966/67  when I first used COBOL as a high school student in an
Explorer Post sponsored by IBM, my professional exposure has been minimal.
I am currently teaching a "File Processing with COBOL" course for sophomore
CS students  where COBOL is their second language (there first was one year
of JAVA and data structures).

Having said that, it would seem to me that these two keywords (GO TO and
THROUGH) lend themselves to unstructured and potentially difficult to debug
programs.  My gut feeling (given my strong background in structured
programming) would be to discourage their use in new programs, but to make
sure that students understood their use so they can read and maintain
program that contain these constructs.

I would stop there and not even ask the question - except I trust the text
and the fact that the author has chosen to include these topics in the midst
of other important concepts would give them greater significance than I
would in the classroom.  I want to make sure that I am not missing
something.

Thanks for any advice or comments.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<npRA5.232$Rd6.65229@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```

"Floyd Johnson" <floydj@netins.net

> I am currently teaching a "File Processing with COBOL" course for
sophomore.....

You illustrate the difference between a book an a teacher. In a book,
everything is in the same size (usually) type and, to the reader, all
things look equally important.

A teacher, on the other hand, can emphasize or diminsh topics
according to their real-world significance.

In a book on COBOL, the author just has to mention GO TO and THRU (and
maybe even ALTER) because they are a part of the language. You job, as
a teacher, is to assign relative values to these topics. You are
correct in your initial impression that GO TO (et al) sucks big time.
Please inform your students accordingly.
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r0o7c02adu@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
I would say that Shop Standards are the key in the workplace.  Use whatever
constructs you'd like, just don't go too far outside the box.  PERFORM THRU
EXIT (which we shall now call PTE, or the PTE Instruction) is the better
choice if that will better comply with shop standards.

Just as with internal sort, the PTE and GOTO verbs can be used very
effectively, but usually it's better to stay a little cleaner if it won't
compromise the system too much.

I also work in PowerBuilder.  It does not have a GO TO verb.  That fact does
not inherently promise, or deliver, better code.

There are situations where different contructs work better.  I could build
you a small system using VSAM, internal sorts, GO TOs, PTEs, workfiles
galore, etc.  and it would fly and be very efficient.  But I'd rather have
it as a flat filed, externally sorted program without GO TOs or PTE.

It just depends.

Jeff

----------
In article <8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>, "Floyd Johnson"
<floydj@netins.net> wrote:


> I suspect that this question may be one that may start a religious war, but
> I want to ask.  I am using Thane Hubbell's book.  In chaptes 10 and 11 he
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D34C6D.6C731CE6@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:
> 
> I suspect that this question may be one that may start a religious war, but
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> program that contain these constructs.

I hate GO TO and THROUGH constructs.  But I have to work with programs
which were written using both constructs.   So I agree with you
completely.

There are people who are advocates of both commands, but only to allow
for a GO TO THIS-PARAGRAPH-EXIT which is a dummy paragraph at the end of
a THRU.  They want a way to exit a paragraph without switches.   With
the next version of COBOL, this will no longer be necessary, as there
will be commands to exit paragraphs, sections, and performs.

And there is one person on this newsgroup who likes GO TO in standard
in-line code.  I wouldn't be surprised if there is another person out in
the industry who agrees with him.   He is a developer who doesn't have
to maintain other people's code, which makes a big difference in which
standards work best.
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%tIA5.9005$bD5.57423@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
"Floyd Johnson" <floydj@netins.net> wrote:
>
> Having said that, it would seem to me that these two keywords (GO TO and
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> something.

You will find most of the experienced programmers around here in close
agreement with you on those points, including Thane Hubbell, who will
probably respond himself.  However, there are some programmers here who
advocate PERFORM THRU for various reasons.  Personally, there is only
one reason why I use either construct in COBOL.  In some of the systems
I write, there is a need for paragraphs containing long series of 'IF'
statements, any of which, if true, should exit the paragraph.  If COBOL
had the EXIT PARAGRAPH construct proposed in the draft standard, it
would be perfect for this purpose (most structured languages provide
an 'exit block' statement).  Also, two of my largest clients still use
COBOL 74, so the END-xxx constructs aren't available.  For this reason,
on these systems I use PERFORM xxx THRU xxx-EXIT and GO TO xxx-EXIT.
As you note, this can be a dangerous practice, so I use a code checker
program that enforces the PERFORM and THRU paragraphs match, there are
no intermediate paragraph names, and it flags any GO TO outside the
paragraph.  But my situation is certainly not typical, I don't use GOTO
in any other language, and will drop PERFORM THRU and GO TO as soon as
EXIT PARAGRAPH is available. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D36FE9.EE8BA39C@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
2000-09-28 17:17:17 MESZ

Floyd Johnson schrieb:

...

I hate GOTOs in every language. But now and
then on encounters a Situation where there
is a need to use it.

For instance, recently I wrote a routine
which inserts some hundred words into a search
tree. The words were alphabethically  ordered,
and inserting them in this order would create
a degenerated tree - functional but not
good for performance. On the other hand,
I did not want to reorder these insertions
for the sake of maintenace.

So I built a GOTO-structure which made
sure that all those insertions are executed
exactly once, but not in the order in which
they are written down. Now the tree
looks fine and is nearly balanced.

That was the only case in which a GOTO was
justified, in my eyes, in 20 years of
programming.

Herwig
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D39BA1.8D5FF53@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:
> 
> I suspect that this question may be one that may start a religious war, but
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> of JAVA and data structures).
<snip>

You are absolutely right, this will start a religious war.

Here is the problem - If the only tool you have is a hammer, then
all problems appear to be nails.  Sometimes management takes away
a tool (yeah, think out of the box) and that causes some
problems.  Let me give some examples:

I have worked in many different environments -- some that
prevented [artificially] any one from using GO TO.  All
paragraphs had to be performed (top down structured).  I finally
won an exception for my code, because I was doing CICS work. 
Once I showed them that I could be very structured using GO TO,
and that my code used much less of CICS' resources that way (read
that, ran much faster, paged less), they allowed the use of GO TO
in CICS programs.  They even accepted the idea of "Fall Through"
code -- NO PERFORMS ALLOWED (except for a search of a table or
something similar).  It ultimately bought them 20% of their CPU
and PAGE delays in their CICS regions which delayed a CPU
upgrade.

I have also seen shops that did not allow Report Writer, because
their management didn't understand it.  Same was true of
DECLARATIVES!  Get them to accept R/W, and they are amazed at how
much productivity you can get from your programmers.  Establish a
base program and use it to create your reports.  4 hours from
specs to production (almost as fast as I can do it w/ RPG!).  

Now, enter some third party software that you have to interface
with (CICS, DL/I, DL/1, IMS, IDMS, etc.), and suddenly, you have
to allow for all kinds of interesting constructs.  Why?  Because
the implementation of the product you are interfacing with forces
it upon you.

How would you like to be called by some routine, that requires
you to call it back?  Why?  Because you can't have multiple entry
points in your code.  So to fix this, you have to call the caller
back so you can pass it the function and paragraph name within
your code so it can call you back directly!  Now, go ahead, do a
perform and try to figure out where you come down on this one! 
Granted, what I'm talking about is rather dated (COBOL '74 and
IMS/DC), but, these are some of the things that dictate how you
must write a program. 

Now, let's take the "THRU" (or "through") construct and examine
it.  In the days of yore, we had sections within the procedure
division and optionally they were numbered (back in the REAL or
CORE based machine days, you had the system build you overlays
for really large programs...).  If you did not specify the THRU
in one place and did in another, well, in ye old IBM terminology,
"the results are unpredictable", particularly when you had GO
TO's embedded.  [I think this is what got the "GO TO" -less code
tirade started.]  So, some shops made it mandatory that you must
always use a "THRU" and you must always use "******-EXIT.  EXIT."
constructs so it was obvious where you ended execution.

So back to where I started.  If the only tool you have is a
hammer, all problems look like nails.

Now with pointers, EVALUATE, END-IF, and the other niceties that
we currently have in COBOL, I don't write code the way I use to
when we were told we had to use the 68 or 74 standards.  But I
still, to this day, use THRU and GO TO to make it obvious where a
paragraph and/or block of code end.  But then, my language of
choice is S/3x0 ALC, and using Priv-Ops (ok, so I'm the wacky sys
prog who writes in RPG, COBOL, and other H/L Languages -- I try
to pick the tool that matches the job).
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** David Bretz <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D461C9.7B57D5D6@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
Actually, I use GO TO and THRU to create very structured programs
(actually, modular programs).  For Example:


Procedure Division.

0000-MAIN-PARAGRAPH.
     PERFORM 1000-PARAGRAPH THRU 1000-EXIT.
     PERFORM 2000-PARAGRAPH THRU 2000-EXIT.
     PERFORM 3000-PARAGRAPH THRU 3000-EXIT.
     STOP RUN.
0000-EXIT.  EXIT.

1000-PARAGRAPH.
     PERFORM 1100-PARAGRAPH THRU 1100-EXIT.
     PERFORM 1200-PARAGRAPH THRU 1200-EXIT.
 
     IF SOME CONDITION
        GO TO 1000-EXIT.
  
      PERFORM 1300-PARAGRAPH THRU 1300-EXIT.
1000-EXIT.  EXIT.

1100-PARAGRAPH.
1100-EXIT.  EXIT.

ETC

ETC


I think you get the idea.  I always use PERFORM THRU and never GO TO
outside a performed paragraph.  Each paragraph in one of my programs
does one function, and I mearly combine the functions to make a complete
program.

Dave Bretz


Dave Bretz

Floyd Johnson wrote:
> 
> I suspect that this question may be one that may start a religious war, but
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Floyd Johnson
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hA5B5.41720$A4.1314587@news1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net> <39D461C9.7B57D5D6@mescoma.com>`

```
Procedure Division.

0000-MAIN-PARAGRAPH.
     PERFORM 1000-PARAGRAPH.
     PERFORM 2000-PARAGRAPH.
     PERFORM 3000-PARAGRAPH.
     STOP RUN.

1000-PARAGRAPH.
     PERFORM 1100-PARAGRAPH.
     PERFORM 1200-PARAGRAPH.
     IF SOME CONDITION
         PERFORM 1300-PARAGRAPH
     END-IF.

1100-PARAGRAPH.

My style, such as it is...
I don't use GO TO, but I don't think about it much.

I feel that THROUGH implies that there's an intermediate way out of the
performed routine, as does SECTION.  By avoiding GO TO, there's no need for
either THROUGH or SECTION, simplifying the code.

Many times, you see a paragraph that has 600 lines of code, peppered with a
few routines that make a decision, then jump past the rest of the code to
the end.  My way is to attempt to isolate the decisions, then PERFORM
straight-line routines as a result of each decision.

But... as a reformed spaghetti programmer (started in FORTRAN), I'm always
on the lookout for better ideas ;-)

Cliff

----------------------------------------
> Procedure Division.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1100-EXIT.  EXIT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g1nr1z0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net> <39D461C9.7B57D5D6@mescoma.com>`

```
On Fri, 29 Sep 2000 05:32:57 -0400, David Bretz wrote:

>Actually, I use GO TO and THRU to create very structured programs
>(actually, modular programs).  For Example:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I do  more or less the same, but using SECTION istead of PERFORM THRU
Your program would look like this

MAIN SECTION.
MAIN-10..
     PERFORM SECT-1000.
     PERFORM SECT-2000.
     PERFORM SECT-3000.
     STOP RUN.
MAIN-EXIT.  EXIT.

SECT-1000 SECTION.
SECT-1000-10.
     PERFORM SECT-1100.
     PERFORM SECT-1200.
 
     IF SOME CONDITION
        GO TO SECT-1000-EXIT.
  
      PERFORM SECT-1300.
SECT-1000-EXIT.  EXIT.

SECT-1100 SECTION.
SECT-1100-EXIT.  EXIT.

The result is exactly the same, but I like the implicit hierarchy.

Of course, using this style PERFORM should only target SECTION and GO TO only
jumps to paragraphs. A cross-reference of the compilation also gives you an
easy tool to do some logic checking as well.

As always, just my opinion




Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QV1B5.11071$bD5.69303@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net> <39D461C9.7B57D5D6@mescoma.com> <39D4944C.8E9D155C@brazee.net>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> That's perfectly structured.  Some shops have syntax checkers to make
> sure there are no typos, but I have actually seen a job working
> (incorrectly) in production where someone cut and pasted a GO TO
> 1100-EXIT, and forgot to change it to GO TO 1200-EXIT.

This is why I believe a program to check for this type of error is
essential if you are using PERFORM THRU and/or GO TO.  When I run my
checker on programs where my clients have been lax in using it, it
almost invariably finds PERFORM THRU the wrong exit, or GO TO outside
the paragraph, caused by exactly what you describe, or mistyping.
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:26:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39d3df9e.88744447@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
The "correct" standard is the one your shop enforces.  We need
programmers that know how to follow shop rules and how to maintain
both styles.

Students need to be exposed to both standards.  My shops code is about
80% PERFORM-THRU/GOTO-EXIT style, and 20% is coded without THRU and
without GOTO.  (We outlawed THRU/GOTO about 8 years ago.)

I've seen it mentioned already in this thread, but some times GO TO is
still justified for pure performance reasons, primarily in mainframe
online transaction systems where every fraction of a second is
considered important, and can slow down other users on the same
system.

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T03:52:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39d41084.4876211@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
At great risk of continuing yet ANOTHER useless GO TO war, here are MY
comments quoted from Page 163 of Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24
Hours.  

------
COBOL, like most other programming languages, has a GO TO statement.
The Go To causes the program to jump to the Paragraph title or Section
header specified in the Go To statement.  As with Perfrom ... Thru,
the advances in the COBOL language with the 1985 standard have
eliminated any need to use Go To.  However, it seems to be the "easy
way out" for many programmers, and I think it is worthwhile to spend
some time explaining its use and abuse.
------

I'm back again.  I wanted to give the reader a complete picture - I go
on to illustrate the use of GO TO and to talk about falling through
code and the like.

Now a personal opinion - as if the above wasn't enough -

If I am writing a NEW program I will not use GO TO.  I don't code to
avoid it -- my style just doesn't require it's use -- I'm not even
tempted.  I will try to use OO if the environment supports it.  That
is my latest style.

However, if I am maintaining code, I will stick with the existing
style of the code.  If Perform ... Thru is used, or GO TO and exit is
used, I will conform to that "standard".  Mixing styles never does the
programmer that follows you any good.

On Thu, 28 Sep 2000 09:11:10 -0400, "Floyd Johnson"
<floydj@netins.net> wrote:

…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Program Logic???? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Program Logic????

- **From:** "Colonel" <psxzone@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dVKA5.27466$HK6.601806@east7.usenetserver.com>`

```

  Hi guys. I was wondering if anyone can help.

System : Fujitsu 3 on win98 platform

All I`m doing is a simple exercise to update a master file. I have a
transaction file, master file and a stock file. The transaction and master
files
are both in Line Sequential and stock file is indexed. All files are sorted
before this program is run. The program uses information within each
transaction record and this information is a pointer to a record within the
stock file.A simple calculation is done with the stock file info etc etc.
I`m sure you all know what I mean. If there is no match between the
transaction file and the stock file an error is reported. The problem is I
can`t get the following extract of code to work. The compiler complains "
Conditional statement 'WRITE' is specified where imperative statement must
be specified. WRITE statement assumed valid. ". Is there anyone who knows
what is happenning as I have been advised the syntax is correct???? I`ve
tried writing an individual MOVE then WRITE , also I`ve tried removing the
AFTER 1 but problem still persists! Any ideas????

Thanks in advance

Colonel
psxzone@btinternet.com

UPDATE-TRANSACTION.
           READ stock-file
              INVALID KEY
                 WRITE PRINT-REC FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER 1    <<<<compiler
complains about this line
              NOT INVALID KEY
                 do a calculation
            END-READ
```

#### ↳ Re: Program Logic????

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qvurv$nao$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dVKA5.27466$HK6.601806@east7.usenetserver.com>`

```
Am I correct that the file associated with PRINT-REC is a (line) sequential
file?  If so, it is the compiler that is wrong and not you.  The "solution"
to get around this is to add an END-WRITE statement immediately after the
WRITE statement that you have already.

If the compiler were working "correctly" - then the NOT INVALID KEY phrase
would be REQUIRED to go with the READ statement (because it is only valid
for an indexed file).  However, it APPEARS from the message that the
compiler thinks that you are allowed to have a NOT INVALID KEY phrase for
your (sequential) WRITE statement.

Can any of the Fujitsu "watchers" in this group, check this out with V3,
V4.2, and V5 - to see when/if Fujitsu fixed this problem.

NOTE WELL:
  If the WRITE statement were to an indexed file, then the compiler would be
correct in saying that you had a "conditional" WRITE where an imperative one
is required/expected.
```

```

It now works.

Colonel
```

```
<fixxit@bright.net> wrote in message
news:msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com...
> "keith smyth" <k17731NO@SPAMMhome.com> wrote:

>http://heather.cs.ucdavis.edu/pub/Immigration/ImmigAndComputerIndustry/SVRe
p
> >ort.html

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I've done it too and I'm sure I'll do it again.  But, H1-Bs cannot do that
nearly as easily as I can.  They are indentured to their sponsors and are
sometimes threatened with being sent back to their home country.  That is
the big problem with H1-B.

The unindentured American workers would look a lot more appealing to the
employers that pay for the H1-B legislation if the H1-Bs had the same level
of "unindenturedness".  Indentured H1-Bs are very appealing to employers and
that is the part that the IT companies paid good money for.

If they removed the indentured part from the H1-B legislation, all of a
suddene wouldn't be such a big push for increases.

-Barry
```

```

"Frank Ney" <croaker@barkingmad.org> wrote in message
news:cjf9tsc3jmkik622kks3og1lf9v5i0orng@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 29 Sep 2000 10:32:33 -0500, an orbiting mind control laser caused
> "Barry Smith" <barry@lincolnplatingNOSPAM.com> to write:
>
> >I've done it too and I'm sure I'll do it again.  But, H1-Bs cannot do
that
> >nearly as easily as I can.  They are indentured to their sponsors and are
> >sometimes threatened with being sent back to their home country.  That is
> >the big problem with H1-B.
>
> They're not really 'indentured' in the way you mean it.  Yes, H1-Bs have
to
> have be employed, but changing sponsors (employers) is not as hard as some
> employers want to make it out to be.

I've read that it is possible to change employers during the normal green
card appeal process, but your new employer has to be prepared to go through
the approval process -- the 8 week delay -- for the new job.  And the H1B
holder is not going to want to give in his notice until the approval comes
in.

So that means that the delay between hiring and the worker starting is about
10 weeks.  That is a big obstacle.

On the otherhand, H1B holders can hire their own lawyer and go for a
National Interest Waiver.  During the NIW appeal process they can change
jobs and even be out of work, apparently.

- Keith

>
```

```
You'll find that most H1-B's are bound by some contract to the  first H1-B employer.
  I.E. Nasty non-compete clauses, penalties for early  termination, etc. 

We may not have full blown debtors prisons, (child support prison),
  here in the U.S., but in the third world almost anything goes.

The second and third world er's will almost sell their soul to get to the states
   by any means. That leaves them in a very weak bargaining position; when
  presented with a one sided H1-B employment contract(s).

Besides, Why do you think that the congress critters are putting  in a lame
   portability clause?. Because they know it will make NO difference in 
  H1-B's near SLAVE status.


Tim Keating
```

## Thread: realia cobol winapi — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### realia cobol winapi

- **From:** "Dennis Komeshak" <compusolve@charter.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<st9h0h28d1ls82@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I am looking for an example of how to call the winapi from Realia COBOL
32bit compiler for renaming and deleting files. I think they are MoveFile
and DeleteFile. I need the format and parameters for the calls. Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: realia cobol winapi

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r30d5$ht7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<st9h0h28d1ls82@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Maybe this will help. Make sure you have the Win platform SDK and that
you include the appropriate libraries in your link step.

 01  BOOL                                  pic s9(09) binary.
 01  KeyName                               PIC  X(79).
 01  Filename                              PIC  X(79).

     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO KEYNAME, FILENAME.
     STRING TPS-FILE-PATH-SERVER DELIMITED BY LOW-VALUE
            '\'
            CCLINK-USER-NAME DELIMITED BY SPACE
            '.txt'
            LOW-VALUE
         DELIMITED BY SIZE
         INTO KEYNAME.
     STRING TPS-FILE-PATH-SERVER DELIMITED BY LOW-VALUE
            '\'
            CCLINK-USER-NAME DELIMITED BY SPACE
            '.inp'
            LOW-VALUE
            DELIMITED BY SIZE
            INTO FILENAME.

     CALL 'S_MoveFileA'
         USING BY REFERENCE KEYNAME
                            FILENAME
         GIVING BOOL.

In article <st9h0h28d1ls82@corp.supernews.com>,
  "Dennis Komeshak" <compusolve@charter.net> wrote:
> I am looking for an example of how to call the winapi from Realia
COBOL
> 32bit compiler for renaming and deleting files. I think they are
MoveFile
> and DeleteFile. I need the format and parameters for the calls.
Thanks,
>
> --
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```
