<a id="t-c685db42ea"></a>
## Thread: Any Training on Object Oriented Cobol OS/390? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Any Training on Object Oriented Cobol OS/390?

- **From:** Dgavin <dmgavin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D4F9BD.B920158E@home.com>`

```
Greetings,

Looking for Training Materials either Net Based, or soucre code examples
on the Object Oriented capabilities of OS 390 Cobol.

Unfortunately am a State Employee, and the my state will not fork out
any money at this time for books or training, so looking for free
assistance.

I'd like to find Samles (other then the Dice one) on Vsam based Object,
with a client application, a CICS Map object and Client application, and
would love any information on building collection classes, as they
don';t seem to be a part of OO Cobol for OS 390.  (I Could be mistaken
on this, just basing it on IBM's less then adaquate manuals).

Thanks,

Darren.
```

#### ↳ Re: Any Training on Object Oriented Cobol OS/390?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D511BA.BDE5CB7C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<39D4F9BD.B920158E@home.com>`

```


Dgavin wrote:
> 
> Greetings,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Darren.

From a Gavan to a Gavin <G>

Darren,

I'm afraid trying to find out IBM offerings is like trying to milk a
bull. Best bet go to objectz.com and check out Bill Klein's FAQ. I can't
recall the author but there was a recent article there, (in
objectz.com), about a German bank (Dresden ???) using OO with an IBM
mainframe. (There is the VisualAge version also).

Are you already conversant with OO from other languages ? You could
write wprice@objectz.com, who has just produced his second edition of
Elements of OO Programming. It is a good tutorial. You can check out Ed
Arranga (also objectz.com) and Frank Coyle via amazon.com for their
book. Both these books go beyond theory with examples - but stress both
are NetExpress - and PC-based. Merant(Micro Focus) offer a University
Edition for about $100; same goes for Fujitsu - go to their site).

In terms of collections/dictionaries I could send you code, but it would
be meaningless without the NetExpress support classes and besides might
be totally different from what IBM have as classes. As yet collections
haven't even been dreamed about by the Standards (J4) and collection
methods between Merant and Fujitsu are poles apart. The nearest approach
to the OO Standard is Hitachi, but they recently informed somebody they
are not marketing Hitachi OO COBOL outside of Japan.

I agree; I didn't much care for the Dice example - shows how to do OO -
but it doesn't really jive when it comes to thinking about business
applications. You would do better looking at his file example, although
he has wrapped that inside Rational Rose - and confused the hell out of
me. Anyway that particular author has just taken a new job and
completely dropped OO COBOL - part of his reasoning - the outrageous
price of COBOL compilers !

If you make any headway stay in touch with me privately.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-c674559730"></a>
## Thread: Merant Mainframe Express — 5 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Merant Mainframe Express

- **From:** "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D502EC.EBDC1D9A@mescoma.com>`

```
I have recently received an evaluation version of Mainframe Express
2.5.  For the next 30 days I'm going to play with it and see if I like
it, and if so, I'll most likely buy it!

My request to the group is some other opinions from people that have
this product and that use it on a regular basis.

I've only just loaded it and started to read the accompaning
documentation, but so far it seems pretty cool.

My major concerns are the debugging tools as they compare to Animator in
Workbench.   I've been using workbench for years now, and I really like
the Animator.  It's saved me countless hours and made me a hero with my
clients on more than one occasion.

The ability to run jobstreams and to have CICS/DB2/IMS all in one
environment is also interesting.   Certainly must be easier than my
current tools!

So.................any thoughts or opinions that you have would be
appreciated.

Dave Bretz
```

#### ↳ Re: Merant Mainframe Express

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RS7B5.46$Rq3.466@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<39D502EC.EBDC1D9A@mescoma.com>`

```
Dave,

If you like MFE/Workbench, then you should check out the C-Cubed tools
available for both MFE and Workbench.  They allow you to transparently
access MVS subroutines directly from the debug environment.  What I mean
by this is that when you "Step" a "CALL PROGRAM USING ..." statement,
the animator/debugger interfaces with C-Cubed to call the subroutine on
MVS and return the results.  It's just like the subroutine was on the
local workstation!  You can also mix-and-match which subroutines get
executed on what platform - MVS or PC.  No more downloading subroutines
to the local PC to test with - just use the existing ones on MVS.

We also provide the same sort of access relating to MVS datasets - VSAM,
QSAM, PDS, Librarian, etc.

Check out the web-site if you are interested - www.c-cubed.net

Thanks - TL
```

#### ↳ Re: Merant Mainframe Express

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r34v4$3hr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39D502EC.EBDC1D9A@mescoma.com>`

```
If you are used to Micro Focus Workbench, then I certainly would NOT argue
against this.  HOWEVER, I am a little "curious" about your mainframe
environment.

If
 - you don't use any Assembler routines
 - you don't still have any OS/VS COBOL routines
 - you want to debug IMS "locally" on the PC

then you MIGHT also want to look at IBM's VisualAge COBOL.  The one
advantage that this has over MERANT's product(s) is that it does use the
EXACT same "front-end" for COBOL as the mainframe.  Therefore, when IBM
introduces new releases/features on the mainframe you may (probably?) will
see them faster in IBM VisualAge COBOL than in MERANT Mainframe Express.  ON
THE OTHER HAND, if you do need to access "asm" routines or PC IMS, I think
that MFE is your best (only?) real option.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Merant Mainframe Express

- **From:** "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D60244.C5B735F7@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<39D502EC.EBDC1D9A@mescoma.com> <8r34v4$3hr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
You're making the same assumption that most people make.  That I'm in one
consistent mainframe environment.  Our company provides highly skilled mainframe
programmers to any number of different mainframe shops, and typically our
programmers have Workbench installed on their laptops.  We don't typically know
ahead of time what sort of routines/problems/environments we may end up in.

However, I'll take a closer look at VisualAge COBOL and see.

Thanks for the information.

Dave
```

<a id="t-622f192874"></a>
## Thread: Group vs element — 4 this month / 7 total

### Group vs element

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D51856.22F4D33E@brazee.net>`

```
I was trying to remember the difference between group and element moves
when I saw a commercial program which had a change which I don't
understand.

01   big-string.	
    05  character pic x occurs 100.

They stuck a redefines of big-string, which was only referenced as a
spot where a STRING went into.  Why is this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Group vs element

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<L0aB5.55$ux3.668@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<39D51856.22F4D33E@brazee.net>`

```
Maybe to access each individual character in the array, as well as to
access the group as a whole.  Could be due to old Cobol code, ie. before
reference-modification was introduced.

news:39D51856.22F4D33E@brazee.net...

moves

> understand.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> spot where a STRING went into.  Why is this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Group vs element

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-30T04:14:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qMdB5.11645$tl2.831033@bgtnsc07-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<39D51856.22F4D33E@brazee.net>`

```
My guess is that the 01 is a group P (x100) and then the programmer at the
moment decided that there was a need for character scanning for some
purpose.

 If I remember correctly Groups used to be handled like records as the
receiving area was right justified and space filled on the left.   Perhaps
not all strings were to be organized in that manner so as an afterthought,
the elementary item was defined.  The occurs used to have an
influence on it, too.

Warren Simmons

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with CBL_GET_KBD_STATUS

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000929192847.21129.00000019@nso-cu.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<WR8B5.554922$Kw2.4859726@flipper>`

```
In article <WR8B5.554922$Kw2.4859726@flipper>, "h.j.w.m.jans"
<h.j.w.m.jans@chello.nl> writes:

>
>I'm trying to use this function. But it is not located in the F3BICLR.LIB,
>which was provided with some sample programs of version 4.
>

As far as I can tell, this function is included in the F3BICBLR.LIB file.
be sure that you have the file named correctly in your project or
in the LINK options.  I usually modify the [Linkage Files]
section of COBOL.CBI to always include the files that are most commonly
used across my programs.
Specifically, I always add XCODESET.LIB to handle the EBCDIC/ASCII
translation stuff.
```

```
What he means is, a crime is a crime is a crime; a criminal is a criminal is
a criminal.

The motivation for the crime does not change the crime into something else;
that is, the relative race, creed, color, sex,national origin,
left-handedness or whether or not the victim is a transvestite who has had
sex with his pet is immaterial to a system of equal justice for all.

By the way, racism is not against the law. Neither is hate, anger, rage, or
bias.

Discrimination in certain activities based on some of these things may be
against the law, but the underlying emotion is not illegal. (Except under
tyrannical regimes where the thought police have real police power).

MCM
```

```
Jerry P wrote:
> "Phil Eamon" <phileam@blarney.att.net
> > As long as you do not *commit* anything, you can think any thoughts
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> You really should get out more. 
Eh... true! Very true.

> Think John Rocker, Jimmy the Greek.
Nothing wrong with these two. As far as I know, they were not imprisoned
for their opinions.

> You can be fired from your job, expelled from your school, voted out
> of office,  asked to leave a restaurant, and probably have your cat
> confiscated for vicious speech.
You're sure about cat? Anyway, you won't be imprisoned. Moreover, if you
can prove that you were fired because of your opinions, you might have a
case against your employer. Of course, it's not easy to prove something
like that--anyone can be fired from a job, expelled from school, and no
one owes anyone to keep him in the office, you see. We were talking
about crime and freedom of speech. If you are a racist, it's not against
the law--till you start acting it out in a way that constitutes a crime.
However, at the same time, freedom of speech doesn't imply obligatory
universal acceptance of what you say, so yeah, if you run for an office
and say what I don't like, I won't vote for you, moreover, I will
probably vote against you. If I'm an employer, I don't owe it to anyone
to employ them, so if you act in a way that irritates me, I'll find a
way to kick you out--equal opportunity my foot.
```

```
> > "Phil Eamon" <phileam@blarney.att.net
> > > As long as you do not *commit* anything, you can think any
thoughts
> > > and speak any speeches whatsoever. Aren't you an American? We
have
> > > freedom of conscience and speech here.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nothing wrong with these two. As far as I know, they were not
imprisoned
> for their opinions.

Living under a bridge, hungry, in the snow, barefoot, is worse than
prison.

>
> > You can be fired from your job, expelled from your school, voted
out
> > of office,  asked to leave a restaurant, and probably have your
cat
> > confiscated for vicious speech.
> You're sure about cat? Anyway, you won't be imprisoned. Moreover, if
you
> can prove that you were fired because of your opinions, you might
have a
> case against your employer.

> Of course, it's not easy to prove something
> like that--anyone can be fired from a job, expelled from school, and
no
> one owes anyone to keep him in the office, you see.

No. Only in states that have "Employment at will' provisions. In many
states, California for one, employment is a contract that may not be
terminated by the employer without cause. In all states, union
agreements - for example - preclude 'arbitrary' termination.


> We were talking
> about crime and freedom of speech. If you are a racist, it's not
against
> the law--till you start acting it out in a way that constitutes a
crime.

Speech IS action. If you can go to jail for sedition, it won't be long
before you can go to jail for 'hate' speech.

> However, at the same time, freedom of speech doesn't imply
obligatory
> universal acceptance of what you say, so yeah, if you run for an
office
> and say what I don't like, I won't vote for you, moreover, I will
> probably vote against you. If I'm an employer, I don't owe it to
anyone
> to employ them, so if you act in a way that irritates me, I'll find
a
> way to kick you out--equal opportunity my foot.
>
```

```
Phil Eamon wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> moral: don't commit crimes.

Don't agree, murder is murder. It's not worse if I'm straight & the
corpse was gay and not so bad if the killer is gay the the corpse was
straight. Same for race, same for sex. 

What this does is create a two-tier criminal system, e.g., a felony -
murder, and a worse felony, hate murder. 

The idea of "hate crimes" comes from the idea of "politically correct"
thought/speech, etc., the logic of which escapes me. We're supposed to
change the way we speak and behave because *somewhere*, *sometime*,
*someone*'s feelings MIGHT be hurt? Isn't that the dept. of tough
noughies? This also turns our legal system around so it punishes crimes
before they actually occur. AFAIK, hurting someone's feelings is NOT a
crime, even a misdemeanor. Too bad, so sad, if you (non-specific
reference) think it should be.

LiamD
```

- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <39D49575.C6396B12@brazee.net> <39D534B8.421F8A5@blarney.att.net> <39D57EF8.4BF49809@optonline.net>`

```
In article <39D57EF8.4BF49809@optonline.net>,
Liam Devlin  <LiamD@optoffline.not> wrote:
>Phil Eamon wrote:
>> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>straight. Same for race, same for sex. 

Eh?  'Murder is murder'?  I'm not a lawyer so I now need to ask... aren't
there recognised, at least by the legal system in the United States of
America, differing degrees of murder... first, second, etc.?

Isn't the highest of these - first degree murder - based on one's mental
state before and after the act?

>
>What this does is create a two-tier criminal system, e.g., a felony -
>murder, and a worse felony, hate murder. 

... as opposed to, say, a system which has things in it like negligent
homicide being differentiated from premeditated murder.

>
>The idea of "hate crimes" comes from the idea of "politically correct"
>thought/speech, etc., the logic of which escapes me.

I'm not sure - and perhaps a scholar out there might assist here - but
given that a goodly chunk of the legal system of the United States of
America is a product of the Judaeo-Christian ethic and that a goodly chunk
of the Christian ethic is concerned with intentionality ('adultery in the
heart' is, to some, a sin... and such an 'adultery' is, it seems, no overt
action but mere intention) then the classification of crime by intention
appears to predate the bugaboo of 'political correctness' by a few
millennia.

DD
```

```
NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message news:PhkB5.7672$l35.149437@iad->
> I'm not sure - and perhaps a scholar out there might assist here - but
> given that a goodly chunk of the legal system of the United States of
> America is a product of the Judaeo-Christian ethic and that a goodly chunk
> of the Christian ethic is concerned with intentionality ('adultery in the
> heart' is, to some, a sin...

Too many confuse 'crime' with 'sin'.  Crime is secular; sin, ideological.
One may sin without commiting a crime; one may commit a crime without
commiting sin.

In my ideal world, sin would equal crime. However, since crime relies on
consensus and compromise among those who would be subject to a law defining
crime, and sin relies in moral absolutes, this ain't gonna happen.

The other main difference between sin and crime, of course, is that for sin,
there's no statute of limitations, getting off on a technicality or jury
nullification.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<XyoB5.94$C17.14771@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <39D49575.C6396B12@brazee.net> <39D534B8.421F8A5@blarney.att.net> <39D57EF8.4BF49809@optonline.net> <PhkB5.7672$l35.149437@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net

> Eh?  'Murder is murder'?  I'm not a lawyer so I now need to ask...
aren't
> there recognised, at least by the legal system in the United States
of
> America, differing degrees of murder... first, second, etc.?
>
> Isn't the highest of these - first degree murder - based on one's
mental
> state before and after the act?

Correct. ALL criminal offenses have, as a necessary element of the
offense, the intent or mental state of the perpetrator. Homicide (the
killing of one human by another), is not a crime - it is a definition.
Homicide becomes one of five (in Texas) possible offenses depending on
the circumstances and mental state of the accused. These are
illustrated by the killing of one deer hunter by another:

1. Murder: The deceased was intentionally shot in the back by the
accused.
1a. Capital Murder: The accused was paid to shoot the deceased.
2. Manslaughter( 2nd degree Murder): The deceased incited the accused
to fight.
3. Negligent Homicide: The accused was drunk or did not take proper
precautions with his weapon (such as throwing it over the fence or not
checking to see if it was loaded).
4. Justifiable Homicide: The deceased shot first - no penalty for the
accused.
5. Excusable Homicide: The deceased was dressed in a deer costume - no
penalty for the accused.

As to the 'hate crime' situation, I can't see how 'hating' a class can
elevate any of the above to the more severe crime above it (well,
maybe Murder could be promoted to Capital Murder).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Phil Eamon <phileam@blarney.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39D605C2.8EF6D37B@blarney.att.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <QHdA5.6985$583.294276@news1.onlynews.com> <k7b5tssdb0rclbaqam5ran5104ngtl1adl@4ax.com> <3URA5.1964$HO3.141800@news1.onlynews.com> <39D40051.5AAF24BA@blarney.att.net> <39D49575.C6396B12@brazee.net> <39D534B8.421F8A5@blarney.att.net> <39D57EF8.4BF49809@optonline.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> Phil Eamon wrote:
> > Howard Brazee wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Don't agree, murder is murder. 
Oh, I don't think that's true. You can shoot a guy invading your house,
you can do the same mistakenly believing that your house it being
invaded, or you can tie some guy to a truck and take him apart alive
because you think that's what you do to this [insert] kind of people.
Seems like the three murders above are different.

> It's not worse if I'm straight & the
> corpse was gay and not so bad if the killer is gay the the corpse was
> straight. Same for race, same for sex.
Again, I disagree with that. The priority being to avoid crimes, it is
important what specifically causes people to commit crimes. Getting back
to the freedom-of-speech aspect of it, as long as you commit no crimes
you can think whatever you want, but that freedom of expression doesn't
imply a guarantee of receptiveness or acceptance of what you think by
others. But you can strive to achieve such an acceptance, otoh.

> What this does is create a two-tier criminal system, e.g., a felony -
> murder, and a worse felony, hate murder.
I'm not sure I disagree with that. I think it is in fact different if
you shoot someone in the head because he did your woman vs you do the
same because he was some kind of irrational hate target, like gay or
whatever. Who cares about gays? You're not required to participate, so
... Plus, your victim could have protected himself by staying away from
your women, but what's a homo to do? That's different.

> The idea of "hate crimes" comes from the idea of "politically correct"
> thought/speech, etc., the logic of which escapes me. 
The logic is that hate as in "hate crimes" is irrational. Now, there's
no law that everyone be rational, but you can't allow people run around
shooting one another because they ate too much sugar or some such thing.
Therefore hate crimes are more grave than "regualar" crimes of the same
outcome. Crimes are resolutions of conflicts, so if the conflict is
rational, there's hope of its being resolved by peaceful means, but if
the conflict is due to loose screws in someone's head, how do you settle
that?

> We're supposed to
> change the way we speak and behave because *somewhere*, *sometime*,
> *someone*'s feelings MIGHT be hurt? 
You're not supposed to change your thinking. Behaving--well, that would
have to depend on what exactly that means. If it means dragging a guy
behind your truck because of hell knows what in your head went boom,
then sorry, that can't be allowed.

> Isn't that the dept. of tough
> noughies? This also turns our legal system around so it punishes crimes
> before they actually occur. AFAIK, hurting someone's feelings is NOT a
> crime, even a misdemeanor. 
Hurting someone's feeling is not a crime. What is specifically a problem
here?

> Too bad, so sad, if you (non-specific
> reference) think it should be.
No, I don't think it should be. Neither do I think it is, quite frankly. 

> 
> LiamD
```

- **Message-ID:** `<x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
hello there
I actually have an example of a simple program you can take a look at. I
will paste it below. if you would like to look at the data file that it uses
or you really have any questions let me know. oh and by the way I ask that
if you are gonna use this to produce something you just give me a credit and
inform me of your intentions. I can be contacted @: doug_xx2@yahoo.com or
doug_xx2@stny.rr.com please contact me first so I can give you my full name
to use when you credit me for my work. but I doubt you will want to use it
for anything special as it is very rudamentary code. I also have examples of
report writers and a sort program and some menu driven stuff. it was writen
for vax cobol so if you are going to use it with something else you may have
to make some compiler specific changes. please find the source pasted below.
and note the alignment was screwed up durring the cut and paste operation.


IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.


PROGRAM-ID. CoBoL-DATA-VALIDATION.
AUTHOR. Douglas (ask for a last name and I will give you mine if you give me
yours).
DATE-WRITTEN. 02/2000.
****************************************************************
* VALIDATION OF RECORDS                                        *
****************************************************************


ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.


CONFIGURATION SECTION.
SOURCE-COMPUTER.     VAX.
OBJECT-COMPUTER.     VAX.

SPECIAL-NAMES.  C01 IS TOP-OF-PAGE.


INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

FILE-CONTROL.


SELECT MASTER-FILE-IN ASSIGN TO "MASTERFILE.DAT".
SELECT ERROR-LIST-RPT ASSIGN TO "ERROR_RPT.RPT".
SELECT CONFIRMED-FILES-OUT ASSIGN TO "VALIDATED_MASTER.DAT".


DATA DIVISION.

FILE SECTION.


FD MASTER-FILE-IN
   LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
   RECORD CONTAINS 56 CHARACTERS.
01 RECORD_IN.
   05 DEPARTMENT_NUMBER_IN              PIC 9(2).
   05 SALES_NUMBER_IN                   PIC 9(3).
   05 NAME_IN                           PIC X(20).
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(7)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 CURRENT_SALES_IN                  PIC 9(7).
   05 COMMISION_RATE_IN                 PIC 9(2).
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(8)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 CURRENT_SALES_RETURN_IN           PIC 9(6).
   05 TYPE_CODE_IN                      PIC 9.


FD ERROR-LIST-RPT
   LABEL RECORDS OMITTED
   RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS.
01 ERROR-REC-OUT                        PIC X(80).


FD CONFIRMED-FILES-OUT
   LABEL RECORDS OMITTED
   RECORD CONTAINS 56 CHARACTERS.
01 VALID-REC-OUT                        PIC X(56).


WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.


01 EOF-SW                               PIC 9            VALUE 0.


01 PAGE-CT                              PIC 999          VALUE 1.


01 LINE-CT                              PIC 99           VALUE 77.


01 NUM-RECS-IN                          PIC 9(3)         VALUE 0.


01 TIME-FOR-NEW-PAGE                    PIC 9            VALUE 1.


01 NUM-OF-GOOD-REC                      PIC 999          VALUE 0.


01 ERR-RPT-MSG1                         PIC X(57)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-RPT-MSG2                         PIC X(39)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-RPT-MSG3                         PIC X(39)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-RPT-MSG4                         PIC X(39)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-RPT-MSG5                         PIC X(39)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-RPT-MSG6                         PIC X(39)        VALUE SPACES.


01 TOT-ERRORS                           PIC 9(4)         VALUE ZEROS.


01 ERRORS-IN-REC                        PIC 99           VALUE ZEROS.


01 RECS-IN-ERR                          PIC 9(3)         VALUE ZEROS.


01 BLANK-RECS                           PIC 9(3)         VALUE ZEROS.


01 DATE-WS.
   05 DATE-YY                           PIC 9(2).
   05 DATE-MM                           PIC 9(2).
   05 DATE-DD                           PIC 9(2).


01 VALID-DETAIL-LN.
   05 VALID-REC-DET-LN                  PIC X(56).
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(24)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-HEAD-DET-LN.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(26)        VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-HEADING                       PIC X(28)        VALUE "DATA
VALIDATION ERROR REPORT".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(26)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-HEAD-DATE-LN.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(10)        VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(4)         VALUE "PAGE".
   05 PAGE-CT-OUT                       PIC ZZ9          VALUE ZEROS.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(40)        VALUE SPACES.
   05 DATE-MM-OUT                       PIC 99/.
   05 DATE-DD-OUT                       PIC 99/.
   05 DATE-YY-OUT                       PIC 99.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(15)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-REC-DET-LN.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERROR-REC-DET           PIC X(56)        VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(19)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-MRK-DET-LN.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-DEPT-MRK                      PIC X(2)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-EMP-MRK                       PIC X(3)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-NAME-MRK                      PIC X(20)        VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(7)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-CURR-SAL-MRK                  PIC X(7)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-COMM-RT-MRK                   PIC X(2)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(8)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-CUR-SAL-RET-MRK               PIC X(6)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-TYP-CD-MRK                    PIC X            VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(19)        VALUE SPACES.


01 ERR-MESS-DET-LN.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 ERR-MESS-OUT                      PIC X(57).
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(36)        VALUE SPACES.


01 TOT-DET-LN1.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(23)        VALUE "Total
records processed".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(2)         VALUE ": ".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 TOT-RECS-OUT                      PIC ZZ9          VALUE ZEROS.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(49)        VALUE SPACES.


01 TOT-DET-LN2.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(28)        VALUE "Total good
records processed".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(2)         VALUE ": ".
   05 TOT-GOOD-REC                      PIC ZZ9          VALUE ZEROS.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(72)        VALUE SPACES.


01 TOT-DET-LN3.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(25)        VALUE "Total
records with errors".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(2)         VALUE ": ".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(3)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 TOT-ERROR-REC                     PIC ZZ9          VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(72)        VALUE SPACES.


01 TOT-DET-LN4.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(22)        VALUE "Total number
of errors".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(2)         VALUE ": ".
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(5)         VALUE SPACES.
   05 TOT-ERRORS-OUT                    PIC ZZZ9         VALUE ZEROS.
   05 FILLER                            PIC X(71)        VALUE SPACES.


PROCEDURE DIVISION.


****************************************************************
*000-MAIN-MODULE                                               *
*THIT IS THE MODULE THAT INITIATES THE ENTIRE PROGRAM. THIS    *
*MODULE STARTS OUT BY OPENING THE INPUT AND OUTPUT FILES.      *
*AFTER OPENING THE INPUT AND OUTPUT FILES THE MODULE GOES ON TO*
*GET THE PRESENT SYSTEM DATE FROM THE SYSTEM AND STORES IT IN  *
*THE DATE-WS FIELD. THE MODULE GOES ON TO MOVE THE DATE-WS     *
*COMPONETS TO THE ERR-HEAD-DATE-LN OUTPUT FIELD (THIS IS IN    *
*PREPARATION OF WRITING THE HEADING IN THE ERROR_RPT.RPT FILE).*
*THIS MODULE GOES ON TO PERFORM THE 150-READ-RTN (TO READ THE  *
*FIRST RECORD IN). AFTER PERFORMING THE 150-READ-RTN THIS      *
*MODULE GOES ON TO PERFORM THE 200-PROCESS UNTIL THE EOF-SW IS *
*TURNED ON. AFTER PERFORMING THE 200-PROCESS MODULE THIS       *
*MODULE GOES ON TO CLOSE THE INPUT AND OUTPUT FILES AND ENDS   *
*THE RUN.                                                      *
****************************************************************

000-MAIN-MODULE.
        OPEN INPUT MASTER-FILE-IN
             OUTPUT ERROR-LIST-RPT
             OUTPUT CONFIRMED-FILES-OUT.
        ACCEPT DATE-WS FROM DATE.
        MOVE DATE-YY TO DATE-YY-OUT.
        MOVE DATE-MM TO DATE-MM-OUT.
        MOVE DATE-DD TO DATE-DD-OUT.
        PERFORM 150-READ-RTN.
        PERFORM 200-PROCESS
                UNTIL EOF-SW=1.
        CLOSE MASTER-FILE-IN
              ERROR-LIST-RPT
              CONFIRMED-FILES-OUT.
        STOP RUN.


****************************************************************
*150-READ-RTN                                                  *
*THIS ROUTINE FIRST READS THE INPUT FILE AND IF IT FINDS ITSELF*
*AT THE END OF FILE IT PERFORMS 400-TOTALS-RTN BEFORE MOVING 1 *
*TO THE EOF-SW (END OF FILE SWITCH). IF IT IS NOT THE END OF   *
*THE INPUT FILE THIS ROUTINE ADDS 1 TO NUM-RECS-IN (USED TO    *
*TOTAL THE NUMBER OF RECORDS READ IN FROM THE INPUT FILE).     *
****************************************************************


150-READ-RTN.
        READ MASTER-FILE-IN
  AT END perform 400-TOTALS-RTN
                       MOVE 1 TO EOF-SW.
        ADD 1 TO NUM-RECS-IN.

****************************************************************
*200-PROCESS                                                   *
*CHECKS THE LINE COUNT TO SEE IF IT NEEDS TO TURN ON THE       *
*TIME-FOR-NEW-PAGE SWITCH. IT THEN CHECKS THE ERRORS-IN-REC    *
*COUNTER TO SEE IF THERE ARE ERRORS AND THEN EITHER PERFORMS   *
*THE 275-VALID-REC-OUT-RTN, OR MOVES ON TO CHECK IF THE TIME   *
*FOR NEW PAGE SWITCH IS TURNED ON. IF THE TIME FOR NEW PAGE    *
*SWITCH IS NOT TURNED ON IT PERFORMS THE 350-ERROR-RPT-OUT-RTN *
*BUT IF IT IS ON IT PERFORMS THE 300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN BEFORE *
*PERFORMING THE 300-ERROR-RPT-OUT-RTN. THE 200-PROCESS THEN    *
*PERFORMS THE 150-READ-RTN.                                    *
****************************************************************


200-PROCESS.
        IF LINE-CT IS > 75
                MOVE 1 TO TIME-FOR-NEW-PAGE.
        PERFORM 250-VALIDATION-RTN.
        IF ERRORS-IN-REC = 0
                PERFORM 275-VALID-REC-OUT-RTN
        ELSE IF TIME-FOR-NEW-PAGE = 1
                PERFORM 300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN
                PERFORM 350-ERROR-RPT-OUT-RTN
             ELSE PERFORM 350-ERROR-RPT-OUT-RTN.
        PERFORM 150-READ-RTN.


****************************************************************
*250-VALIDATION-RTN                                            *
*VALIDATES THE FIELDS TO BE CHECKED AND MOVES THE APROPRIATE   *
*ERROR MESSAGES AND FIELD MARKERS FOR EACH RECORD THAT HAS     *
*ERRORS.                                                       *
****************************************************************


250-VALIDATION-RTN.
        IF Type_Code_IN IS NOT = 1
                MOVE "Type code does not indicate load record (Not type code
1)" TO ERR-RPT-MSG1
                ADD 1 TO ERRORS-IN-REC
                MOVE "*" TO ERR-TYP-CD-MRK.
        IF DEPARTMENT_NUMBER_IN IS NOT NUMERIC
                MOVE "Dept. # is not numeric                 " TO
ERR-RPT-MSG2
                ADD 1 TO ERRORS-IN-REC
                MOVE "**" TO ERR-DEPT-MRK.
        IF SALES_NUMBER_IN IS NOT NUMERIC
                MOVE "Sales # is not numeric                 " TO
ERR-RPT-MSG3
                ADD 1 TO ERRORS-IN-REC
                MOVE "***" TO ERR-EMP-MRK.
        INSPECT CURRENT_SALES_IN REPLACING ALL SPACES BY ZEROS.
        IF CURRENT_SALES_IN IS NOT NUMERIC
                MOVE "Current sales is not numeric           " TO
ERR-RPT-MSG4
                ADD 1 TO ERRORS-IN-REC
                MOVE "*******" TO ERR-CURR-SAL-MRK.
        INSPECT Commision_Rate_In REPLACING ALL SPACES BY ZEROS.
        IF Commision_Rate_In IS NOT NUMERIC
                MOVE "Commision rate is not numeric          " TO
ERR-RPT-MSG5
                ADD 1 TO ERRORS-IN-REC
                MOVE "**" TO ERR-COMM-RT-MRK.
        INSPECT Current_Sales_Return_In REPLACING ALL SPACES BY ZEROS.
        IF Current_Sales_Return_In IS NOT NUMERIC
                MOVE "Current sales return is not numeric    " TO
ERR-RPT-MSG6
                ADD 1 TO ERRORS-IN-REC
                MOVE "******" TO ERR-CUR-SAL-RET-MRK.

****************************************************************
*275-VALID-REC-OUT-RTN                                         *
*THIS ROUTINE MOVES RECORD_IN (THE INPUT FILE) TO THE          *
*VALID-REC-DET-LN. IT THEN WRITES THE VALID RECORD TO THE      *
*VALIDATED_MASTER.DAT FILE USING THE FORMAT IT FINDS IN THE    *
*VALID-DETAIL-LN. AFTER WRITING THE VALID RECORD OUT THIS      *
*ROUTINE ADDS 1 TO THE NUM-OF-GOOD-REC COUNTER AND GOES ON TO  *
*INITIALIZE THE VALID-REC-DET-LN.                              *
****************************************************************


275-VALID-REC-OUT-RTN.
        MOVE RECORD_IN TO VALID-REC-DET-LN.
        WRITE VALID-REC-OUT FROM VALID-DETAIL-LN.
        ADD 1 TO NUM-OF-GOOD-REC.
        INITIALIZE VALID-REC-DET-LN.


****************************************************************
*300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN                                        *
*THE 300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN STARTS OUT BY MOVING THE VALUE OF  *
*THE PAGE-CT COUNTER TO THE PAGE-CT-OUT FIELD IN THE           *
*ERR-HEAD-DATE-LN. AFTER MOVING THE COUNTER THIS ROUTINE WRITES*
*TO THE ERROR_RPT.RPT FILE FROM THE ERR-HEAD-DET-LN AFTER A    *
*PAGE FEED. AFTER THE PAGE FEED THE ROUTINE WRITES TO THE      *
*ERROR_RPT.RPT FILE FROM THE ERR-HEAD-DATE-LN AFTER FOUR BLANK *
*LINES.THE ROUTINE THEN GOES ON TO MOVE 6 TO THE LINE-CT       *
*COUNTER, ADD 1 TO PAGE-CT (WHICH IS ONLY NEEDED AT THE END OF *
*THIS ROUTINE BECAUSE THE INITIAL VALUE IS 1), AND TURNS OFF   *
*THE TIME-FOR-NEW-PAGE SWITCH.                                 *
****************************************************************


300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN.
        MOVE PAGE-CT TO PAGE-CT-OUT.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-HEAD-DET-LN AFTER TOP-OF-PAGE.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-HEAD-DATE-LN AFTER 4.
        MOVE 06 TO LINE-CT.
        ADD 1 TO PAGE-CT.
        MOVE 0 TO TIME-FOR-NEW-PAGE.



****************************************************************
*350-ERROR-RPT-OUT-RTN                                         *
*THIS ROUTINE STARTS OUT BY CHECKING THE LINE COUNT TO SEE IF  *
*THERE IS ENOUGH ROOM TO WRITE THE RECORD IN ERROR AND ALL THE *
*ERROR MESSAGE BEFORE THE END OF THE PAGE. IF THERE IS NOT     *
*ENOUGH ROOM THE ROUTINE PERFORMS THE 300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN.  *
*AFTER CHECKING FOR ROOM AND POSSIBLY RUNNING THE HEADING      *
*ROUTINE THIS ROUTINE MOVES RECORD_IN TO THE ERROR-REC-DET     *
*FIELD. AFTER MOVING THE RECORD THIS ROUTINE WRITES THE        *
*ERROR_RPT.RPT FILE FROM ERR-REC-DET-LN AFTER 3 BLANK LINES.   *
*AFTER WRITING THE ERROR REPORT THE ROUTINE WRITES THE         *
*ERROR.RPT FROM THE ERR-MRK-DET-LN.(THIS DETAIL LINE IS WHERE  *
*THE FIELD MARKERS FOR THE FIELDS IN ERROR ARE WRITEN INTO THE *
*ERROR REPORT. AFTER WRITING THE RECORD IN ERROR OUT TO THE    *
*REPORT ALONG WITH IT'S MARKERS THE ROUTINE GOES ON TO CHECK   *
*THE SIX ERROR MESSAGE HOLDING FIELDS TO SEE IF THEY ARE EQUAL *
*TO SPACES OR NOT. IF THE FIELDS ARE SPACES THEY DO NOT GET    *
*MOVED OR WRITEN TO THE ERROR REPORT. IF THEY ARE NOT          *
*SPACES THEY GET MOVED AND WRITEN TO THE ERROR REPORT AND 1    *
*GETS ADDED TO THE LINE-CT COUNTER FOR EACH MESSAGE WRITTEN TO *
*THE ERROR REPORT. AFTER THE ROUTINE IS DONE CHECKING ALL THE  *
*ERROR MESSAGE HOLDERS IT ADDS ERRORS-IN-REC TO TOT-ERRORS     *
*(IN ORDER TO KEEP TRACK OF THE TOTAL NUMBER OF ERRORS FOUND), *
*MOVES 0 TO ERRORS IN REC (IN ORDER TO GET IT READY FOR THE    *
*NEXT RECORD), AND ADDS 1 TO RECS-IN-ERR (TO COUNT THE NUMBER  *
*OF ERROR RECORDS PROCESSED).                                  *
****************************************************************


350-ERROR-RPT-OUT-RTN.
        IF LINE-CT IS > 64
                PERFORM 300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN.
        MOVE RECORD_IN TO ERROR-REC-DET.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-REC-DET-LN AFTER 3.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-MRK-DET-LN.
        ADD 5 TO LINE-CT.
        INITIALIZE ERR-MRK-DET-LN.
        INITIALIZE ERROR-REC-DET.
        IF ERR-RPT-MSG1 IS NOT = SPACES
                MOVE ERR-RPT-MSG1 TO ERR-MESS-OUT
                WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-MESS-DET-LN
                INITIALIZE ERR-RPT-MSG1
  ADD 1 TO LINE-CT.
        IF ERR-RPT-MSG2 IS NOT = SPACES
                MOVE ERR-RPT-MSG2 TO ERR-MESS-OUT
                WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-MESS-DET-LN
                INITIALIZE ERR-RPT-MSG2
  ADD 1 TO LINE-CT.
        IF ERR-RPT-MSG3 IS NOT = SPACES
                MOVE ERR-RPT-MSG3 TO ERR-MESS-OUT
                WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-MESS-DET-LN
                INITIALIZE ERR-RPT-MSG3
  ADD 1 TO LINE-CT.
        IF ERR-RPT-MSG4 IS NOT = SPACES
                MOVE ERR-RPT-MSG4 TO ERR-MESS-OUT
                WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-MESS-DET-LN
                INITIALIZE ERR-RPT-MSG4
  ADD 1 TO LINE-CT.
        IF ERR-RPT-MSG5 IS NOT = SPACES
                MOVE ERR-RPT-MSG5 TO ERR-MESS-OUT
                WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-MESS-DET-LN
                INITIALIZE ERR-RPT-MSG5
  ADD 1 TO LINE-CT.
        IF ERR-RPT-MSG6 IS NOT = SPACES
                MOVE ERR-RPT-MSG6 TO ERR-MESS-OUT
                WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM ERR-MESS-DET-LN
                INITIALIZE ERR-RPT-MSG6
  ADD 1 TO LINE-CT.
        INITIALIZE ERR-REC-DET-LN.
        ADD ERRORS-IN-REC TO TOT-ERRORS.
        MOVE 0 TO ERRORS-IN-REC.
        ADD 1 TO RECS-IN-ERR.


****************************************************************
*400-TOTALS-RTN
*
*THE 400-TOTALS-ROUTINE STARTS OUT BY CHECKING THE LINE-CT TO  *
*SEE IF THERE IS ROOM FOR A NEW PAGE. IF IT IS TIME FOR A NEW  *
*PAGE IT PERFORMS THE 300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN. IF THERE IS ROOM *
*FOR ALL OF THE TOTALS TO PRINT ON THE PRESENT PAGE IT MOVES   *
*NUM-OF-GOOD-REC (NUMBER OF GOOD RECORDS) TO TOT-GOOD-REC.     *
*THEN IT MOVES TOT-ERRORS (THE TOTAL NUMBER OF ERRORS FOUND)   *
*TO TOT-ERRORS-OUT, MOVES RECS-IN-ERR (NUMBER OF RECORDS WITH  *
*ERRORS) TO TOT-ERROR-REC, AND NUM-RECS-IN (NUMBER OF RECORDS  *
*READ FROM THE DATA FILE) TO TOT-RECS-OUT. AFTER PERFORMING    *
*ALL OF IT'S MOVES IT WRITES THE ERROR REPORT FROM             *
*TOT-DET-LN1,2,3,AND 4.                                        *
****************************************************************


400-TOTALS-RTN.
        IF LINE-CT IS > 63
                PERFORM 300-ERROR-RPT-HDNG-RTN.
        MOVE NUM-OF-GOOD-REC TO TOT-GOOD-REC.
        MOVE TOT-ERRORS TO TOT-ERRORS-OUT.
        MOVE RECS-IN-ERR TO TOT-ERROR-REC.
        MOVE NUM-RECS-IN TO TOT-RECS-OUT.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM TOT-DET-LN1 AFTER 3.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM TOT-DET-LN2.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM TOT-DET-LN4.
        WRITE ERROR-REC-OUT FROM TOT-DET-LN3.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
I think this program is pretty good, but I see one problem...

I am against such wordy comments.  The problem is, any maintenance
programmers, even good ones, come along and assume, with such thorough
comments, that the comments are correct.  Unfortunately, even after only a
few programmers have touched the program, this is often not true.  Comments
go out of 'style' faster than style itself.  Inaccurate comments, as is
usually the case, are often dangerous.

COBOL, by it's very nature, is self-documenting.  This is probably one of
the biggest reasons why it's still around as a viable method of processing
data.  Because of it's durability, an average program has probably been
touched a few times, at least, and an inconsistency in programming style can
have an overall impact on the 'content' or logic of the program itself.
Comments confound, or multiply, this problem.

If any language doesn't need comments, COBOL is it.  Comments are certainly
worthwhile, and appreciated, in tricky code.  If the comment helps us
understand how this routine fits into the bigger picture of the system, or
if the code is particularly 'non-intuitive' to an average programmer, then
comments are essential.  Comments at the beginning of the program are always
good, as well as a blurb at the beginning of each paragraph, even if this is
only the (well chosen hopefully) paragraph name repeated, centered, with a
box or some asterisks surrounding it.

But these comments, in the program presented are un-neccesarily (sp?) wordy.
If I didn't speak English too well, then these comments would certainly make
the program more 'arbitary' or less understandable or more confusing.

One of the most impressive programmers I know of in our shop spent many
months in PowerBuilder, Informix, and AIX.  He is Polish, and no longer
works with us.  The code he wrote is very impressive, but let me tell you,
it is not easy reading Polish code.

I like to stress the importance of Shop Standards.  In a tightly integrated
shop, these comments would be considered largely a waste of typing time,
although nobody would really care that much as long as the system work
correctly.  If everyone conforms to a certain basic set of rules, then many
things become uniformed, or standardized, and this makes later modification
easier because there is less confusion as to what exactly the program is
trying to do.  Many things are 'given' at that point.  That is the beauty of
Shop Standards.

Any good programmer realizes that the end product is the main goal.  People
who use computers, guess what, they don't give a fuck how the shit works.
They could care less that we ran out of space, or blew the schedule, or that
there's a bug in the system, they just want it to work.  We, as programmers,
need to continue to ascend to those levels to provide entire packages to the
consumers, something that the consumer, or user, can trust.

Jeff

----------

<doug_xx2@yahoo.com> wrote:

> I actually have an example of a simple program you can take a look at. I
…[492 more quoted lines elided]…
```
